stem: A 31-year-old woman attends her GP. She had a Mirena 52 mg levonorgestrel intrauterine system inserted 4 years and 11 months ago for contraception and heavy menstrual bleeding. Insertion was via a private clinic that printed a removal-reminder card stating 'replace at 5 years'. She has had no bleeding for 18 months, no pain, threads visible at the routine 4-yearly check. She is in a stable monogamous relationship, has no plans to conceive for at least 5 more years and would prefer to keep this device as long as possible. She specifically asks whether the device must be replaced this month or whether she can defer. Cycles, partner, BMI and screening history are all unremarkable.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate advice regarding her current Mirena device?

options.t: Replace the device this month because the heavy-menstrual-bleeding indication caps duration at 5 years
Replace the device this month because all LNG-IUS devices share a 5-year contraceptive licence in Australia
Continue the current device for contraception until 8 years from insertion, then replace
Continue indefinitely as LNG-IUS devices are effective until menopause once inserted after age 25
Replace with a copper IUD now to take advantage of its 10-year licence

stem: A 24-year-old woman attends a sexual-health clinic 96 hours after a single episode of condom failure. Her cycles are regular at 28 days; today is day 16 (intercourse occurred close to her predicted ovulation). She has a BMI of 33 and uses no regular medications. She declines an intrauterine device today because she is needle-phobic about the speculum/insertion. She has no allergies. She wants the oral option with the lowest failure rate given the 96-hour interval and her BMI, and she accepts that she will need to wait 5 days before resuming hormonal contraception if started on this method.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate oral emergency contraceptive in this scenario?

options.t: Levonorgestrel 1.5 mg orally as a single dose now
Levonorgestrel 3 mg orally as a single dose now, doubled for BMI
Ulipristal acetate 30 mg orally as a single dose now
Yuzpe regimen using combined oral contraceptive pills now
Commence the combined oral contraceptive pill today and abstain for 7 days

stem: A 26-year-old G1P0 woman at 9 weeks presents with three weeks of progressive nausea and vomiting, six to ten episodes daily, unable to keep down most fluids, 3.5 kg weight loss (5%), PUQE score 14. She has tried small frequent meals, ginger and oral pyridoxine 25 mg three times daily without benefit. BP 102/68 with a 15 mmHg postural drop, HR 104, dry mucous membranes, ketones 3+ on urinalysis. Na 134, K 3.3, urea 7.8. TSH normal. Ultrasound: single live intrauterine pregnancy. She has been admitted, IV fluids with thiamine have been commenced and the team is choosing the next antiemetic step.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next antiemetic step?

options.t: Add oral doxylamine 12.5 mg with pyridoxine 25 mg four times daily as second-line therapy
Switch directly to oral ondansetron 8 mg three times daily as monotherapy
Commence IV methylprednisolone 16 mg three times daily today
Discharge home on oral ginger and reassure that symptoms typically improve by 12 weeks
Commence intramuscular prochlorperazine 12.5 mg every 6 hours as monotherapy

stem: A 29-year-old G2P1 RhD-negative woman is at 13 weeks and 5 days gestation by certain LMP confirmed on first-trimester ultrasound. She presents with a 48-hour history of fresh red vaginal bleeding which has now settled, and has passed several small clots at home. She is haemodynamically stable. At booking she was confirmed Rh(D) negative with a negative antibody screen, and the screen remains negative today. The previous pregnancy ended in a term vaginal birth without complications and she did not receive routine antenatal anti-D in that pregnancy (declined at the time). TVUS today shows a single live intrauterine pregnancy of 13 weeks 5 days with appropriate growth. The decision is to manage expectantly with follow-up in one week.
lead_in: What is the appropriate anti-D immunoglobulin regimen for this presentation in Australian practice?

options.t: No anti-D is required as bleeding has settled and antibody screen is negative
Anti-D 250 IU IM as a single dose now
Anti-D 625 IU IM as a single dose now
Anti-D 1500 IU IM as a single dose now
Withhold anti-D until Kleihauer testing confirms feto-maternal haemorrhage

subtopic_detail: GBS screening - timing of universal swab
stem: A 33-year-old G3P2 woman attends her midwife at 35 weeks and 4 days. Her two prior births were uncomplicated term vaginal deliveries; her second neonate developed early-onset GBS sepsis confirmed on blood culture at 18 hours of life and recovered fully after IV benzylpenicillin. This pregnancy has been uncomplicated with normal antenatal serology and OGTT. There has been no preterm labour, no PROM, no fever and no GBS bacteriuria this pregnancy. The local maternity service offers either universal culture-based screening at 35-37 weeks or a risk-factor pathway, and her midwife is planning intrapartum management.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate plan regarding GBS screening and intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis for this woman?

options.t: Collect a low vaginal-anorectal swab now and only give intrapartum prophylaxis if positive
Collect a low vaginal-anorectal swab at 36-37 weeks and decide prophylaxis on the result
Do not screen; give intrapartum IV benzylpenicillin in labour regardless of swab status
Do not screen and do not give intrapartum prophylaxis as she had no GBS bacteriuria this pregnancy
Screen on admission in labour using a rapid intrapartum PCR and treat if positive

stem: A 34-year-old woman has her routine 5-yearly cervical screening test through her GP using clinician-collected liquid-based cytology and HPV. She has had two previous CSTs, both negative for oncogenic HPV. She received the quadrivalent HPV vaccine series at age 14. She has no abnormal bleeding, no postcoital bleeding and no pelvic pain. Today's result: HPV oncogenic non-16/18 detected; reflex liquid-based cytology shows possible high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (pHSIL). She is not pregnant.
lead_in: Under the Australian National Cervical Screening Program, what is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Repeat HPV cervical screening test in 12 months
Repeat HPV cervical screening test in 5 years as her prior history is reassuring
Refer directly for colposcopy
Refer directly for diagnostic cone biopsy without colposcopy
Offer a self-collected vaginal HPV swab in 12 months to confirm the result

stem: A 31-year-old G2P2 woman attends her child-health-nurse appointment 6 weeks after an uncomplicated term vaginal birth. She describes 4 weeks of low mood, poor sleep beyond infant needs, anhedonia and intrusive thoughts she is a 'bad mother'. She denies thoughts of self-harm or harm to the baby. She has a supportive partner and is breastfeeding well. There is no history of bipolar disorder, prior psychiatric admissions or postpartum psychosis. The nurse administers the COPE-recommended screening instrument: she scores 16 out of 30 with item 10 (self-harm thoughts) scored 0. There are no language or literacy barriers.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Repeat the EPDS in 2-4 weeks without GP referral as the score is below threshold
Refer to her GP for clinical assessment for postnatal depression within 1-2 weeks
Refer to the emergency department for acute psychiatric assessment today
Commence sertraline 50 mg daily today without GP review as it is safe in breastfeeding
Reassure that this represents normal 'baby blues' and review at the 8-week immunisation visit

subtopic_detail: Menopause MHT - first-line for vasomotor symptoms
stem: A 52-year-old woman attends her general practitioner with 14 months of severe vasomotor symptoms (multiple hot flushes daily, drenching night sweats, sleep disruption). She has had no menstrual period for 18 months. She has an intact uterus. She has no history of breast cancer, no personal or family history of venous thromboembolism, no migraine with aura, BMI 26, BP 124/78, is a non-smoker, and has minimal alcohol intake. Her FBC, LFTs, lipids and TSH are normal. Mammogram and cervical screening are up to date. She has tried non-pharmacological measures without benefit and would like to commence menopausal hormone therapy. She has read about transdermal oestrogen and asks which regimen is recommended.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line MHT regimen?

options.t: Oral conjugated equine oestrogen alone, daily continuous regimen
Oral oestradiol with cyclical oral medroxyprogesterone, daily regimen
Transdermal oestradiol patch with continuous combined micronised progesterone
Oral tibolone 2.5 mg daily as continuous monotherapy
Vaginal oestriol cream 0.5 mg twice weekly as monotherapy

stem: A 27-year-old G2P0 (one prior tubal ectopic managed by salpingostomy 18 months ago) presents to the early pregnancy assessment unit at an estimated 6 weeks and 4 days gestation by LMP with 36 hours of crampy left lower quadrant pain and intermittent dark vaginal spotting. She is haemodynamically stable. The current pregnancy was conceived spontaneously and she did not use contraception. She has no shoulder tip pain. Her partner has accompanied her.
lead_in: What is the single most likely diagnosis?

options.t: Threatened miscarriage of viable intrauterine pregnancy with subchorionic haematoma
Complete miscarriage with retained corpus luteum on adnexa
Pregnancy of unknown location (PUL) requiring serial beta-hCG monitoring
Tubal ectopic pregnancy in the right ampulla
Haemorrhagic corpus luteum cyst with concurrent early intrauterine pregnancy

stem: A 32-year-old G1P0 woman attends for booking ultrasound at an estimated 10 weeks and 2 days gestation by certain LMP. She has had no bleeding or abdominal pain. Her early pregnancy symptoms (nausea, breast tenderness) settled approximately 7 days ago. She has no significant medical history, is on folate 500 mcg and iodine 150 mcg daily, and has no allergies. She has not had a previous miscarriage, ectopic, or IVF cycle, and the dating by LMP correlates with sonographic measurements within the expected range. The transvaginal ultrasound is performed by an accredited sonographer with appropriate views obtained on a high-resolution probe in a tertiary unit.
lead_in: Which finding most definitively confirms early pregnancy loss in this scan?

options.t: Crown-rump length >=7 mm with no foetal cardiac activity on TVUS
Mean gestational sac diameter >=25 mm with no embryo on TVUS
Beta-hCG plateau over 48 hours with empty uterus on TVUS
Loss of pregnancy symptoms during the first trimester with closed cervical os
Mean sac diameter to CRL ratio less than 5 mm on TVUS

stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 woman at 36 weeks and 4 days gestation is brought to the birth suite by ambulance following a witnessed generalised tonic-clonic seizure at home lasting 90 seconds. She had been complaining of a 24-hour frontal headache and epigastric pain. There is no known history of epilepsy, head injury, illicit drug use or hyponatraemia. She had documented systolic readings of 150-160 mmHg at her two most recent antenatal visits with proteinuria 2+ on dipstick. On arrival she is post-ictal, drowsy but maintaining her airway, and there is no further seizure activity. The foetal heart rate is 138 bpm with a reassuring CTG.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line drug to administer now to prevent further seizures?

options.t: Intravenous diazepam 5 mg slow push, repeat at 5 minutes
Intravenous phenytoin loading 1 g over 20 minutes
Intravenous magnesium sulphate 4 g loading then 1 g/hour maintenance
Intravenous levetiracetam 1.5 g over 5 minutes as load
Immediate Caesarean section under general anaesthesia without anticonvulsant therapy

stem: A 34-year-old G3P2 woman at 31 weeks and 2 days gestation presents with sudden painless bright red vaginal bleeding while resting on the couch. The bleeding has filled one sanitary pad in 30 minutes. She has had no abdominal pain, no contractions, no rupture of membranes and reports normal foetal movements. Her previous two births were by lower-segment Caesarean section for breech and for failure to progress. At her 20-week morphology scan the placenta was reported as anterior, fundal and well clear of the internal os. She is a non-smoker, denies cocaine or other substance use, and there has been no recent intercourse or trauma. She is haemodynamically stable.
lead_in: What is the most likely cause of her antepartum haemorrhage?

options.t: Placental abruption with concealed retroplacental haematoma
Low-lying placenta or placenta praevia covering internal os
Vasa praevia with foetal vessels traversing the os
Uterine rupture along a prior LSCS scar
Cervical ectropion bleed from friable columnar epithelium

stem: A 29-year-old G2P1 woman at 38 weeks and 6 days gestation is admitted in early labour. The foetus is in transverse lie despite an ECV attempt one week prior, and the obstetric team is considering Caesarean section. Spontaneous rupture of membranes occurs during the admission examination with a moderate gush of clear liquor. Immediately afterward the midwife identifies a pulsating loop of umbilical cord protruding through the cervix into the vagina. The CTG shows an abrupt foetal heart rate deceleration to 80 bpm. The obstetric team is being called and the emergency theatre is being readied for Category 1 Caesarean section.
lead_in: What is the most important immediate manoeuvre while the theatre is being prepared?

options.t: Replace cord gently into uterus through cervix, observe maternal vitals while preparing for delivery
Manually elevate the presenting part off the cord and place in knee-chest or steep Trendelenburg position
Start IV oxytocin augmentation to expedite vaginal birth at 3 cm cervical dilatation in current transverse lie
Fill the bladder with 500 mL warmed saline via Foley catheter only and avoid manual elevation of presenting part
Administer terbutaline 250 mcg SC to reduce contractions and observe the cord pulsation before further intervention

stem: A 30-year-old G1P0 woman attends the birth suite at 39 weeks and 1 day gestation reporting a sudden gush of clear fluid 4 hours ago with ongoing leakage. She has no contractions, no fever, normal foetal movements, and no per-vaginal bleeding. Her pregnancy has been uncomplicated: booking bloods normal, OGTT normal at 28 weeks, group B streptococcus low vaginal-anorectal swab at 36 weeks was negative, and her 20-week anatomy scan was normal. She has no allergies, takes no regular medications, and is a non-smoker.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.t: Discharge home with daily community midwife review and induction if not labouring by 96 hours after rupture
Offer induction of labour now or expectant management for up to 24 hours, with shared decision-making
Proceed to immediate Category 2 Caesarean section regardless of patient preference or GBS status today
Commence broad-spectrum IV antibiotics now regardless of GBS status, temperature, or duration of rupture
Tocolysis with oral nifedipine and IM corticosteroids for foetal lung maturation before any delivery plan

stem: A 24-year-old nulligravid woman attends her general practice with a 3-year history of progressively worsening cyclical pelvic pain. Pain begins 1-2 days before menses, peaks on day 1-2, and is associated with deep dyspareunia and occasional dyschezia. She has no urinary symptoms, no postcoital bleeding and a normal cervical screening test 18 months ago. She has tried regular ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily during menses with partial benefit. She is in a stable monogamous relationship, is sexually active and is not currently planning pregnancy in the next 2 years; she is open to hormonal therapy and has no contraindications to oestrogen.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.t: Diagnostic laparoscopy with peritoneal biopsy before initiating any empirical medical therapy in clinic
Commence GnRH agonist (e.g. goserelin) with hormonal add-back therapy from the outset
Trial of continuous combined oral contraceptive pill plus NSAID as needed for pain
Refer for laparoscopic hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy as first-line therapy
Serum CA-125 testing to confirm endometriosis diagnosis before any therapy is initiated

stem: A 22-year-old nulligravid woman attends her GP with 18 months of oligomenorrhoea (cycles 45-90 days), facial and chest hair growth requiring weekly waxing, weight gain of 6 kg over 12 months, and acanthosis nigricans. BMI 29, modified Ferriman-Gallwey 12. Total testosterone is at the upper limit of normal, SHBG low, free androgen index elevated, 17-hydroxyprogesterone normal, TSH and prolactin normal, beta-hCG negative. Pelvic ultrasound is technically suboptimal due to bowel gas: each ovary appears to contain 'multiple small follicles' but follicle count cannot be quantified and ovarian volumes are not reported. Anti-Mullerian hormone is 58 pmol/L (markedly elevated).
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step under the 2023 International Evidence-Based PCOS Guideline?

options.t: Diagnose PCOS now on the basis of oligo-anovulation plus clinical and biochemical hyperandrogenism
Defer diagnosis until a repeat pelvic ultrasound quantifies follicle count and ovarian volume
Defer diagnosis as elevated AMH alone is sufficient and other criteria are not required
Defer diagnosis until LH:FSH ratio confirms elevated LH consistent with PCOS
Defer diagnosis until adrenal hyperplasia is excluded with an ACTH stimulation test

stem: A 44-year-old G3P3 woman attends her general practitioner with 8 months of progressively heavier menstrual bleeding. Her cycles remain regular at 28 days but periods now last 9-10 days with passage of clots and she requires double protection (tampon and pad) on the heaviest days. She has needed to take time off work and has felt increasingly fatigued. There is no intermenstrual or postcoital bleeding. She is not on hormonal therapy or anticoagulants. There is no family history of bleeding disorders. Cervical screening is up to date and negative. She had a normal pelvic ultrasound 18 months ago for unrelated symptoms.
lead_in: Using the FIGO PALM-COEIN classification, which is the most likely cause of her abnormal uterine bleeding?

options.t: AUB-P (endometrial polyp on TVUS)
AUB-A (adenomyosis on TVUS/MRI)
AUB-L (leiomyoma/fibroid on TVUS)
AUB-O (ovulatory dysfunction on history)
AUB-C (coagulopathy on bleeding history)

stem: A 31-year-old G2P1 woman (one prior uncomplicated term vaginal birth 3 years ago, birth weight 3,650 g) attends for her routine 28-week 75 g oral glucose tolerance test. She has a BMI of 30 (pre-pregnancy 28), a sister with type 2 diabetes diagnosed at age 38, and identifies as South Asian. Her booking HbA1c was 5.5% and booking fasting glucose was 4.7 mmol/L. The pregnancy has been otherwise uncomplicated: BP averaging 110-118/68-74, fundal height appropriate, foetal movements normal, 20-week morphology scan normal. She is on pregnancy multivitamin only, no allergies, non-smoker, social drinker (ceased), and has had her influenza and pertussis vaccinations. She follows a primarily vegetarian diet and walks 30 minutes most days.
lead_in: Using ADIPS 2014 (RANZCOG-endorsed) 75 g OGTT thresholds, which interpretation is correct?

options.t: Normal OGTT; reassure and continue routine antenatal care without insulin or metformin
Impaired fasting glucose only - repeat OGTT in 4 weeks before commencing therapy
GDM diagnosed - fasting and 2-hour values both at or above ADIPS thresholds
Overt diabetes in pregnancy - commence subcutaneous insulin immediately at booking visit
GDM diagnosed - the 1-hour value alone is diagnostic per ADIPS criteria

stem: A 32-year-old G3P2 woman is in second-stage labour at 40 weeks and 4 days gestation in a public hospital birth suite. Her two previous births were uncomplicated vaginal deliveries at 3,750 g and 3,920 g. This pregnancy was complicated by gestational diabetes managed on metformin with reasonable glycaemic control (most fasting BGLs 5.0-5.4, postprandial 6.5-7.2). The 36-week growth scan suggested an EFW of 4,250 g (95th centile). She has been pushing for 25 minutes. The foetal head delivers in OA position but appears to retract tightly against the perineum (turtle sign). Routine downward traction does not deliver the anterior shoulder. The midwife calls the obstetric emergency.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line manoeuvre after calling for help?

options.t: Apply fundal pressure to dislodge the anterior shoulder from behind the symphysis
Perform McRoberts manoeuvre and apply suprapubic pressure as first manoeuvres
Proceed directly to Zavanelli manoeuvre and emergency Caesarean section now
Perform symphysiotomy at the bedside before any other manoeuvre is attempted
Cut the umbilical cord at the perineum and proceed to emergency Caesarean

stem: A 22-year-old nulligravid woman attends the sexual health service with 5 days of progressive lower abdominal pain, deep dyspareunia and abnormal vaginal discharge. She is sexually active with a new male partner over the past 2 months, has not used condoms consistently, and is on the COCP. Her cervical screening test is up to date and negative. She has no past surgical history, no allergies and is otherwise well. There is no urinary frequency, dysuria, fever, vomiting or rectal bleeding. She is haemodynamically stable and not pregnant. She has no IUD and no history of ectopic pregnancy. She has not yet had any antibiotics.
lead_in: Which empirical outpatient antibiotic regimen is recommended by Therapeutic Guidelines for mild-moderate PID?

options.t: Amoxicillin 1 g PO three times daily for 7 days plus metronidazole 400 mg BD for 7 days
Ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO twice daily for 14 days plus metronidazole 400 mg BD for 14 days
Doxycycline 100 mg PO twice daily for 14 days as monotherapy without gonorrhoea or anaerobe cover
Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM stat, plus doxycycline 100 mg BD for 14 days, plus metronidazole 400 mg BD
Azithromycin 1 g PO single dose alone without ceftriaxone, doxycycline or metronidazole cover today

stem: A 38-year-old G3P3 woman attends her general practice for contraceptive counselling. Her youngest child is 4 months old and exclusively breastfed. Her BMI is 33. She has migraine with aura (typical scintillating scotoma followed by unilateral headache, average twice per month) since age 19. She had a left ankle ORIF 6 weeks ago for a complex fracture and remains in a removable boot, mobilising with crutches; her physiotherapist plans to discontinue the boot in 2 weeks. Her mother had a provoked DVT on the combined oral contraceptive at age 25. She is planning a long-haul flight (16 hours) in two weeks, which already imposed by her reduced mobility post-fracture. She is normotensive (118/74) and a non-smoker. She has no liver disease, no breast cancer history, no current pelvic infection and no abnormal uterine bleeding.
lead_in: Which contraceptive method is most appropriate at this consultation?

options.t: Combined oral contraceptive pill - daily continuous regimen
Combined transdermal contraceptive patch - weekly application regimen
Levonorgestrel 52 mg intrauterine system (Mirena) - clinic insertion
Depot medroxyprogesterone acetate (DMPA) - IM every 12 weeks
Progestogen-only pill (desogestrel) - daily continuous regimen

subtopic_detail: STEMI ECG criteria - posterior MI recognition
stem: A 58-year-old man presents to a regional emergency department with 90 minutes of crushing central chest pain radiating to the left arm, associated with nausea and diaphoresis. He has a history of hypertension and is an ex-smoker. He took two of his wife's GTN sprays without relief. The nearest primary PCI centre is 45 minutes away by road. Vital signs show HR 92, BP 138/86, SpO2 98% on room air, RR 18. He is in distress but mentating well. The initial 12-lead ECG shows 1.5 mm ST depression in V1 to V3 with prominent R waves in V2, upright T waves in V1 to V3, and 1 mm ST depression in II, III and aVF. There is no anterior ST elevation. A right-sided and posterior lead ECG is performed and shows 1 mm ST elevation in V7, V8 and V9. High-sensitivity troponin returns at 320 ng/L (reference <14). He has no contraindications to fibrinolysis.
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate reperfusion strategy?

options.t: Conservative medical management with aspirin, ticagrelor and enoxaparin only, with serial troponins
Transfer for primary PCI without giving fibrinolysis, accepting the 45 minute transfer delay plus catheter-lab activation time, with full anticoagulation en route
Administer fibrinolysis with tenecteplase now, plus aspirin, clopidogrel and enoxaparin, then transfer for pharmaco-invasive PCI within 3 to 24 hours
Treat the changes as non-ST-elevation, give aspirin, ticagrelor and fondaparinux, and refer for angiography within 72 hours
Repeat ECG in 30 minutes and reassess; if persistent changes, then consider fibrinolysis

subtopic_detail: NSTE-ACS - timing of invasive strategy and GRACE risk
stem: A 72-year-old woman presents to a tertiary metropolitan emergency department with three episodes of central chest pain over the last 12 hours, each lasting around 15 minutes, the last occurring 90 minutes ago at rest while she was reading on her couch. She has type 2 diabetes mellitus (HbA1c 8.2%), longstanding hypertension, chronic kidney disease stage 3a (eGFR 52, baseline stable), and was admitted 18 months ago with a non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction managed conservatively at that admission. Her current medications are aspirin 100 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg, perindopril 5 mg, metformin 1 g twice daily and gliclazide modified-release 60 mg. She is an ex-smoker (quit 8 years ago, 25 pack-years prior) and has a sister with CABG at 64. Vital signs at triage are HR 88 regular, BP 152/84, SpO2 97% on room air, RR 18, afebrile. She is mildly distressed but well perfused with warm peripheries. Examination is unremarkable apart from a grade 2 soft systolic murmur at the apex without radiation. The 12-lead ECG shows 1 mm horizontal ST depression in V4 to V6 with biphasic T waves in V5 and V6; there is no ST elevation in any territory and no posterior or right-sided changes on additional leads. Initial high-sensitivity troponin is 78 ng/L (reference <14) drawn at 1 hour after symptom resolution. There is no pulmonary oedema clinically or radiographically and no haemodynamic instability. The cardiology registrar has calculated a GRACE score of 158.
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate timing of coronary angiography?

options.t: Within 24 hours of presentation, as part of an early invasive strategy
Within 2 hours of presentation, as part of an immediate invasive strategy
Within 72 hours of presentation, as part of a delayed invasive strategy
Selective invasive strategy guided by non-invasive ischaemia testing after discharge
Defer angiography given CKD stage 3a; manage medically and reassess at 6 weeks

stem: A 61-year-old man underwent successful primary PCI with a single drug-eluting stent to the proximal LAD 6 weeks ago for anterior STEMI. He has been compliant with aspirin 100 mg daily, ticagrelor 90 mg twice daily, atorvastatin 80 mg, perindopril 5 mg, and metoprolol 50 mg twice daily. He has no atrial fibrillation, no prior stroke, eGFR 78, and no prior bleeding. He attends cardiology review and asks about antiplatelet duration. Two weeks ago he was diagnosed with a 3 cm right colonic adenocarcinoma on screening colonoscopy and is scheduled for elective laparoscopic right hemicolectomy in 4 weeks. He has had no chest pain since the procedure.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate antiplatelet management plan around the upcoming hemicolectomy?

options.t: Continue aspirin perioperatively, stop ticagrelor 5 days before surgery, and restart ticagrelor as soon as surgical haemostasis allows (typically 24 to 72 hours postoperatively)
Stop both aspirin and ticagrelor 5 days before surgery and resume both after discharge
Continue both aspirin and ticagrelor throughout the perioperative period without interruption
Bridge ticagrelor with prophylactic low molecular weight heparin from 5 days pre-op until restart of ticagrelor
Proceed with surgery now without changing antiplatelet therapy because the stent was placed more than a month ago

stem: An 82-year-old woman attends her general practice for review of newly diagnosed non-valvular atrial fibrillation found on a 24 hour Holter for palpitations. Her history includes essential hypertension on perindopril-indapamide, ischaemic stroke 3 years ago with full recovery, and no diabetes or heart failure. She weighs 56 kg. Current medications: perindopril-indapamide 5/1.25 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg, paracetamol as required. BP 132/80, HR 78 irregularly irregular. Bloods today: creatinine 138 micromol/L, eGFR 38 mL/min/1.73m^2, HCO3 24. She has no prior bleeding and her HAS-BLED score is 2. She prefers an oral medication strategy without routine INR monitoring.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate stroke-prevention prescription?

options.t: Apixaban 5 mg twice daily as standard non-valvular AF dose
Apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily based on age, weight, and creatinine criteria
Warfarin titrated to INR 2.0 to 3.0 with point-of-care monitoring
Rivaroxaban 20 mg once daily with the main meal of the day
Aspirin 100 mg daily as sole long-term antithrombotic therapy

subtopic_detail: Rate vs rhythm control in AF with heart failure
stem: A 58-year-old man with HFrEF (LVEF 32% on TTE 4 weeks ago) and persistent atrial fibrillation diagnosed 7 months ago is reviewed in the dedicated heart failure clinic of a metropolitan tertiary hospital. He is on sacubitril/valsartan 49/51 mg twice daily, bisoprolol 7.5 mg daily, spironolactone 25 mg daily, dapagliflozin 10 mg daily and apixaban 5 mg twice daily, all at maximum tolerated doses with documented adherence on pharmacy refill records. Despite these guideline-directed therapies, he reports NYHA class III symptoms with breathlessness when flat-walking 50 metres on level ground and orthopnoea requiring two pillows at night, with one episode of paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea in the past week. Resting heart rate is 78 in AF on the clinic ECG; 6 minute walk test today is 280 metres (down from 360 m at his last review 3 months ago). He had one direct current cardioversion 4 months ago, which restored sinus rhythm initially with relapse to AF within 6 weeks despite continuation of beta-blocker therapy. He has no significant comorbidities (no diabetes, no CKD, no prior stroke) and lives 30 minutes from a metropolitan electrophysiology centre with established AF ablation programme. Symptoms have not improved despite optimal heart failure medical therapy for 3 months and adequate rate control.
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step to improve symptoms and outcomes?

options.t: Increase bisoprolol to 10 mg daily for tighter rate control and continue current management
Add amiodarone 200 mg daily after loading, for pharmacological rhythm control
Repeat DCCV and load amiodarone for maintenance
AV node ablation with biventricular pacing
Refer for catheter ablation of atrial fibrillation as the rhythm-control strategy of choice in his HFrEF

subtopic_detail: HFrEF - four pillars guideline-directed medical therapy
stem: A 62-year-old man with newly diagnosed HFrEF (LVEF 28% on transthoracic echocardiogram performed during admission) presents to the heart failure clinic at a metropolitan tertiary centre 2 weeks after discharge from his index admission with acute pulmonary oedema. The admission echocardiogram showed global hypokinesis without focal wall motion abnormality, mild left atrial dilatation and no significant valvular disease. Cardiac MRI during admission excluded an infiltrative cardiomyopathy and showed no late gadolinium enhancement suggestive of myocarditis. Coronary angiography demonstrated non-obstructive coronary artery disease. He is in sinus rhythm. Current medications post-discharge are ramipril 5 mg daily, bisoprolol 5 mg daily, and frusemide 40 mg daily. He has type 2 diabetes mellitus (HbA1c 7.4%, on metformin 1 g twice daily), no atrial fibrillation, no significant valve disease, eGFR 64, potassium 4.2 mmol/L, sodium 138 mmol/L, BP 122/76 supine and 118/74 standing, HR 64 sinus rhythm, and NYHA class II symptoms when walking up two flights of stairs. He has no history of angio-oedema, no symptomatic hypotension or dizziness, and no recent gout flare. His weight is 88 kg (down 4 kg since admission with diuresis) and he is keen to optimise medical therapy following written education from the heart failure nurse practitioner.
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next addition or modification to his heart failure regimen?

options.t: Switch ramipril to sacubitril/valsartan and add spironolactone 12.5 to 25 mg daily and dapagliflozin 10 mg daily over subsequent visits
Add digoxin 125 micrograms daily for symptom relief
Add ivabradine 5 mg twice daily
Increase frusemide to 80 mg daily and add metolazone 2.5 mg as required
Refer immediately for cardiac resynchronisation therapy with defibrillator (CRT-D) without further uptitration of oral guideline-directed medical therapy

subtopic_detail: HFpEF - SGLT2 inhibitor as first-line disease-modifying therapy
stem: A 73-year-old woman presents to a community cardiology clinic with progressive exertional dyspnoea over 6 months, now limiting her ability to walk to her letterbox without stopping. She has longstanding essential hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus (HbA1c 7.6%, on metformin 1 g twice daily and gliclazide modified-release 60 mg daily), obesity (BMI 34, weight 92 kg), and moderate obstructive sleep apnoea well-controlled on nightly CPAP at 9 cmH2O with good compliance documented. She had a hospital admission 3 months ago for fluid overload requiring intravenous diuresis and was discharged on oral frusemide as needed. Her examination today shows BP 138/82, HR 76 sinus, JVP not elevated, mild bibasal end-inspiratory crackles, and no peripheral oedema. Transthoracic echocardiogram performed last week shows preserved LVEF 58%, grade II diastolic dysfunction, left atrial volume index 38 mL/m2 (dilated), septal e' velocity 6 cm/s, E/e' ratio of 14, and no significant valvular disease. NT-proBNP is 612 pg/mL (elevated for her age and BMI, after BMI correction). Her current medications are perindopril 10 mg daily, indapamide 2.5 mg, metformin 1 g BD, gliclazide MR 60 mg, and atorvastatin 40 mg. Renal function: eGFR 58, K 4.3 mmol/L, creatinine 92 micromol/L. There is no history of gout, recurrent urinary infection, or genital fungal infection. She is keen on therapies that will reduce hospitalisation and would prefer to avoid additional injectable medications.
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate addition to her management for heart failure outcomes?

options.t: Add sacubitril/valsartan 49/51 mg twice daily
Add spironolactone 25 mg daily
Add bisoprolol 2.5 mg daily
Add empagliflozin 10 mg daily as first-line disease-modifying therapy for HFpEF
Increase frusemide as required and emphasise weight loss and CPAP adherence only

subtopic_detail: Cardiac vs vasovagal syncope - high-risk features
stem: A 72-year-old man with hypertension and a 30 pack-year smoking history presents to ED after collapsing without warning while shopping. Bystanders report he became briefly stiff with a few arm jerks; he was unresponsive for around 30 seconds and recovered immediately on the floor with no confusion. He had two similar episodes in the past 2 months, one while sitting watching television. Examination: BP 142/82 lying, 140/82 standing, HR 60 sinus, no postural drop, normal cardiovascular and neurological exam. ECG: sinus rhythm 58, PR 220 ms, QRS 110 ms, RBBB plus left anterior fascicular block. Bloods within reference. He drives a private car daily for his work as a courier.
lead_in: Which combination is most appropriate as his next steps?

options.t: Admit for telemetry and TTE, advise him not to drive a private vehicle until cause is identified and treated, and notify him of his obligation to inform the licensing authority
Admit for telemetry and TTE but allow private driving once telemetry is normal for 48 hours
Discharge with reassurance, advise hydration and counterpressure manoeuvres for vasovagal syncope, and continue driving
Arrange outpatient CT brain and EEG for suspected new-onset seizure disorder and advise no driving for 6 months under epilepsy rules
Discharge home for outpatient 24-hour Holter and tilt-table testing within 4 weeks; continue driving in the interim

stem: A 46-year-old woman is referred to a metropolitan hypertension clinic with progressively worsening hypertension that has resisted intensification over 18 months. Her home blood pressure log, taken with a validated upper-arm device twice daily, averages 162/96 mmHg despite perindopril 10 mg, amlodipine 10 mg, and indapamide 2.5 mg taken daily with documented adherence on pharmacy refill records over the past 12 months. She has no obstructive sleep apnoea symptoms (no snoring, no witnessed apnoeas, Epworth 4), no flushing or palpitations, no abdominal bruit on examination, and no family history of phaeochromocytoma, paraganglioma or thyroid medullary carcinoma. She has had intermittent hypokalaemia (lowest documented potassium 3.1 mmol/L) attributed by her referring GP to indapamide. Her most recent UEC shows: Na 142, K 3.4, Cl 102, HCO3 28 mmol/L, urea 5.6, creatinine 72 micromol/L, eGFR 89. Fasting glucose 5.4 mmol/L, HbA1c 5.7%. TSH and free T4 within reference range. Urinary catecholamine and metanephrine screen normal on two separate 24-hour collections. Renal artery Doppler ultrasonography shows no evidence of renal artery stenosis bilaterally. ECG shows left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria (Sokolow-Lyon positive). She is keen to investigate a potentially reversible secondary cause given her young age and target-organ damage on ECG.
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next investigation?

options.t: 24-hour urinary metanephrines (repeat)
MR angiography of renal arteries
Overnight 1 mg dexamethasone suppression test
Plasma aldosterone-to-renin ratio after appropriate medication adjustment
Add spironolactone 25 mg empirically and reassess BP at 12 weeks

subtopic_detail: NHFA hypertension - lifestyle as first-line for grade 1 low-risk
stem: A 38-year-old man presents to his GP for a workplace medical. He is asymptomatic, exercises twice a week, drinks 8 standard drinks weekly, has BMI 28, does not smoke, has no diabetes, and no family history of premature CVD. Three separate clinic BP readings over 6 weeks average 156/96, and home BP averaged over 7 days is 152/94. Bloods: total cholesterol 4.6, LDL 2.8, HDL 1.4, triglycerides 1.2, fasting glucose 5.1, eGFR over 90, normal urea/electrolytes and urine ACR. ECG normal. His Australian absolute 5-year CV risk (NVDPA calculator) is 4 percent. He has tried 12 weeks of structured lifestyle change with his GP including reduced salt, DASH-style diet, alcohol reduction to 8 standard drinks per week, and increased exercise; BP today remains 154/94.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Commence perindopril 5 mg daily and continue ongoing lifestyle measures, with home BP review at 6 weeks
Continue lifestyle modification for another 6 months before considering pharmacotherapy
Refer for 24-hour ambulatory BP monitoring to exclude white-coat hypertension before any pharmacotherapy
Commence atorvastatin 20 mg daily for global CV risk reduction in addition to lifestyle
Investigate for secondary hypertension with plasma aldosterone-renin ratio, renal artery imaging and 24-hour urinary metanephrines today

subtopic_detail: Mobitz II AV block - indication for permanent pacemaker
stem: A 68-year-old woman with hypertension and hyperlipidaemia is admitted to a regional hospital with two episodes of presyncope over 48 hours. She has no chest pain. Her medications are perindopril 5 mg and atorvastatin 40 mg; she takes no AV-blocking agents. On telemetry she has predominantly sinus rhythm at 62 bpm with intermittent dropped QRS complexes; the preceding PR intervals are constant at 200 ms before each non-conducted P wave. The QRS during conducted beats is 140 ms with a right bundle branch block morphology. Her BP is 132/76 between episodes and drops to 84/52 during prolonged pauses (longest documented pause 4.2 seconds). Echocardiogram is structurally normal with LVEF 60%. Bloods: TSH 2.1, electrolytes normal, troponin negative, no infection on screen, no Lyme exposure. She has no reversible cause identified (no recent cardiac surgery, no AV-blocking medications, no acute MI, no electrolyte derangement).
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate definitive management?

options.t: Permanent dual-chamber pacemaker implantation under Class I indication for symptomatic Mobitz II AV block
Atropine 600 microgram IV bolus and observe
Isoprenaline infusion and discharge with outpatient cardiology follow-up
Reduce perindopril and observe with daily telemetry on the ward
Electrophysiology study with potential AV node ablation as a definitive procedure to control her bradyarrhythmia and presyncope episodes

subtopic_detail: Acute coronary syndrome - reperfusion choice and timing in STEMI
stem: A 58-year-old man presents to a rural hospital ED with 90 minutes of crushing central chest pain radiating to the left jaw, associated with diaphoresis and nausea. He has a 30 pack-year smoking history, dyslipidaemia on atorvastatin 40 mg, and untreated hypertension. He took two GTN sprays at home with minimal relief. He has no prior bleeding history, no recent surgery, no stroke, and is not on anticoagulation. Initial ECG shows 3 mm ST elevation in V1 to V4 with reciprocal inferior ST depression. The nearest PCI-capable centre is 180 minutes away by road, and the local rural transfer team estimates first-medical-contact-to-balloon time of 200 minutes if transferred immediately. The local ED can administer fibrinolysis within 20 minutes. Troponin I has been sent; bedside echocardiogram shows anteroseptal hypokinesis. The team specifically asks whether transfer for primary PCI is still preferred even with predicted door-to-balloon exceeding 120 minutes, whether to delay reperfusion pending troponin confirmation, whether IV unfractionated heparin alone with next-day angiography would be safe, and whether conservative anti-ischaemic therapy with high-dose nitrates and beta-blockade is acceptable given only minor reciprocal changes.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate reperfusion strategy?

options.t: Transfer immediately for primary PCI even though first-medical-contact-to-balloon time will exceed 120 minutes
Administer fibrinolysis with tenecteplase now and arrange transfer for angiography within 3 to 24 hours
Withhold reperfusion and load with ticagrelor and aspirin while awaiting troponin result
Give intravenous unfractionated heparin alone and transfer for elective angiography next day
Treat conservatively with high-dose nitrates and beta-blockade given the small reciprocal change

subtopic_detail: Posterior STEMI - ECG recognition with isolated ST depression in V1 to V3
stem: A 67-year-old man presents with 2 hours of severe central chest pain radiating to the back, associated with diaphoresis and nausea. He has a history of hypertension on amlodipine and a 40 pack-year smoking history. He has no prior cardiac history. On arrival he is in pain, clammy and pale. The 12-lead ECG shows horizontal ST depression of 2 mm in V1, V2 and V3 with prominent upright R-waves in V1 and V2 (R/S greater than 1), tall upright T waves in V1 to V3, and no ST elevation or depression in any other lead. There is no Q-wave. A posterior 15-lead ECG with leads V7, V8 and V9 placed below the left scapula shows 1 mm ST elevation in V8 and V9. Troponin I is pending. Bedside echocardiogram shows inferolateral wall hypokinesis. The ED registrar asks whether to manage as NSTEMI with conservative pathway and 24-hour angiography, whether the ECG could represent Wellens syndrome, right bundle branch block, or digoxin effect, and whether emergency cath lab activation is justified for a STEMI equivalent in the absence of overt ST elevation on the standard 12-lead.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Manage as NSTEMI with conservative pathway and angiography within 24 hours
Activate the cath lab for emergency primary PCI as a STEMI equivalent
Treat as Wellens syndrome with urgent outpatient cardiology referral
Diagnose right bundle branch block and arrange echocardiogram in the morning
Diagnose digoxin effect and check digoxin level urgently

subtopic_detail: ACS - DAPT choice and duration post-PCI
stem: A 62-year-old woman undergoes successful primary PCI with a single drug-eluting stent to the proximal LAD for an anterior STEMI. She has hypertension on perindopril, dyslipidaemia, no diabetes, and no prior bleeding. Pre-procedure haemoglobin was 138 g/L and platelets 240 x 10^9/L. She was loaded with aspirin 300 mg and ticagrelor 180 mg in the ED. Post-procedurally she is haemodynamically stable, has no ongoing chest pain, and an echocardiogram shows an LVEF of 42 percent with anteroseptal hypokinesis. She has no history of stroke, intracranial haemorrhage, peptic ulcer disease, or anticoagulant use. The cardiology team plans discharge on day 3. She has no history of stroke, no bleeding ulcers, no prior peptic disease and asks specifically about ticagrelor versus clopidogrel versus prasugrel, the role of aspirin alone, and whether apixaban should be added given her stent. She has private cover and no PBS concerns about device or drug choice.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate ongoing antiplatelet regimen?

options.t: Aspirin 100 mg daily plus ticagrelor 90 mg twice daily for 12 months, then aspirin alone indefinitely
Aspirin 100 mg daily plus clopidogrel 75 mg daily for 12 months, then aspirin alone
Aspirin 100 mg daily alone, with clopidogrel added only if recurrent ischaemia occurs
Aspirin 100 mg daily plus prasugrel 10 mg daily for 6 months, then aspirin alone
Aspirin 100 mg daily plus apixaban 5 mg twice daily for 12 months

subtopic_detail: Acute decompensated heart failure - initial management priorities
stem: A 74-year-old man with known HFrEF (LVEF 32 percent) presents to the ED with 2 days of progressive dyspnoea, orthopnoea, and 3 kg weight gain. He admits to non-adherence with his frusemide for 1 week. He has a background of ischaemic cardiomyopathy, hypertension, and CKD stage 3 (baseline creatinine 140 micromol/L). On arrival he is sitting upright, working hard to breathe, and unable to complete sentences. He has bibasal crackles to the mid-zones and elevated JVP. He has no chest pain and his ECG shows old anterior Q-waves without acute ischaemic changes. His usual medications are frusemide 40 mg BD, perindopril 5 mg, bisoprolol 5 mg, spironolactone 25 mg, and dapagliflozin 10 mg. The team specifically asks whether IV fluid bolus and IV metoprolol for the tachycardia would help, whether IV morphine with high-flow nasal cannula at 60 L/min is appropriate, whether to withhold his ACE inhibitor and beta-blocker indefinitely while starting dobutamine, and whether oral diuretics would suffice.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial management?

options.t: IV frusemide 80 mg bolus, GTN infusion, and non-invasive ventilation with CPAP
IV fluid bolus 500 mL Hartmann's and IV metoprolol 5 mg for the tachycardia
IV morphine 5 mg with high-flow nasal cannula oxygen at 60 L/min and observation
Withhold his ACE inhibitor and beta-blocker indefinitely and start a dobutamine infusion
Oral frusemide 80 mg, oral metolazone 5 mg, and admit to a monitored bed

subtopic_detail: Chronic HFrEF - guideline-directed medical therapy escalation
stem: A 66-year-old woman with HFrEF (LVEF 28 percent, ischaemic aetiology) attends the heart failure clinic. She is NYHA class II to III with mild exertional dyspnoea climbing one flight. She has type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 7.2 percent), hypertension, and CKD stage 3a (eGFR 52, baseline). She has been on stable doses for 3 months of perindopril 8 mg daily, bisoprolol 7.5 mg daily, frusemide 40 mg daily, and atorvastatin 80 mg. Her BP is 118/72 mmHg, HR 64 bpm, potassium 4.4 mmol/L, sodium 138 mmol/L, creatinine 118 micromol/L. She has no symptomatic hypotension. She is keen to optimise prognosis-modifying therapy and has private cover for medication cost. She specifically asks whether to add digoxin for prognosis, whether to increase her frusemide and add amlodipine for blood pressure, whether to refer for cardiac resynchronisation now, or whether to switch to candesartan with ivabradine instead of the ARNI and MRA approach. She has no contraindication to MRA, ARNI or SGLT2 inhibitor and has tolerated her ACE inhibitor and beta-blocker without symptomatic hypotension over the past 3 months. She has private cover and PBS access to all four pillars.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step to optimise prognosis?

options.t: Add spironolactone 25 mg daily and dapagliflozin 10 mg daily, and plan switch of perindopril to sacubitril/valsartan after a 36-hour washout
Add digoxin 125 microgram daily for prognostic benefit and to address ongoing exertional dyspnoea despite optimised first-line guideline-directed medical therapy
Increase frusemide to 80 mg daily and add amlodipine 5 mg daily for tighter blood pressure control without adding any additional prognosis-modifying agent
Refer for cardiac resynchronisation therapy without further medical optimisation given the persistent NYHA class II to III symptoms despite current triple therapy regimen
Switch perindopril to candesartan 8 mg daily and add ivabradine 5 mg twice daily for additional symptomatic and prognostic benefit in HFrEF management

subtopic_detail: Atrial fibrillation - rate vs rhythm control strategy
stem: A 68-year-old woman is referred for symptomatic palpitations and exertional dyspnoea over the past 3 weeks. ECG confirms atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 118 bpm at rest. She has hypertension on perindopril, no diabetes, no prior stroke, no vascular disease, and no heart failure. Her LVEF on echo done last week is 58 percent with a mildly dilated left atrium (44 mm) and no significant valvular disease. She is otherwise active and reports the palpitations are interfering with her hiking. She has no contraindications to anticoagulation, antiarrhythmics, or cardioversion. Her current symptom burden is EHRA class IIb. Her TSH and electrolytes are normal. She specifically asks whether rate control alone is acceptable, whether early electrical cardioversion is preferred, whether anticoagulation alone is sufficient pending spontaneous reversion, whether digoxin monotherapy without anticoagulation would be appropriate given her age, or whether amiodarone loading without anticoagulation is reasonable in someone with such low overall stroke risk.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management strategy for her atrial fibrillation?

options.t: Rate control only with bisoprolol; defer rhythm control until two recurrences despite optimal rate control
Commence apixaban and a rate-controlling beta-blocker, then refer for early electrical cardioversion with consideration of catheter ablation
Anticoagulation alone with apixaban; AF often spontaneously reverts without further intervention
Digoxin monotherapy for rate control without anticoagulation as her CHA2DS2-VA score is 1
Amiodarone loading orally for 4 weeks followed by maintenance, with no anticoagulation given low stroke risk

stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with sudden palpitations for 50 minutes. She has had similar self-terminating episodes since her teens. She is haemodynamically stable. Her past history includes moderate persistent asthma managed with budesonide-formoterol; she had a hospital admission with status asthmaticus 3 years ago and reports wheeze with viral illnesses. She takes the combined oral contraceptive pill and drinks 2 to 3 coffees daily. On arrival she is anxious but well perfused. ECG shows a regular narrow-complex tachycardia at 184 bpm with no clearly visible P waves and a normal QRS axis. A modified Valsalva manoeuvre with leg elevation has been attempted twice without success.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: IV adenosine 6 mg rapid push followed by 20 mL saline flush with continuous ECG recording
IV verapamil 5 mg slowly over 2 minutes with continuous ECG and blood pressure monitoring
Synchronised DC cardioversion at 50 joules under procedural sedation with continuous monitoring
Oral metoprolol 25 mg with discharge home and cardiology outpatient follow-up if symptoms persist
IV amiodarone 300 mg loading dose over 60 minutes via a peripheral large-bore cannula

subtopic_detail: COPD - GOLD group classification and inhaler escalation
stem: A 68-year-old man with a 50 pack-year smoking history attends his GP for review. Spirometry 4 weeks ago confirmed COPD with post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC 0.58 and FEV1 62 percent predicted (GOLD 2). Over the past year he has had 2 exacerbations managed in the community with prednisolone and amoxicillin, and no hospital admissions. His mMRC dyspnoea score is 2 (stops for breath after about 100 m on the flat) and CAT score is 18. He is currently on as-needed salbutamol inhaler only. He stopped smoking 6 months ago, is up to date with influenza and pneumococcal vaccines, and is enrolled in a pulmonary rehabilitation program. He has no diagnosis of asthma, no significant atopy, and a blood eosinophil count of 120 cells/microL. His GP specifically asks whether to add a LAMA alone, to add a fixed-dose LAMA/LABA combination, to add a LABA/ICS combination, to commence triple therapy with LAMA/LABA/ICS, or to add oral theophylline.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next inhaled therapy?

options.t: Add tiotropium 18 microg daily (LAMA)
Add a LAMA/LABA combination inhaler
Add a fixed-dose LABA/ICS combination
Triple therapy with LAMA/LABA/ICS in a single inhaler
Add oral theophylline 200 mg twice daily

subtopic_detail: COPD exacerbation - acute management and ABG interpretation
stem: A 71-year-old woman with severe COPD (FEV1 38 percent predicted) is brought to ED by ambulance with worsening dyspnoea, productive cough with greenish sputum, and confusion over the past 24 hours. She is a current smoker. On arrival she was placed on 8 L/min Hudson mask oxygen by paramedics; SpO2 is now 98 percent. She is drowsy with bounding peripheral pulses, flapping tremor, and warm sweaty palms. She is using salbutamol/ipratropium nebulisers continuously since arrival. An arterial blood gas is taken on the current oxygen flow rate. Her usual inhalers are tiotropium and salmeterol/fluticasone, and she has had 3 prior admissions for COPD exacerbations in the past year, two requiring non-invasive ventilation. The ED registrar specifically asks whether to maintain current oxygen and proceed straight to intubation, to reduce oxygen to nasal prongs 2 L/min without ventilatory support, to administer IV doxapram to stimulate respiration, or to switch to nasal high-flow oxygen at 60 L/min and continue nebulisers as definitive management before any non-invasive ventilation trial is attempted. A BiPAP machine is available now in resus. ICU has been notified of likely admission.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Titrate oxygen down to a target SpO2 of 88 to 92 percent and initiate non-invasive ventilation with BiPAP
Maintain the current 8 L/min Hudson mask oxygen flow rate and proceed straight to endotracheal intubation and invasive mechanical ventilation
Reduce oxygen to nasal prongs at 2 L/min and observe in resus without ventilatory support pending repeat arterial blood gas in 1 hour
Administer intravenous doxapram infusion to stimulate respiratory drive and continue current Hudson mask oxygen flow rate while awaiting ICU review
Switch to nasal high-flow oxygen at 60 L/min, FiO2 0.6, and continue back-to-back salbutamol/ipratropium nebulisers without BiPAP

stem: A 24-year-old woman with known moderate persistent asthma presents to ED with 6 hours of progressively worsening dyspnoea, wheeze, and dry cough. She had a viral URTI 3 days ago. She has been using her salbutamol inhaler every 30 minutes for the last 2 hours with minimal relief. She has a background of eczema and rhinitis, no other medical history, and her usual inhaler is budesonide/formoterol 200/6 maintenance and reliever. Her best-ever PEF is 460 L/min. On arrival she can only speak in short phrases, is sitting upright, and is using accessory muscles. There is widespread polyphonic expiratory wheeze without focal crackles. There is no chest pain, no haemoptysis, and no calf swelling. The ED registrar asks specifically whether the exacerbation is mild-moderate, severe, or life-threatening, and which initial treatment bundle applies under the Australian Asthma Handbook for this patient today.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate classification of severity and corresponding initial treatment?

options.t: Mild to moderate exacerbation - salbutamol pMDI 4 to 12 puffs via spacer and reassess in 20 minutes for ongoing wheeze
Severe exacerbation - continuous salbutamol nebulisers with ipratropium, IV hydrocortisone, and oxygen titrated to SpO2 93 to 95 percent
Life-threatening exacerbation - immediate intubation and mechanical ventilation in the resus bay regardless of clinical response to bronchodilator therapy
Moderate exacerbation - oral prednisolone 50 mg and discharge home with safety-net advice and GP review in 24 hours for reassessment
Severe exacerbation - IV magnesium sulfate 2 g as first-line and observe response before commencing salbutamol nebulisers or systemic corticosteroid

stem: A 31-year-old woman is reviewed by her GP for asthma. Over the past 3 months she has had daytime symptoms 3 to 4 times per week, nocturnal waking once weekly, and uses her salbutamol inhaler roughly 4 times per week. She has had no exacerbations requiring oral steroids in the past year. Her inhaler technique is excellent on observation. She is currently on salbutamol 100 microgram pMDI as needed only. She is planning pregnancy in the next 6 months and asks specifically about whether she can keep using her reliever as her only inhaler in pregnancy. FEV1 is 88 percent predicted with bronchodilator reversibility of 14 percent. She has no rhinitis, reflux, or known occupational trigger.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.t: Continue salbutamol PRN alone with a written asthma action plan and review after conception
Commence low-dose budesonide-formoterol 200/6 used as anti-inflammatory reliever only with written action plan
Commence daily montelukast 10 mg nocte with continued salbutamol PRN as reliever
Commence high-dose fluticasone-salmeterol 500/50 twice daily with salbutamol PRN
Commence oral prednisolone 25 mg daily for 2 weeks then taper with continued salbutamol PRN

subtopic_detail: Pulmonary embolism - risk stratification and outpatient management
stem: A 52-year-old man is diagnosed with acute segmental pulmonary embolism on CTPA, performed for pleuritic chest pain and mild dyspnoea after a long-haul flight from London 5 days ago. He has no prior VTE, no malignancy, no chronic cardiopulmonary disease, and no recent surgery. On arrival he is haemodynamically stable, ambulating, eating, and drinking. His vital signs are stable as below. His troponin I and NT-proBNP are normal, and the CTPA reports no right heart strain. He has stable home circumstances, lives 10 minutes from the hospital, and has a partner at home. He is not pregnant. He has no significant bleeding history and is not on antiplatelets or NSAIDs. His haemoglobin is 142 g/L, platelets 230 x 10^9/L, and creatinine 78 micromol/L. The ED registrar specifically asks whether outpatient DOAC management is safe given sPESI 0 and no right heart strain, or whether inpatient warfarin bridging, systemic thrombolysis, IVC filter or aspirin monotherapy would be more appropriate.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management strategy?

options.t: Outpatient management with apixaban 10 mg twice daily for 7 days then 5 mg twice daily for 3 months
Inpatient management on a therapeutic enoxaparin bridge to warfarin titrated to INR 2.0 to 3.0 for at least 3 months of continued anticoagulation
Systemic thrombolysis with alteplase 100 mg over 2 hours followed by therapeutic heparin infusion and admission to a coronary care monitored bed
Inferior vena cava filter insertion without therapeutic anticoagulation given the recent long-haul flight as a transient provoking factor
Aspirin 100 mg daily indefinitely without therapeutic anticoagulation given the segmental clot burden and absence of right heart strain

subtopic_detail: Aspiration pneumonia - antibiotic choice and risk factors
stem: An 82-year-old nursing home resident with advanced Parkinson disease and known dysphagia is brought to ED with 24 hours of fever, productive cough, and confusion. The nursing home reports she was found coughing during dinner the previous evening. She was hospitalised 3 weeks ago for a urinary tract infection and received IV ceftriaxone and oral ciprofloxacin during that admission. She has no documented multi-drug-resistant organism. On examination she is febrile and tachypnoeic with right lower zone crackles. CXR shows right lower lobe consolidation. Bloods show neutrophilic leukocytosis and creatinine 96 micromol/L. She has no penicillin allergy.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate empirical antibiotic therapy?

options.t: IV amoxicillin-clavulanate 1.2 g eight-hourly empirically without antipseudomonal cover
IV piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g six-hourly empirically with daily microbiology review
Oral amoxicillin 1 g three times daily as monotherapy without anaerobic cover
IV ceftriaxone 1 g daily plus IV metronidazole 500 mg twelve-hourly empirically
IV vancomycin 1.5 g twelve-hourly plus IV ciprofloxacin 400 mg twelve-hourly empirically

subtopic_detail: Atrial fibrillation - cardioversion timing and anticoagulation
stem: A 58-year-old man presents to ED with palpitations and mild dyspnoea that began approximately 32 hours ago, but he reports a similar but milder episode 4 days ago that he ignored. He has hypertension on perindopril and no other medical history. He is haemodynamically stable. ECG confirms atrial fibrillation at 102 bpm. Bedside transthoracic echocardiogram shows preserved LV function (LVEF 60 percent), mild left atrial dilatation (LA 42 mm), and no significant valvular disease. He has no prior stroke, peripheral vascular disease, diabetes, or heart failure, and has never been anticoagulated. CHA2DS2-VA score is 1 (hypertension). The team would like to attempt rhythm control on this presentation.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate approach to cardioversion?

options.t: Proceed to electrical cardioversion now without anticoagulation since current episode is under 48 hours
Commence therapeutic anticoagulation and perform TOE-guided cardioversion, then continue anticoagulation for at least 4 weeks regardless of CHA2DS2-VA
Commence aspirin 100 mg daily and proceed to elective electrical cardioversion in 24 hours
Defer cardioversion indefinitely and use rate control with metoprolol without anticoagulation
Discharge home, commence flecainide pill-in-pocket strategy and review in clinic next week

stem: A 56-year-old man attends his GP for review of fatigue and nocturia over the last three months. He has hypertension on perindopril 5 mg, BMI 31, and his father has T2DM. He has had two haemoglobinopathy screens incidentally documented in the past (beta thalassaemia minor). He is not on glucocorticoids and has had no transfusions or recent illness. A fasting venous plasma glucose taken in a NATA-accredited laboratory two weeks ago was 7.3 mmol/L; today's laboratory HbA1c is 50 mmol/mol (6.7 percent); a random venous glucose today is 9.4 mmol/L. He has acanthosis nigricans and is otherwise well, with no polyuria, weight loss, or vomiting.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step to establish or refute the diagnosis of type 2 diabetes?

options.t: Diagnose type 2 diabetes now on the basis of HbA1c 50 mmol/mol plus fasting glucose 7.3 mmol/L and commence metformin
Repeat the fasting venous plasma glucose in a NATA-accredited laboratory on a separate day to confirm fasting glucose at least 7.0 mmol/L
Discharge without diagnosis because the random glucose of 9.4 mmol/L is below 11.1 mmol/L
Order fructosamine as the primary diagnostic test since HbA1c is unreliable in haemoglobinopathy
Order an oral glucose tolerance test with 2-hour glucose at 75 g load, but commence metformin today regardless of result

stem: A 64-year-old man with type 2 diabetes for 9 years attends his GP for diabetes review. He is on metformin XR 2 g daily and has been adherent for 18 months. He has heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (LVEF 35 percent) on echocardiography after an anterior STEMI two years ago, treated with stented LAD, and is on perindopril 8 mg, bisoprolol 5 mg, atorvastatin 80 mg and aspirin 100 mg. He has chronic kidney disease with eGFR 48 mL/min/1.73m^2 and ACR 6.4 mg/mmol on two occasions. He has no symptoms of urinary tract infection, no recurrent thrush and no history of diabetic foot ulceration. BMI is 29 kg/m^2 and he is normotensive. His most recent HbA1c is 62 mmol/mol (7.8 percent), up from 56 mmol/mol six months ago. He denies hypoglycaemic episodes and is keen to optimise his cardiovascular and renal protection in addition to glycaemic control. He has no history of pancreatitis and no personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or MEN2. He has never had a diabetic foot ulcer, retinal laser or amputation. He is on no NSAID, no potassium-sparing diuretic and has no documented contrast allergy. He fully understands sick-day rules and is independently mobile, performs his own injections if needed and has reliable transport for monitoring bloods.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in pharmacological management?

options.t: Add a sulfonylurea such as gliclazide MR 30 mg daily
Add an SGLT2 inhibitor such as empagliflozin 10 mg daily
Add basal insulin glargine starting 10 units nocte
Add a DPP4 inhibitor such as sitagliptin 100 mg daily
Add pioglitazone 15 mg daily for insulin sensitisation

stem: A 71-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 14 years attends her GP. She is on metformin XR 2 g, empagliflozin 25 mg and dulaglutide 1.5 mg weekly. She has tolerated triple therapy for 18 months without hypoglycaemia, weight loss has plateaued and her HbA1c has risen from 58 to 76 mmol/mol (9.1 percent) over the last six months. She has no acute illness, drinks no alcohol and lives independently with her husband who can assist with injections. She is willing to start insulin but anxious about night-time hypoglycaemia. Her eGFR is 62 mL/min/1.73m^2, weight 78 kg, and she has retinopathy on annual review. Fasting capillary glucose self-monitoring shows readings of 11 to 14 mmol/L; pre-dinner readings are 8 to 10 mmol/L. There is no significant post-prandial glucose excursion documented. She drives a private car only and her vision is corrected to 6/9. She is up to date with her sick-day plan for the SGLT2 inhibitor.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate insulin regimen to commence?

options.t: Premixed biphasic insulin (e.g. NovoMix 30) twice daily before breakfast and dinner
Basal-bolus regimen with insulin glargine nocte and rapid-acting insulin with each meal
Long-acting basal insulin glargine 10 units nocte titrated by 2 units every 3 days
Twice-daily NPH insulin in equal split doses administered each morning and again each night
Prandial-only rapid-acting insulin aspart given with only the largest carbohydrate meal of the day

stem: A 22-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents to ED with 18 hours of vomiting, polyuria and abdominal pain after running out of insulin glargine three days ago. She is alert and oriented but exhausted. Her usual basal-bolus regimen is glargine 18 units nocte and aspart with meals. She has no chest pain and no focal sepsis. On arrival she is tachycardic, hypotensive and breathing deeply. Capillary glucose is high (off the meter), capillary ketones are 5.8 mmol/L and a venous blood gas shows pH 7.10, bicarbonate 7 mmol/L, anion gap 28. Serum potassium on the gas is 5.4 mmol/L. Sodium is 132 mmol/L, creatinine 118 micromol/L, eGFR 52 mL/min/1.73m^2. She has voided 200 mL since arrival 30 minutes ago. ECG shows sinus tachycardia without peaked T waves. Urinalysis confirms ketonuria and glycosuria; there is no leucocyte esterase or nitrites and her CXR is clear.
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management within the first 15 minutes?

options.t: IV 0.9 percent sodium chloride 500 mL bolus over 15 to 30 minutes then reassess
Commence fixed-rate IV insulin infusion at 0.1 units/kg/hour without any IV fluid
Administer IV sodium bicarbonate 50 mmol as an immediate stat bolus dose
Give IV potassium chloride 40 mmol prior to any insulin or other therapy
Subcutaneous long-acting insulin glargine 18 units administered immediately without IV fluid

stem: A 78-year-old man with type 2 diabetes is brought from his nursing home with one week of progressive confusion and reduced oral intake. He has hypertension, chronic kidney disease and a recent UTI treated with trimethoprim. His regular medications include metformin 500 mg BD, gliclazide MR 60 mg and lisinopril 10 mg. Staff report he has been refusing to drink for the last two days. On arrival he is drowsy, severely dehydrated and has a temperature of 38.4 degrees. Capillary glucose is 48 mmol/L. Venous gas shows pH 7.34, bicarbonate 20 mmol/L, anion gap 14, lactate 1.8. Capillary ketones are 0.6 mmol/L. Sodium 152 mmol/L, potassium 4.1, urea 24, creatinine 198 micromol/L (baseline 110), corrected sodium for glucose 158 mmol/L. Calculated effective serum osmolality is 348 mosm/kg. Urinalysis confirms leucocytes and nitrites; he is on no SGLT2 inhibitor. There are no Kussmaul respirations and no ketotic breath.
lead_in: Which feature best distinguishes this presentation as hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state (HHS) rather than DKA?

options.t: The blood glucose level of 48 mmol/L on bedside capillary testing today
Effective osmolality above 320, capillary ketones under 3 and pH above 7.30 without acidosis
The acute kidney injury with creatinine rising from baseline 110 to 198 micromol/L
The presence of a urinary tract infection acting as the precipitating event here
The reduced Glasgow Coma Scale score on arrival to the emergency department

stem: A 74-year-old woman with T2DM on metformin 1 g BD, gliclazide MR 120 mg daily and insulin glargine 22 units nocte is brought to ED by paramedics after her daughter found her diaphoretic and confused. Capillary glucose was 1.8 mmol/L pre-hospital. She received IM glucagon 1 mg followed by oral juice with rapid clinical improvement; her glucose now is 6.4 mmol/L and she is fully oriented. Over the last week she has reported anorexia, fatigue and unintentional weight loss of 2 kg. She started cefalexin five days ago for a urinary tract infection and the same day her GP added trimethoprim 300 mg daily after sensitivities returned. She has CKD stage 3b (eGFR 38 mL/min/1.73m^2 baseline). She lives alone and reports occasional missed meals. Her last HbA1c three months ago was 49 mmol/mol (6.6 percent). She has no chest pain. ECG shows sinus rhythm rate 78 with no acute ischaemic changes.
lead_in: Which factor is most likely to have precipitated this hypoglycaemic episode?

options.t: The basal insulin glargine dose, which should be reduced as the default first action
Trimethoprim potentiating gliclazide via reduced clearance in CKD plus the reduced oral intake
Direct metformin-induced hypoglycaemia from her usual twice-daily 1 g metformin dose
Cefalexin-induced hypoglycaemia from the recently started urinary tract infection antibiotic
Inadequate paramedic glucagon dose given prior to her arrival in the emergency department

stem: A 38-year-old woman is brought to ED with two days of worsening agitation, vomiting and fever after stopping carbimazole 10 days ago because she ran out of her script. She was diagnosed with Graves disease six months ago. On arrival she is agitated, sweaty and tremulous. She is in atrial fibrillation at 152 bpm with a blood pressure of 96/52 mmHg. Temperature is 39.6 degrees and she has had one episode of vomiting in ED. She has bilateral exophthalmos and a smooth diffuse goitre. Her last TSH four weeks ago was <0.01 mIU/L with free T4 48 pmol/L. She is alert but confused (GCS 14) and not following commands consistently. There is no chest pain. Bedside echo at the bedside shows preserved LV function. Capillary glucose is 7.2 mmol/L. She has no recent travel, no respiratory symptoms and clear chest on auscultation. Beta-hCG is negative. CRP is 18 mg/L and white cell count is 11.2 x10^9/L without left shift. She has no recent travel, no known infectious contacts and her urinalysis is negative. There is no rash and no neck stiffness. The endocrinology registrar has been called. She has no documented allergy to iodine, propranolol, propylthiouracil, carbimazole or hydrocortisone.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial medical management?

options.t: IV propranolol, oral propylthiouracil, Lugol's iodine after PTU, hydrocortisone, paracetamol and active cooling
IV amiodarone 300 mg for prompt rate control of her new atrial fibrillation
Empirical IV ceftriaxone 2 g and acyclovir for presumed sepsis or encephalitis
IV levothyroxine 200 microgram and IV hydrocortisone 100 mg for presumed myxoedema coma
Oral carbimazole 60 mg single dose only and immediate discharge home with outpatient endocrinology follow-up

stem: A 32-year-old woman attends her GP for preconception counselling. She is planning pregnancy in the next 3 months. Blood tests ordered 8 weeks ago showed TSH 5.6 mIU/L with normal free T4. A repeat panel today shows TSH 5.4 mIU/L, free T4 13 pmol/L (normal), and TPO antibodies markedly elevated at 540 IU/mL. She has no goitre, no compressive symptoms, and no other thyroid history. She has hypercholesterolaemia on atorvastatin and is otherwise well. She has no constipation or weight change.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Commence levothyroxine to target a pre-conception TSH below 2.5 mIU/L and review TFTs in 6 to 8 weeks
Repeat TFTs and TPO antibodies in 6 to 12 months with no treatment now and advise contraception until reassessed
Stop atorvastatin and recheck thyroid function in 6 weeks since statin may interfere with the TSH assay
Refer for ultrasound-guided fine needle aspiration of the thyroid for cytology
Commence T3 (liothyronine) 20 microgram daily as primary thyroid replacement

stem: A 52-year-old woman is reviewed on the surgical ward 14 hours after total thyroidectomy for a multinodular goitre with compressive symptoms. She reports peri-oral and finger tingling for the last hour and one episode of carpal spasm when the cuff was inflated. She is otherwise well, with no airway compromise and no haematoma at the wound. She is on regular paracetamol and prophylactic enoxaparin. Bloods drawn at the onset of symptoms show: corrected calcium 1.78 mmol/L (corrected for albumin 38 g/L), ionised calcium 0.92 mmol/L, magnesium 0.74 mmol/L, phosphate 1.6 mmol/L, parathyroid hormone 0.4 pmol/L (low), 25-OH vitamin D 64 nmol/L, potassium 4.1, creatinine 76. Her ECG shows a prolonged QTc of 510 ms. She has no history of renal stones, no malignancy and her pre-operative calcium was 2.34 mmol/L. She is alert with GCS 15. There is no respiratory distress.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.t: Oral calcium carbonate 1.5 g elemental calcium with calcitriol 0.5 microgram and clinical review in 4 hours
IV 10 percent calcium gluconate 10 mL bolus followed by infusion, replace magnesium, then oral calcium plus calcitriol
IV 10 percent calcium chloride 10 mL given via a peripheral cannula stat dose
IV magnesium sulfate 10 mmol over 20 minutes given as the only acute therapy
IV cinacalcet 30 mg given as a single calcimimetic dose for acute symptom control

stem: A 68-year-old man with diabetic nephropathy (eGFR 22 mL/min/1.73m^2) attends ED after his GP found hyperkalaemia on routine bloods. He takes perindopril 8 mg, spironolactone 25 mg (started two weeks ago for resistant hypertension) and metformin which was stopped on his GP's advice. He feels generally fatigued and has had mild palpitations but no syncope. He is haemodynamically stable. ED bloods confirm potassium 7.2 mmol/L (venous, repeat 7.0), creatinine 268 micromol/L (baseline 220), bicarbonate 18 mmol/L, glucose 8.4 mmol/L, calcium 2.31 mmol/L. ECG shows peaked T waves in V2 to V5, PR interval 220 ms and QRS 110 ms with normal axis; there are no ischaemic changes. He is on no other K-sparing medication and has not used potassium-containing salt substitutes. Urine output is preserved. There is no evidence of haemolysis on the sample. He has no salt substitute use at home, no recent NSAID use and no missed dialysis sessions. He has no pre-existing pacemaker.
lead_in: Which is the single most important immediate intervention?

options.t: IV 10 percent calcium gluconate 10 mL over 2 to 3 minutes to stabilise the myocardium
IV insulin actrapid 10 units with 50 mL of 50 percent dextrose to shift potassium
Nebulised salbutamol 10 to 20 mg via continuous oxygen-driven nebuliser at the bedside
Oral resonium A (sodium polystyrene sulfonate) 30 g as the immediate priority intervention
Urgent haemodialysis arranged after a CT scan to exclude obstructive uropathy first

stem: A 72-year-old woman attends her GP three hours after an episode of left arm and face weakness lasting 35 minutes, now completely resolved. She has hypertension on amlodipine, T2DM on metformin, current smoker (20 pack-years), and no prior atrial fibrillation. Examination is now normal. BP today 168/96, blood glucose 9.2, ECG sinus rhythm, no carotid bruit. She has no anticoagulation, no bleeding tendency, and no recent head injury.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate combination of management steps?

options.t: Same-day ED transfer for non-contrast CT brain and CT angiography of head and neck, load with aspirin 300 mg now, and arrange admission to a stroke unit with cardiac monitoring
Calculate ABCD2 and refer urgently only if 4 or more; otherwise manage in primary care with aspirin and review in 1 week
Reassure and arrange outpatient specialist clinic review in 2 weeks with aspirin 100 mg daily today
Commence rivaroxaban 20 mg daily empirically for presumed paroxysmal AF and arrange outpatient Holter monitor
Arrange a carotid Doppler within the next 4 weeks and review her again after the result is back

stem: A 34-year-old man is brought to ED by his partner after a witnessed generalised tonic-clonic seizure lasting 90 seconds while watching television.
