He has no prior seizures, no head injury, no drug or alcohol use and takes no medications. He was confused for about 20 minutes after the event and bit the side of his tongue. On arrival he is back to his baseline. Capillary glucose, electrolytes, calcium, magnesium and a urine drug screen are normal. ECG is normal sinus rhythm. He works as a self-employed delivery driver using a standard private licence (no commercial endorsement). His non-contrast CT brain is normal. He wants to know when he can drive and what investigations are needed. He has no fever, no headache, no neck stiffness and no focal neurology. He has no family history of epilepsy and no recent travel. He is otherwise well and has not started any new medications.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate combination of next investigation and driving advice?

options.t: MRI brain and EEG as outpatient; no driving for at least 6 months (Austroads private standard)
Commence sodium valproate immediately and clear him to return to driving in 1 month
Lumbar puncture today and start IV ceftriaxone for presumed bacterial meningitis empirically
Continuous video-EEG admission for 72 hours starting today as the immediate next investigation
Cleared to return to commercial driving as soon as he feels well, neurology in 6 weeks

stem: A 56-year-old man with known epilepsy on sodium valproate is brought to ED in convulsive status epilepticus. The seizure started 25 minutes ago in the ambulance. Paramedics gave 10 mg IM midazolam at 12 minutes and another 10 mg IV at 20 minutes without resolution; he continues to have generalised tonic-clonic activity. On arrival his airway is maintained with nasopharyngeal airway and SpO2 96 percent on 15 L non-rebreather. BP is 156/92, HR 124, temperature 37.1. Capillary glucose is 7.4 mmol/L. He has IV access and is now 27 minutes into the seizure. He has no recent head injury and his valproate level is pending. There is no fever, no rash, and no obvious focal sepsis. His airway position is maintained and there is no audible stridor. His pupils are equal and reactive. Bedside ECG shows sinus tachycardia without ischaemic changes.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.t: Repeat a further IV midazolam 10 mg bolus dose as third benzodiazepine attempt
IV levetiracetam 60 mg/kg (maximum 4.5 g) given over 10 minutes as second-line agent
IV propofol 2 mg/kg induction and intubate without any further antiepileptic drug given
Oral phenytoin 300 mg administered via nasogastric tube as the next antiepileptic agent
IV thiamine 200 mg and 50 mL of 50 percent dextrose given as the next intervention

stem: A 74-year-old woman presents to her GP with a three-week history of new bilateral temporal headache progressively worsening, with scalp tenderness on hair brushing and jaw pain with chewing tough food. Yesterday she had a 10-minute episode of curtain-like vision loss in her right eye that has now resolved. Today on examination her right eye visual acuity is 6/9 (was 6/6 four months ago at optometry) with a relative afferent pupillary defect; left eye 6/6. Temporal arteries tender and pulsatile bilaterally. Bloods: ESR 92 mm/hr, CRP 84 mg/L, Hb 108 g/L, platelets 484. She has hypertension on perindopril and T2DM on metformin (HbA1c 58 mmol/mol). Bone mineral density was T-score -1.4 at the lumbar spine 6 months ago.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.t: Admit for IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3 days followed by oral prednisolone 60 mg daily, with same-day ophthalmology and rheumatology review and arrangement of temporal artery biopsy within 1 to 2 weeks
Commence oral prednisolone 60 mg daily today and arrange temporal artery biopsy within 1 to 2 weeks
Order CT brain today and start prednisolone only if temporal artery biopsy is positive within 2 weeks
Commence tocilizumab subcutaneous 162 mg weekly as first-line monotherapy
Treat empirically as migraine with sumatriptan 50 mg and arrange neurology follow-up in 2 weeks

stem: A 48-year-old previously well woman presents to ED 14 hours after the abrupt onset of the worst headache of her life while gardening. The headache reached maximum intensity within seconds and was associated with one episode of vomiting. She now has no neurological deficits, GCS 15, no neck stiffness, no rash, no photophobia. BP 162/94. She is not anticoagulated and has no recent trauma. A non-contrast CT brain is performed and reported by an experienced radiologist as normal. She has had similar but less severe headaches with exertion previously, never investigated.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Perform lumbar puncture for CSF xanthochromia and red cell count
Discharge with simple analgesia since the CT brain is normal and arrange neurology follow-up in 1 week
Proceed directly to CT angiography of intracranial vessels for unruptured aneurysm screening
Empirical IV ceftriaxone 2 g and IV dexamethasone 10 mg for presumed bacterial meningitis
Order MRI brain with gadolinium and FLAIR sequences as the definitive next investigation

stem: A 28-year-old woman is brought to ED 25 minutes after eating a peanut-containing dessert. She has a known peanut allergy. On arrival: stridor and dyspnoea, widespread urticaria and lip swelling, BP 78/42, HR 132, SpO2 91 percent on 15 L non-rebreather, RR 28, alert but distressed. You give IM adrenaline 0.5 mg into the anterolateral mid-thigh and lie her supine with legs elevated. Five minutes later, BP is 82/46, HR 130, RR 28, SpO2 92 percent, with persistent stridor. A second IM adrenaline 0.5 mg is given. Five minutes after that, BP is 84/48, HR 128, SpO2 92 percent, stridor persists. Two large-bore IVs are in. She has no past cardiac history and takes no regular medications.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Commence IV adrenaline infusion at 5 microgram per minute titrated to clinical response, give 20 mL/kg IV crystalloid bolus, and prepare for advanced airway management
Continue IM adrenaline 0.5 mg every 5 minutes indefinitely and avoid intravenous adrenaline
Give IV hydrocortisone 200 mg and IV promethazine 25 mg and observe for further response
Give IV glucagon 1 to 5 mg bolus as the next escalation step
Nebulised salbutamol 5 mg and nebulised adrenaline 5 mg via oxygen-driven nebuliser, then reassess

subtopic_detail: Postmenopausal IDA mandates upper and lower GI endoscopy
stem: A 64-year-old postmenopausal woman attends her GP with three months of progressive fatigue and exertional dyspnoea climbing two flights of stairs. She has no overt PR bleeding, no melaena and no haematemesis but notes mild epigastric discomfort relieved by food. She takes ibuprofen 400 mg PRN for osteoarthritis (about three times per week) and is otherwise well. She has never had a colonoscopy. Her father died of colorectal cancer at 67. She does not drink alcohol and has never smoked. Her diet includes red meat twice weekly. There is no overt haemorrhage source on history. Examination shows conjunctival pallor without jaundice or lymphadenopathy. Abdomen is soft with mild epigastric tenderness, no masses, PR exam reveals no haemorrhoids and the stool sample sent for FOBT is positive. Coeliac serology is pending. She has not had bariatric surgery and is not vegetarian. She wants to know what investigation is most appropriate now.
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next investigation?

options.t: Capsule endoscopy of the small bowel
Oral ferrous sulfate trial and reassess Hb in three months
CT colonography as first-line in place of colonoscopy
Repeat FBC and ferritin in 6 weeks before any endoscopic referral
Bidirectional upper and lower GI endoscopy at the same session

subtopic_detail: Subacute combined degeneration - parenteral B12 indicated
stem: A 72-year-old woman presents with six months of progressive fatigue and three months of paraesthesiae in both feet that have ascended to mid-calf. She reports unsteadiness in the dark and a recent fall when reaching for an upper shelf. She has type 1 diabetes for 20 years on basal-bolus insulin, autoimmune hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 75 micrograms and takes metformin 1 g BD for adjunctive insulin resistance. She has been on long-term proton pump inhibitor for reflux. She does not drink alcohol and is not vegan. She has had no bariatric surgery. On examination she has a beefy red tongue, brisk knee jerks but absent ankle jerks, impaired joint position and vibration sense to the mid-shins and a positive Romberg. There is no anaemic murmur. The GP requests urgent bloods. The MMSE is 28/30 with reduced recall. She is independently mobile with a four-wheel walker she has been using for the last month.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.t: Oral cyanocobalamin 1000 micrograms daily indefinitely and review symptoms in clinic in 4 weeks
Hydroxocobalamin 1 mg IM on alternate days until improvement plateaus, then 1 mg every 2 months
Start oral folic acid 5 mg daily while awaiting the Schilling test or holotranscobalamin level
Cease the metformin and recheck the B12 level at routine follow-up in 3 months
Transfuse 2 units of packed red cells today given the new neurological symptoms and anaemia

stem: A 58-year-old woman with well-controlled SLE on hydroxychloroquine presents with 10 days of fatigue, exertional dyspnoea and dark urine, with mild scleral icterus for 3 days. She has no fever, weight loss or recent infections, has not started any new medications, and has never been transfused. Examination: pallor, scleral icterus, 2 cm spleen tip, no lymphadenopathy, no acrocyanosis. Bloods: Hb 62 g/L, MCV 102, reticulocytes 11 percent, LDH 920 U/L, haptoglobin under 0.1, unconjugated bilirubin 64 micromol/L, DAT IgG strongly positive and C3d weakly positive, blood film spherocytes and polychromasia, cold agglutinin titre negative. She is haemodynamically stable but has new exertional angina on transferring to the bed. ECG shows no ischaemic changes; troponin is mildly elevated (28 ng/L, normal under 14).
lead_in: Which combination of treatments is most appropriate as first-line management?

options.t: Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily, transfuse the least incompatible packed red cells with close clinical and laboratory monitoring, and add folate replacement
Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily and strictly avoid blood transfusion regardless of severity
Urgent splenectomy and IV immunoglobulin 1 g/kg as first-line therapy
Plasma exchange with fresh frozen plasma replacement and rituximab 375 mg/m^2 weekly
Continue hydroxychloroquine only and recheck FBC in 1 week before considering steroid initiation

subtopic_detail: Indications for red cell exchange transfusion
stem: A 28-year-old man with homozygous HbSS sickle cell disease presents with three days of bilateral pleuritic chest pain, fever and dyspnoea. He was admitted six days ago for a typical vaso-occlusive crisis of the lower limbs and has been on regular morphine PCA, IV fluids and incentive spirometry. He has had four prior admissions for VOC this year and one previous episode of acute chest syndrome two years ago. He is fully vaccinated including pneumococcal and is on penicillin V prophylaxis. He has no known allergies. He is normally on hydroxyurea 20 mg/kg daily but adherence has been poor. He works as a delivery driver. On examination he is in respiratory distress, with new bilateral basal crepitations and oxygen saturation of 88% on room air, improving to 93% on 6 L via Hudson mask. There is no calf swelling. CXR shows new bilateral lower lobe infiltrates. Hb has dropped from baseline 88 to 64 g/L. He has a leucocytosis with WCC 22 and CRP 180. He is started on IV ceftriaxone and azithromycin.
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.t: Simple top-up transfusion of 1 unit packed red cells
Empirical therapeutic dose enoxaparin for presumed pulmonary embolism
Urgent red cell exchange transfusion targeting HbS less than 30%
Continue current oxygen, fluids and antibiotics with hourly observations only
Increase hydroxyurea dose to drive HbF production

stem: A 74-year-old woman attends ED with one day of right-sided pleuritic chest pain and mild exertional dyspnoea. She had a knee arthroplasty 8 weeks ago. She has hypertension on perindopril, osteoarthritis, no haemoptysis, no calf swelling, no malignancy and is otherwise active. Examination: comfortable at rest, clear chest, no calf signs, SpO2 96 percent on room air. ECG sinus tachycardia 102. CXR normal. Bloods: troponin negative, FBC normal, eGFR 68. Wells score is 1.5 (recent surgery 1.5; PE most likely diagnosis criterion not counted). Age-adjusted D-dimer returns at 680 microgram/L FEU.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Exclude PE based on Wells unlikely plus D-dimer 680 microgram/L below the age-adjusted cut-off of 740, and discharge with safety netting
Proceed to CTPA because the D-dimer is above the standard fixed cut-off of 500 microgram/L
Start therapeutic anticoagulation empirically and image with CTPA within 24 hours
Perform V/Q scan as the first-line confirmatory test because CXR is normal
Repeat D-dimer in 24 hours before deciding on imaging

stem: A 67-year-old man with newly diagnosed metastatic colorectal cancer (liver metastases) presents with a confirmed unprovoked left iliofemoral DVT and segmental subsegmental PE on CTPA. He started FOLFOX chemotherapy 10 days ago. He is haemodynamically stable, normotensive and not hypoxic. He has chronic kidney disease with eGFR 38 mL/min/1.73m^2, no liver dysfunction (bilirubin 16, ALT 28), and no prior GI bleeding. He has a colostomy after primary resection. There is no upper GI lesion documented on endoscopy 3 months ago. He has no thrombocytopenia (platelets 188) and no anaemia. He is not on antiplatelets. He has no history of intracranial haemorrhage. He weighs 78 kg. He is on ondansetron for nausea, dexamethasone PRN with chemotherapy, paracetamol for analgesia and atorvastatin 40 mg. He is keen to take an oral agent and avoid daily injections if possible. He understands that anticoagulation must continue for at least the duration of active malignancy. He has no platelet transfusion history and no autoimmune disease.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate anticoagulation choice?

options.t: Rivaroxaban 15 mg BD for 21 days then 20 mg daily
Dabigatran 150 mg BD after 5 days of LMWH lead-in
Warfarin bridged with LMWH until INR 2-3 stable
Apixaban 10 mg BD for 7 days then 5 mg BD
Aspirin 100 mg daily as secondary prevention

stem: A 72-year-old man is on day 8 post coronary artery bypass grafting. He has been on prophylactic enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily since day 1 plus an intraoperative and 48-hour post-op unfractionated heparin infusion. He develops a new right calf DVT confirmed on ultrasound. Platelets have fallen from 280 to 78 (a 72 percent drop). No bleeding, no skin necrosis, no schistocytes on film. PF4-heparin ELISA is strongly positive and a serotonin release assay is pending. 4Ts score is 7. He has CKD with eGFR 38 (creatinine 168 micromol/L) and severe hepatic dysfunction from chronic right heart failure (bilirubin 62, INR 1.6).
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate anticoagulation strategy?

options.t: Stop all heparin (UFH and LMWH) and commence IV bivalirudin at adjusted dose given combined renal and hepatic impairment, with close aPTT monitoring
Stop heparin and commence fondaparinux 7.5 mg SC daily off-label at standard dose
Stop heparin and commence IV argatroban at standard dose
Stop heparin and immediately commence warfarin bridging to therapeutic INR 2.0 to 3.0
Stop all heparin and observe expectantly without alternative anticoagulation until platelets recover

stem: A 32-year-old woman presents with one week of mucocutaneous bruising, gum bleeding, two short epistaxis episodes, and today new heavy menstrual bleeding requiring an overnight pad change every 90 minutes. She takes no medications, has not been recently vaccinated, has no recent viral illness, no pregnancy. No lymphadenopathy or hepatosplenomegaly. Bloods: isolated platelets 4 x10^9/L with normal Hb, WCC, differential, coagulation profile, renal and liver function. Blood film: large platelets, no schistocytes, no blasts. HIV, hepatitis B and C, H. pylori, ANA all negative. She has no contraindication to corticosteroids or IVIG. The team plans urgent treatment.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment?

options.t: IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3 days plus IVIG 1 g/kg as combination first-line therapy
Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily as monotherapy and review platelet count in 1 week
Platelet transfusion of 2 adult therapeutic doses to correct count above 50 x10^9/L
Rituximab 375 mg/m^2 weekly for 4 weeks as first-line therapy
Bone marrow biopsy first to confirm the diagnosis before commencing any treatment

subtopic_detail: Urgent FBC + blood film + bone marrow
stem: A 46-year-old woman presents to her GP with three weeks of progressive fatigue, easy bruising of the limbs, gum bleeding and a low-grade fever. She had two episodes of unexplained epistaxis lasting 15 minutes each. She has no past medical history of note and takes no medications. She has lost 3 kg unintentionally in the last month and has drenching night sweats. She has no foreign travel and no contact with TB. On examination she is pale, with widespread bruising and petechiae, gum hypertrophy and bleeding, a 4 cm palpable spleen and shotty cervical lymphadenopathy. Bloods at her GP show pancytopenia: Hb 76 g/L, WCC 38 x10^9/L (with 70% blasts on differential), platelets 14 x10^9/L. Blood film reports myeloid blasts with Auer rods. Coagulation profile shows fibrinogen 1.0 g/L (low) and a raised D-dimer. She has not received transfusion. She is haemodynamically stable but the GP is concerned about possible acute promyelocytic leukaemia (APML). She has no current bleeding source other than gums.
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.t: Discharge home with routine outpatient haematology referral in 2 weeks for further workup
Start empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics and observe in ED for 24 hours before specialist referral
Transfuse 2 units of packed red cells and 1 pool of platelets then reassess clinically
Same-day transfer to haematology for urgent bone marrow biopsy with empirical ATRA if APML suspected
Repeat the full blood count in 48 hours to confirm the pancytopenia before any specialist referral

stem: A 48-year-old man is referred from his GP after a routine FBC for life insurance showed WCC 86 x10^9/L with a full myeloid maturation spectrum (myelocytes, metamyelocytes, basophilia 5 percent, eosinophilia 4 percent, blasts under 2 percent), Hb 124, platelets 480. He has mild left upper quadrant discomfort, no fevers, weight loss, or B symptoms. He has 6 cm splenomegaly. He has a 12-year history of bipolar disorder on lithium 800 mg daily and has a recurrent left-sided pleural effusion from chronic congestive heart failure (LVEF 40 percent). Cytogenetics confirm t(9;22) and BCR-ABL1 transcript by PCR. Bone marrow shows 1 percent blasts (chronic phase). He has no other comorbidities.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line BCR-ABL1 tyrosine kinase inhibitor?

options.t: Imatinib 400 mg orally daily
Dasatinib 100 mg orally daily
Nilotinib 300 mg orally twice daily
Ponatinib 45 mg orally daily as first-line therapy
Hydroxyurea 1 to 2 g daily as definitive cytoreductive therapy

stem: A 68-year-old man with stage A (Binet) CLL diagnosed 18 months ago has been on watch-and-wait. His lymphocyte count has been stable around 18-22 x10^9/L. He attends with three weeks of unilateral right cervical lymph node enlargement that is now 5 cm, firm and rapidly growing. He has lost 6 kg in the last month, has drenching night sweats and a temperature of 38.4 deg C this morning. His Hb is 102 g/L (was 132), LDH is 920 U/L (was 220) and uric acid is 0.48 mmol/L. He has no infection elsewhere. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis shows bulky cervical, mediastinal and abdominal lymphadenopathy with a dominant 5 x 4 cm right cervical node. PET-CT shows the dominant node has SUVmax 18 with significantly higher avidity than other nodes. He has not had previous chemotherapy. He has not received rituximab. His original CLL FISH showed del(13q) only. He has no concurrent infection on cultures.
lead_in: Which finding most strongly suggests Richter transformation requiring biopsy?

options.t: Lymphocyte count rising from 18 to 22 x10^9/L over 18 months
Smudge cells on blood film
PET avidity SUVmax >10 in a dominant node alongside rising LDH and B symptoms
Hb 102 g/L alone
Original FISH showing del(13q)

stem: A 24-year-old previously well woman has been diagnosed with classical Hodgkin lymphoma after excisional left supraclavicular lymph node biopsy. Immunohistochemistry: CD30 positive, CD15 positive, CD20 negative Reed-Sternberg cells in a mixed inflammatory infiltrate. She reports drenching night sweats, fevers above 38, and 8 kg weight loss over 6 weeks. PET-CT shows supradiaphragmatic-only nodal involvement (left supraclavicular, mediastinal, bilateral cervical). Bloods: Hb 116, WCC normal, ESR 68, LDH normal, HIV negative. Echocardiogram shows LVEF 60 percent. Pulmonary function tests are normal. She has not commenced any treatment yet. She has no children but wishes to preserve fertility.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step before commencing chemotherapy?

options.t: Refer urgently for fertility preservation counselling and oocyte or embryo cryopreservation, then commence ABVD chemotherapy
Commence ABVD chemotherapy immediately and defer fertility discussion until after first cycle
Commence BEACOPP chemotherapy as first-line for B symptoms instead of ABVD
Proceed directly to involved-site radiotherapy as monotherapy without chemotherapy
Repeat excisional lymph node biopsy to confirm the immunohistochemistry findings before treatment

stem: A 70-year-old man is being evaluated for symptomatic multiple myeloma. He has back pain (mid-thoracic, worse at night), 4 kg unintentional weight loss, two recent pneumonias, T6 spinal tenderness without neurological deficit. Bloods: Hb 92 g/L, creatinine 198 micromol/L (eGFR 28; baseline 78), corrected calcium 2.78 mmol/L (RR 2.10 to 2.60), albumin 28, IgG kappa paraprotein 42 g/L, kappa free light chain 320 mg/L (ratio 28), urinary Bence Jones protein positive. CT shows multiple lytic lesions and a T6 compression fracture. Bone marrow biopsy: 45 percent clonal plasma cells.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate combination of next steps in management?

options.t: Urgent MRI whole spine for cord compression risk, IV hydration with isotonic crystalloid, IV zoledronic acid (dose-adjusted for renal function), and refer to haematology to commence bortezomib-based induction
Refer for bone marrow biopsy and skeletal survey before commencing any treatment
Commence high-dose dexamethasone 40 mg daily and discharge on outpatient follow-up
Commence high-dose lenalidomide-based induction immediately without renal dose adjustment
Commence emergency haemodialysis to clear paraprotein and defer chemotherapy until renal function improves

stem: A 38-year-old man is admitted with newly diagnosed Burkitt lymphoma with extensive abdominal disease, bulky retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy and bone marrow involvement. He is haemodynamically stable. Pre-chemotherapy bloods show urate 0.62 mmol/L (RR 0.20-0.42), potassium 4.6, phosphate 1.6 mmol/L, calcium 2.30, creatinine 92 (eGFR 86), LDH 4800 U/L (>10x upper limit), WCC 14 with 8% blasts on film. He has no known G6PD deficiency but has not been formally tested. He is not pregnant. He is to start intensive R-CODOX-M/IVAC chemotherapy within 24 hours. He has no significant cardiac history. He is in a tertiary haematology unit with continuous monitoring. He has good IV access. He has no allopurinol allergy. The haematology team classifies him as high risk for tumour lysis syndrome by Cairo-Bishop criteria. He has no contraindication to IV hydration. The unit has rasburicase available. Renal output is currently 80 mL/hr. He weighs 75 kg.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate prophylactic strategy for tumour lysis syndrome in this high-risk patient?

options.t: Rasburicase 0.2 mg/kg IV daily plus IV hydration 3 L/m^2/day with close electrolyte monitoring
Allopurinol 300 mg daily and IV fluid hydration 2 L/m^2/day, without any rasburicase use
Urinary alkalinisation with intravenous sodium bicarbonate infusion targeting urine pH above 7.5 consistently
Delay chemotherapy by 7 days to allow oral allopurinol to reduce baseline serum urate substantially
Prophylactic upfront haemodialysis arranged via nephrology prior to commencing the first chemotherapy cycle

stem: A 54-year-old woman with AML on day 12 of induction chemotherapy presents to the haematology ward with a temperature of 38.5 deg C on two readings 30 minutes apart. She has had mild rigors. She has no localising symptoms. Her central venous catheter exit site is clean. Her oral mucosa shows grade 2 mucositis without obvious infection. Her last chemotherapy was cytarabine plus daunorubicin completed 8 days ago. She has no penicillin allergy. She has been on aciclovir and fluconazole prophylaxis. She has had no recent travel. She is haemodynamically stable. Her current bloods show neutrophil count 0.2 x10^9/L (severe neutropenia), Hb 92, platelets 38, creatinine 72, lactate 1.4, CRP 78. Blood cultures from the line and a peripheral site are drawn before any antibiotic is given. CXR is clear. Urine dipstick is negative. There is no diarrhoea or cough. She has good IV access. Her MASCC score is calculated as 18 (lower risk, but with mucositis). She has had no antibiotics in the last 90 days. She has no recent C. difficile.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate empirical antibiotic regimen?

options.t: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate plus ciprofloxacin home outpatient regimen
Meropenem 1 g IV every 8 hours as first-line
Piperacillin-tazobactam plus vancomycin plus caspofungin from the outset
Ceftriaxone 1 g IV daily
Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV every 6 hours after cultures

stem: A 64-year-old man with metastatic prostate cancer (PSA 480 ng/mL, known bony metastases) on androgen deprivation therapy presents to ED with 3 days of progressive mid-thoracic back pain that is worse at night and on coughing, and 12 hours of bilateral lower limb weakness. He noticed difficulty initiating micturition this morning. He has had 4 kg weight loss recently. He has no fevers and no constitutional features of infection. On examination he has marked tenderness over the T7-T8 spinous processes. Power is 3/5 bilaterally in hip flexion and knee extension, with brisk knee jerks, upgoing plantars and a sensory level at approximately T8. There is no saddle anaesthesia tested. The post-void bladder scan shows 580 mL retained urine. He is haemodynamically stable. He has not received recent radiotherapy. He has had no falls. He has no anticoagulation. He has no penicillin allergy. He is otherwise mentating normally. He arrived in ED 90 minutes ago. The on-call oncology team is contactable. He has good IV access. He has no contraindication to dexamethasone.
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management step?

options.t: Plain spine X-rays in the emergency department and outpatient MRI booked within 1 week
Dexamethasone 16 mg IV stat AND urgent MRI of the whole spine within 24 hours
Lumbar puncture with CSF analysis to rule out an infective transverse myelopathy first
Commence opioid analgesia with regular paracetamol and review symptoms in clinic next week
Whole-body bone scan to identify the lesion site before any definitive further management

stem: A 68-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer (bony and visceral disease) on letrozole presents with 5 days of progressive lethargy, polyuria, polydipsia, constipation and nausea. She is now confused (GCS 13, normally GCS 15). Her family report poor oral intake for 3 days. She has no fever and no localising signs of infection. She has CKD with baseline creatinine 95 (eGFR 56). Bloods show corrected calcium 3.6 mmol/L (RR 2.10-2.60), creatinine 168 (eGFR 28), albumin 32, phosphate 0.84, magnesium 0.74, PTH suppressed at less than 0.5 pmol/L, PTHrP elevated, alkaline phosphatase 280, vitamin D 62. She is haemodynamically stable but mildly hypotensive at 102/64 with HR 104 reflecting dehydration. ECG shows shortened QT interval, no arrhythmia. She has no current anticoagulation. She has no recent dental work and no osteonecrosis history. She is otherwise mentating moderately. There is no constipation impacting bowel obstruction. She has good IV access. The renal team is contactable.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.t: IV 0.9% sodium chloride 200-300 mL/hr titrated to urine output, then zoledronic acid 4 mg once euvolaemic
IV furosemide 80 mg before adequate fluid resuscitation to enhance urinary calcium excretion early
Subcutaneous calcitonin as sole monotherapy without bisphosphonate or any IV fluid resuscitation at all
Oral prednisolone 40 mg daily without intravenous fluids or bisphosphonate therapy as first-line treatment
Haemodialysis arranged as the first-line treatment regardless of underlying renal function or volume status

stem: A 56-year-old man with cirrhosis presents with massive haematemesis. He arrives in haemorrhagic shock (BP 78/42, HR 142, GCS 13). The massive transfusion protocol is activated and he receives 6 units packed red cells, 4 units FFP and 1 pool of platelets via rapid infuser over 60 minutes. He is being continued on the MTP and IV tranexamic acid 1 g over 10 minutes was given on arrival. Repeat bloods now: Hb 78, platelets 62, INR 2.1, fibrinogen 1.1 g/L, potassium 6.2 mmol/L, ionised calcium 0.84 mmol/L (RR 1.10 to 1.30), pH 7.18, lactate 8.4, base excess minus 12, magnesium 0.62, temperature 35.4 degrees C. ECG shows tall T waves in V2 to V4 with PR 200 ms; QRS 100 ms.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate single next intervention?

options.t: IV calcium gluconate 10 percent 10 mL given over 2 to 3 minutes via a peripheral large-bore cannula
IV insulin 10 units with 50 mL of 50 percent dextrose to shift potassium intracellularly
IV sodium bicarbonate 100 mmol bolus to correct metabolic acidosis
Active rewarming to address hypothermia as the priority complication
Stop the massive transfusion protocol and reassess once haemodynamics improve

subtopic_detail: Adult community-acquired sepsis: empirical regimen per eTG
stem: A 72-year-old man presents to a regional emergency department with 36 hours of dysuria, rigors and increasing confusion. He has type 2 diabetes (metformin), benign prostatic hyperplasia (tamsulosin) and no documented antibiotic allergies. He was independent at baseline and lives with his wife. He has not travelled recently and is fully vaccinated. Triage observations show temperature 38.9 degrees C, heart rate 118 bpm, respiratory rate 26 per minute, blood pressure 96/54 mmHg and SpO2 95 percent on room air. He is peripherally cool with capillary refill 4 seconds and a tender suprapubic region. Bedside urinalysis is positive for nitrites, leucocytes and blood. Venous blood gas shows pH 7.31, lactate 3.6 mmol/L, bicarbonate 18 mmol/L. Initial bloods reveal WCC 16.4 x10^9/L, neutrophils 14.1 x10^9/L, CRP 248 mg/L, creatinine 168 micromol/L (baseline 92), eGFR 32 mL/min/1.73m^2. Two large-bore cannulae are sited. Blood and urine cultures have just been taken. The team is preparing 30 mL/kg crystalloid resuscitation per Surviving Sepsis. He is not pregnant, not neutropenic and has no indwelling devices.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate empirical antibiotic regimen to commence within the next hour?

options.t: IV gentamicin 5 mg/kg single dose plus IV amoxicillin 2 g every 6 hours
IV ceftriaxone 1 g daily plus IV metronidazole 500 mg every 12 hours
Oral trimethoprim 300 mg daily for 7 days after a single IV gentamicin dose
IV piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g every 8 hours as sole agent
IV meropenem 1 g every 8 hours pending blood culture results

subtopic_detail: qSOFA, vasopressor initiation threshold and MAP target
stem: A 58-year-old woman with diet-controlled type 2 diabetes presents from a nursing aged-care facility with 24 hours of productive cough, fevers and lethargy. Background includes COPD (GOLD 2, FEV1 62 percent), ischaemic heart disease with two coronary stents (aspirin, clopidogrel ceased 6 months post-PCI), and chronic kidney disease stage 3a (baseline creatinine 110 micromol/L). She has no penicillin allergy. On arrival, T 39.1 degrees C, HR 124 bpm, RR 28 per minute, BP 84/48 mmHg, SpO2 91 percent on 4 L/min nasal prongs, GCS 13 (E3 V4 M6). Chest examination reveals right basal bronchial breathing and crackles. CXR confirms right lower lobe consolidation. Initial bloods: WCC 21 x10^9/L, neutrophils 18.6, CRP 312 mg/L, lactate 4.8 mmol/L, creatinine 198 micromol/L, eGFR 24, bicarbonate 17 mmol/L. Blood cultures and a respiratory PCR have been collected. IV ceftriaxone 1 g and IV azithromycin 500 mg are running. She has received 30 mL/kg of compound sodium lactate (Hartmann) over 90 minutes. Repeat BP after fluid resuscitation is 86/52 mmHg, HR 118, MAP 63. Urine output is 12 mL/hr. She has no signs of pulmonary oedema and JVP is not elevated. An arterial line and central line are being placed. She has an advanced care directive consenting to ICU-level care.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Continue further 20 mL/kg crystalloid bolus and reassess in 30 minutes
Start IV noradrenaline infusion targeting MAP at least 65 mmHg
Commence IV hydrocortisone 200 mg/day without starting a vasopressor
Switch antibiotics to IV meropenem 1 g 8-hourly before any vasopressor
Transfuse 1 unit of packed red cells to optimise oxygen delivery

stem: A 68-year-old woman presents to a metropolitan emergency department with 12 hours of severe headache, fever, neck stiffness and one episode of vomiting. Her husband reports she has become drowsy in the last hour. Background includes systemic lupus erythematosus on prednisolone 10 mg daily and azathioprine 100 mg daily, and well-controlled hypertension. She has no penicillin allergy and is fully vaccinated, including pneumococcal and the recent meningococcal ACWY booster offered to immunosuppressed patients. There has been no overseas travel and no household contact illness. On arrival, T 39.6 degrees C, HR 112 bpm, RR 22, BP 144/86 mmHg, SpO2 98 percent on room air. GCS 13 (E3 V4 M6). She has neck stiffness, photophobia and no rash. There are no focal neurological deficits, no papilloedema and no recent seizure. CT brain is being arranged before lumbar puncture because of her age, immunosuppression and reduced GCS. Bloods are pending. Blood cultures have been taken. The team is preparing empirical antibiotic and adjunctive steroid therapy without delay. There is no clinical evidence of raised intracranial pressure. The Trust protocol mirrors eTG and Therapeutic Guidelines recommendations.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate empirical regimen to administer now, before CT or LP?

options.t: IV ceftriaxone 2 g 12-hourly alone after CT brain results, without Listeria cover or adjunctive steroid
IV vancomycin 1.5 g 12-hourly plus IV meropenem 2 g 8-hourly without any adjunctive dexamethasone
IV dexamethasone 10 mg with or just before IV ceftriaxone 2 g and IV benzylpenicillin 2.4 g
IV ceftriaxone 2 g 12-hourly plus IV aciclovir 8-hourly empirically, with no Listeria cover
Delay all antimicrobial therapy until lumbar puncture is performed to confirm the causative organism

stem: A previously well 34-year-old primary school teacher presents to the emergency department with three days of worsening headache, fever to 38.6 degrees C and now a generalised seizure witnessed by paramedics, lasting two minutes with full post-ictal recovery over 20 minutes. Her husband describes 24 hours of out-of-character irritability, word-finding difficulty and difficulty naming family pets. She has no past medical history, takes no regular medications, has no allergies and is up to date with vaccinations. There has been no overseas travel, no animal bites and no household sick contacts. On examination she is afebrile post-paracetamol, GCS 13 (E3 V4 M6), with expressive dysphasia, mild right pronator drift and no neck stiffness. There is no rash. Vital signs HR 96, BP 132/78, SpO2 98 percent. A non-contrast CT brain is normal. LP cannot proceed for 60 minutes while a CT venogram is reviewed. Bloods show WCC 9.8 x10^9/L, CRP 22 mg/L, sodium 134 mmol/L, glucose 5.4 mmol/L. There is no coagulopathy. A consultant suspects herpes simplex encephalitis based on the temporal-lobe-pattern features.
lead_in: What is the single best next intervention?

options.t: Order MRI brain and EEG before initiating any antimicrobial therapy
Wait for CSF HSV PCR result before starting any antiviral therapy
Commence IV ceftriaxone 2 g 12-hourly as the priority therapy
Commence IV aciclovir 8-hourly empirically without waiting for LP
Give IV phenytoin loading dose and observe for further seizures

stem: A 27-year-old man who has sex with men presents to a sexual-health clinic 14 days after a single episode of condomless receptive anal intercourse with a partner of unknown serostatus. He reports 5 days of fever to 38.5 degrees C, sore throat, generalised maculopapular rash on the trunk, mouth ulcers, myalgia and tender cervical lymphadenopathy. He is not on PrEP. He has no past medical history and is on no medication. He is otherwise systemically well, alert, drinking and eating. There is no meningism. On examination, T 38.4 degrees C, HR 96, BP 124/76, multiple posterior cervical nodes 1 cm, palatal ulcers, truncal maculopapular rash. Bloods: WCC 4.1 with relative lymphopenia, ALT 92 U/L, AST 84 U/L, CRP 38 mg/L, EBV serology negative for acute infection, CMV IgM negative, monospot negative. He attends specifically asking whether he might have just acquired HIV, and the clinic is following ASHM guidelines for testing in acute infection.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test for suspected acute HIV infection?

options.t: Antibody-only rapid finger-prick HIV 1/2 test as the sole screening investigation
A combined 4th-generation HIV antigen/antibody test plus HIV RNA viral load
HIV Western blot as the primary screening test in suspected acute seroconversion
CD4 lymphocyte count alone as the initial diagnostic investigation in this presentation
Repeat HIV antibody test in 12 weeks before any testing now

subtopic_detail: PJP and toxoplasma prophylaxis CD4 thresholds
stem: A 41-year-old man with newly diagnosed HIV infection is reviewed in the immunology clinic. He has had untreated HIV for an unknown duration and presented with a 4 week history of dry cough and exertional dyspnoea, with desaturation from 96 percent to 88 percent on a 6 minute walk test. He has had thrush and seborrhoeic dermatitis. His CD4 count is 134 cells/microL, HIV viral load 480 000 copies/mL, and toxoplasma IgG positive. CMV IgG positive, CMV IgM negative. Latent TB has been excluded with QuantiFERON-TB Gold. He has no prior allergic reactions to sulfonamides. CXR shows bilateral perihilar interstitial infiltrates. Sputum induction PCR detects Pneumocystis jirovecii. Treatment for the active PJP infection is to be commenced and the team is planning ongoing chemoprophylaxis. He is to start antiretroviral therapy within two weeks. He has no haematological cytopenias, normal LFTs and a creatinine of 78 micromol/L.
lead_in: Which long-term opportunistic infection prophylaxis is most appropriate at his current CD4 count?

options.t: Azithromycin 1250 mg weekly for Mycobacterium avium complex prophylaxis only
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 160/800 mg daily covering PJP and toxoplasma
Valganciclovir 900 mg daily for CMV prophylaxis
Itraconazole 200 mg daily for endemic fungal prophylaxis
Isoniazid 300 mg daily for latent TB prophylaxis

subtopic_detail: Non-occupational PEP within 72 hours after high-risk exposure
stem: A 22-year-old woman presents 36 hours after an episode of condomless receptive vaginal intercourse with a man known to be HIV positive on antiretroviral therapy. Her partner has been undetectable on viral load for 14 months on his last clinic letter, but she cannot reach him to confirm. She has no other STI risk factors recently, is on the combined oral contraceptive pill, has had a normal cervical screening three months ago, and her last HIV test (12 months ago) was negative. She has no significant medical history, no allergies and normal renal function. She has not been on PrEP. She is anxious about acquiring HIV and asks specifically about post-exposure prophylaxis. The clinic follows the ASHM National PEP Guidelines. There are no contraindications to standard PEP. She is not pregnant on point-of-care testing. Hepatitis B serology is negative, hepatitis C antibody negative. She is up to date with HPV vaccination. She agrees to follow-up testing per protocol.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.t: Reassure that PEP is not indicated because the source partner is reportedly virally suppressed
Start daily oral tenofovir-emtricitabine as PrEP, no third agent needed
Start daily oral tenofovir-emtricitabine as PrEP without adding a third antiretroviral agent
Repeat baseline HIV serology in 12 weeks without commencing any antiretroviral therapy now
A single intramuscular dose of cabotegravir as one-off post-exposure prophylaxis therapy

subtopic_detail: QuantiFERON-TB Gold over Mantoux in BCG-vaccinated patients
stem: A 28-year-old woman who arrived from Vietnam 6 months ago is starting a graduate nursing programme at a metropolitan tertiary hospital. She received BCG vaccination in childhood. She is asymptomatic, with no fever, cough, weight loss or night sweats. She has no household TB contacts. She is not immunocompromised, not pregnant, and on no medications. Examination is unremarkable. Pre-employment screening with QuantiFERON-TB Gold returns a positive result. CXR is clear with no parenchymal abnormality, no apical scarring and no calcified granulomata. She has no symptoms or signs of active TB on review. LFTs and HIV serology are normal.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.t: Commence treatment for latent TB infection with rifampicin 600 mg daily for 4 months after baseline LFTs
Confirm the positive result with a Mantoux tuberculin skin test before treating
Commence full 4-drug active TB therapy with rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide and ethambutol under directly observed therapy
No treatment; arrange annual chest X-ray surveillance and clinical review for 5 years
Repeat QuantiFERON in 8 weeks; treat only if the second test remains positive

subtopic_detail: WHO severity criteria and parenteral therapy
stem: A 35-year-old man returns to Sydney from a 3 week trip across rural Papua New Guinea. He took no antimalarial chemoprophylaxis. Five days after return he presents with 4 days of fevers, chills, headache and confusion. He has no past medical history, no allergies, takes no medication, and is up to date with vaccinations. On arrival, T 39.4 degrees C, HR 122, RR 26, BP 102/64, SpO2 96 percent on room air, GCS 13. He has scleral icterus, no rash and no meningism. Bloods: Hb 92 g/L (baseline 145), platelets 38 x10^9/L, WCC 6.4, creatinine 198 micromol/L (baseline 80), bilirubin 64 micromol/L, ALT 86 U/L, lactate 4.2 mmol/L, glucose 3.1 mmol/L, parasitaemia on thick film 8 percent with Plasmodium falciparum gametocytes and ring forms. He has no spontaneous bleeding. Urine output is 30 mL in the last 4 hours. He is being managed in a high-dependency unit. The local infectious diseases service has been contacted and IV artesunate is available on the Special Access Scheme. There are no contraindications, and pregnancy is not relevant (male patient).
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.t: IV artesunate 2.4 mg/kg at 0, 12 and 24 hours then daily, with ICU-level supportive care
Oral artemether-lumefantrine 4 tablets every 12 hours for 3 days as monotherapy
Oral chloroquine 600 mg loading dose followed by a 14-day primaquine course
IV quinine 20 mg/kg loading dose then 10 mg/kg 8-hourly as the first-line agent
Oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily as monotherapy for 7 days without other agents

subtopic_detail: Cellulitis vs erysipelas vs necrotising soft tissue infection
stem: A 58-year-old man with type 2 diabetes (metformin, sitagliptin) and obesity (BMI 38) presents with a 30 hour history of pain in his right calf, out of proportion to the modest superficial erythema. He had a small abrasion at the time of mowing the lawn 3 days ago. He now feels systemically unwell. On arrival, T 38.9 degrees C, HR 124, RR 26, BP 96/58, SpO2 96 percent. The right calf is tense, exquisitely tender beyond the area of redness, with patchy dusky violaceous discolouration and a small area of haemorrhagic bullae developing over the lateral calf in the last 90 minutes. He has no penicillin allergy. He has reduced sensation over the calf compared with the contralateral side. Bedside ultrasound shows fascial-plane gas. Bloods: WCC 19.4 x10^9/L, sodium 130 mmol/L, glucose 16.4 mmol/L, CRP 312 mg/L, creatinine 156 micromol/L, haemoglobin 124 g/L, lactate 3.8 mmol/L. He has no documented HIV or other immunosuppression. He has not received antibiotics yet. The surgical registrar is reviewing.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Urgent surgical exploration and debridement plus IV meropenem 1 g 8-hourly and IV clindamycin 600 mg 8-hourly
IV flucloxacillin 2 g 6-hourly as monotherapy and surgical review in the morning
Limb elevation and oral cefalexin 500 mg 6-hourly with GP follow-up in 48 hours
Hyperbaric oxygen therapy alone without surgical exploration or debridement of necrotic tissue
MRI of the lower limb with contrast to confirm fasciitis before any surgical decision is made

stem: A 74-year-old man with COPD on inhaled salmeterol-fluticasone and tiotropium, type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 58 mmol/mol) and stage 3a CKD (eGFR 52) presents on day 6 of COVID-19 infection. He tested positive 6 days ago on RAT and has been hypoxic at home for 18 hours. On arrival, T 38.4 degrees C, HR 96, RR 26, BP 132/76, SpO2 86 percent on room air, rising to 93 percent on 4 L/min nasal prongs. He is up to date with COVID-19 vaccination (5 doses) and influenza vaccination. He has no allergies. CXR shows bilateral peripheral infiltrates. Bloods: WCC 8.2, CRP 96 mg/L, creatinine 118 micromol/L (baseline 110), LFTs normal. He has no signs of bacterial superinfection and procalcitonin is 0.18 micrograms/L. He has had no recent steroid course at home. He is not on any contraindicated CYP3A4-interacting medication for nirmatrelvir-ritonavir. He is being admitted to the general medical ward with telemetry.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate primary pharmacological therapy for his hospitalised hypoxic COVID-19 illness?

options.t: Oral or IV dexamethasone 6 mg once daily for up to 10 days as primary therapy
Oral nirmatrelvir-ritonavir 300/100 mg every 12 hours for 5 days as primary therapy
IV ceftriaxone 1 g daily plus oral azithromycin 500 mg daily empirically for 5 days
A single dose of IV tocilizumab as the first-line pharmacological agent today
IV pulse methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3 days instead of standard-dose dexamethasone

subtopic_detail: Early treatment of high-risk hospitalised patients
stem: A 79-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes, COPD and chronic heart failure presents in early August with 36 hours of fever 38.7 degrees C, myalgia, dry cough and progressive breathlessness. She had her annual quadrivalent influenza vaccination 6 weeks ago. Chronic medications include metformin, perindopril, bisoprolol, frusemide and inhaled corticosteroids. On arrival: T 38.7, HR 110, RR 24, BP 134/76, SpO2 92 percent on room air. Bibasal crackles without focal consolidation. CXR clear. Influenza A PCR positive. Bloods: WCC 8.2, CRP 56, eGFR 38 mL/min/1.73m^2, creatinine 134 micromol/L (was 96 last month). She is being admitted for supportive care.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate antiviral and supportive management?

options.t: Oral oseltamivir 75 mg once daily (renal-dose reduced) for 5 days, plus targeted supportive care without empirical antibiotics
Oral oseltamivir 75 mg twice daily standard dose with concurrent IV ceftriaxone for empirical bacterial cover
Single oral dose of baloxavir marboxil 40 mg as one-off antiviral therapy
No antiviral therapy because symptom onset is approaching 48 hours and she was vaccinated 6 weeks ago
IV zanamivir 600 mg twice daily as first-line antiviral therapy in renal impairment

subtopic_detail: Empirical IV antibiotics per eTG and oral step-down
stem: A 32-year-old woman presents with 24 hours of right flank pain, fever to 38.8 degrees C, rigors and dysuria. She has no past medical history except recurrent UTIs (3 in the last 12 months, all treated with short oral courses without admission). She is not pregnant (urine beta-hCG negative). She takes the combined oral contraceptive pill. She has no allergies. On arrival, T 38.8, HR 108, RR 18, BP 110/68, SpO2 99 percent. She has right costovertebral angle tenderness. Bloods: WCC 14.8, CRP 134, creatinine 78 micromol/L, lactate 1.4 mmol/L. Urinalysis: nitrites positive, leucocytes positive, blood positive. She is well-perfused, alert, no signs of septic shock. The team plans to admit for IV antibiotic therapy and supportive care, with oral step-down once afebrile. She has had no recent overseas travel, no recent hospitalisation and no recent antibiotic exposure that would raise concern for resistant organisms.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate empirical antibiotic regimen?

options.t: IV meropenem 1 g 8-hourly as first-line empirical therapy
Oral nitrofurantoin 100 mg twice daily for 5 days managed as an outpatient
Oral trimethoprim 300 mg daily for 14 days as outpatient
IV gentamicin (weight- and renal-adjusted) plus IV amoxicillin as first-line empirical therapy
Oral ciprofloxacin 500 mg twice daily for 7 days without inpatient admission

subtopic_detail: Methotrexate first-line per ACR/EULAR and Australian Rheumatology Association
stem: A 46-year-old woman presents to her GP with a 10 week history of bilateral painful, swollen MCP and PIP joints with morning stiffness lasting more than 90 minutes, and fatigue. She has no skin lesions, no nail changes, no recent diarrhoeal illness and no preceding URTI. She has never smoked, drinks no alcohol and has had no preconception planning. She is using condoms for contraception and is not pregnant. Past medical history is unremarkable. She is on no regular medications. On examination, T 36.8, HR 76, BP 124/76. She has symmetrical tender, swollen MCPs and PIPs bilaterally, no DIP involvement, with positive squeeze test of the MCPs. There is no rash, no nail pitting, no enthesitis. Bloods: rheumatoid factor positive (78 IU/mL), anti-CCP antibody strongly positive (180 U/mL), CRP 42 mg/L, ESR 56 mm/hr, FBC, UEC, LFTs normal, hepatitis B and C negative, HIV negative, QuantiFERON-TB Gold negative. She meets ACR/EULAR 2010 classification criteria for rheumatoid arthritis with a score of 8. X-rays of hands show no erosions yet. She is referred urgently to rheumatology with a working diagnosis of early seropositive rheumatoid arthritis.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial conventional synthetic DMARD?

options.t: Oral methotrexate 10-15 mg weekly plus folic acid 5 mg weekly
Oral hydroxychloroquine 400 mg daily as initial DMARD monotherapy without methotrexate
Long-term oral prednisolone 20 mg daily as monotherapy without DMARDs
Subcutaneous adalimumab 40 mg every 2 weeks as initial therapy
Oral methotrexate 10-15 mg once weekly plus folic acid 5 mg once weekly

stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with a 6 month history of fatigue, alopecia, oral ulcers, photosensitive malar rash sparing the nasolabial folds, and intermittent symmetrical polyarthralgia of small joints. She has no nephritic features, no neurological symptoms and no serositis. She is on no regular medications, has no allergies, does not smoke and has occasional alcohol. She has not been pregnant and is using condoms. On examination she is afebrile, BP 124/76. There is a malar rash sparing the nasolabial folds, scarring alopecia, oral ulcers and tender MCPs without synovitis. There is no rash on sun-protected areas. Bloods: ANA strongly positive (homogeneous, titre 1:1280), anti-dsDNA positive (340 IU/mL), anti-Smith positive, C3 0.62 g/L (low), C4 0.08 g/L (low), CRP 6 (normal), ESR 64 mm/hr, FBC: Hb 108 g/L, platelets 124 x10^9/L, leukopenia 3.2 x10^9/L. Urinalysis: no protein, no blood, no casts. Renal function and LFTs normal. She meets ACR/EULAR 2019 classification criteria for SLE. The rheumatologist plans to commence first-line therapy as per Australian Rheumatology Association guidance.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line maintenance therapy plus baseline assessment?

options.t: Hydroxychloroquine up to 5 mg/kg per day plus baseline ophthalmology screening
Long-term oral prednisolone 20 mg daily as sole maintenance
Oral methotrexate 15 mg once weekly as monotherapy without hydroxychloroquine
IV rituximab as initial first-line therapy for cutaneous-musculoskeletal SLE
Oral NSAID monotherapy (e.g. naproxen 500 mg BD) with paracetamol as required

subtopic_detail: Sight-saving high-dose steroid and temporal artery biopsy timing
stem: A 72-year-old woman presents with 3 weeks of new-onset right temporal throbbing headache, scalp tenderness when combing hair, and jaw claudication when chewing tougher foods. Today she reports brief loss of vision in her right eye lasting 6 minutes, fully resolved. She has a 4 month history of bilateral shoulder and hip girdle stiffness lasting 90 minutes each morning, consistent with polymyalgia rheumatica. She has type 2 diabetes (metformin), hypertension (perindopril) and hyperlipidaemia (atorvastatin). She has no penicillin or steroid allergy. On examination, T 36.9, HR 80, BP 144/86. Her right temporal artery is non-pulsatile and tender. Visual acuity is currently 6/9 right and 6/6 left with no afferent pupillary defect. Fundoscopy is unremarkable. There is shoulder and hip girdle tenderness without active synovitis. Bloods: ESR 92 mm/hr, CRP 142 mg/L, FBC normal, glucose 8.1 mmol/L, UEC normal, LFTs normal. The team plans urgent ophthalmology assessment and is determining the immediate management while awaiting temporal artery biopsy.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.t: Defer all corticosteroid therapy until the temporal artery biopsy result returns for confirmation
Oral prednisolone 15 mg daily and outpatient temporal artery biopsy in 6 weeks
IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3 days then high-dose oral prednisolone with biopsy within 1-2 weeks
Oral aspirin 100 mg daily and clinical observation without commencing any corticosteroid therapy
MRI of the head with contrast as the definitive diagnostic test prior to any steroid

subtopic_detail: Distinguishing PMR from GCA overlap and steroid dose
stem: A 68-year-old woman presents to her GP with a 6 week history of bilateral shoulder and hip girdle pain and stiffness, worst in the mornings with 90 minutes of stiffness improving through the day. Over the past week she has developed a new left-sided temporal headache that is constant and aching, and yesterday her jaw ached after chewing toast. Her vision is normal and there is no scalp tenderness on direct questioning. She has no fevers, no rash, no joint swelling. Past medical history includes hypertension on perindopril and type 2 diabetes on metformin. Examination: T 36.6, BP 132/78. Shoulder and hip girdle tender, passive range of movement painful but full; temporal arteries palpable and slightly tender on the left; normal fundoscopy, normal visual acuity, no neurological deficit. Bloods: ESR 78 mm/hr, CRP 92 mg/L, CK normal, TSH normal, anti-CCP and rheumatoid factor negative.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial management?

options.t: Oral prednisolone 15 mg daily with arrangement of an urgent rheumatology review
Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg (60 mg) daily commenced immediately, with urgent temporal artery biopsy arranged within 1 to 2 weeks
IV pulse methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3 days followed by oral prednisolone 60 mg daily
NSAID monotherapy with naproxen 500 mg twice daily plus PPI cover and review in 2 weeks
Oral methotrexate 15 mg weekly as initial steroid-sparing monotherapy with folate cover

subtopic_detail: KDIGO eGFR/ACR matrix + KHA-CARI referral thresholds
stem: A 64-year-old man attends his GP for a chronic disease check. He has type 2 diabetes for 12 years, hypertension, and a BMI of 33 kg/m^2. Medications include perindopril 5 mg, metformin 1 g BD, empagliflozin 10 mg, and atorvastatin 40 mg. He denies haematuria, frothy urine, oedema or new urinary symptoms and has had no recent illness, NSAIDs or contrast exposure. He has never been seen by a nephrologist. Examination shows BP 142/86 mmHg, no peripheral oedema, normal heart sounds and chest. Fundi show background diabetic retinopathy. Today's results in stable conditions: serum creatinine 168 micromol/L (was 162 three months ago and 158 six months ago); eGFR 38 mL/min/1.73m^2; urine albumin to creatinine ratio (ACR) on a first-morning sample is 42 mg/mmol, confirmed on a repeat sample two weeks later at 38 mg/mmol; serum potassium 4.6 mmol/L; HbA1c 58 mmol/mol; haemoglobin 128 g/L. He has had no episodes of AKI in the past year.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in his renal management?

options.t: Continue GP-led care and recheck eGFR and ACR in 6 months without specialist input
Refer only if eGFR falls below 30 mL/min/1.73m^2 on the next test
Stop empagliflozin because the eGFR has fallen below 45 mL/min/1.73m^2
Stop perindopril given the modest creatinine rise and switch to amlodipine
Refer to nephrology for assessment given persistent ACR above 30 mg/mmol

subtopic_detail: Iron repletion before ESA, KDIGO Hb thresholds
stem: A 72-year-old woman with CKD stage 4 (eGFR 22 mL/min/1.73m^2, secondary to long-standing hypertension and obstructive uropathy after pelvic radiotherapy) is seen in renal outpatients. She reports six weeks of fatigue and exertional dyspnoea climbing one flight of stairs. She denies overt bleeding, melaena, haematuria or menorrhagia (post-menopausal). Medications: amlodipine 10 mg, irbesartan 300 mg, atorvastatin 40 mg, cholecalciferol 1000 IU. She does not take NSAIDs. There has been no recent infection or hospitalisation. Examination: BP 138/82, conjunctival pallor, mild ankle oedema, otherwise unremarkable. Bloods (today): Hb 96 g/L, MCV 88 fL, ferritin 78 microgram/L, transferrin saturation 14 percent, CRP 4 mg/L, B12 and folate normal, reticulocyte count low, creatinine 232 micromol/L (stable), eGFR 22, K 4.4 mmol/L, PTH 18 pmol/L, corrected Ca 2.30 mmol/L, phosphate 1.4 mmol/L. Faecal occult blood is negative on three samples.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in managing her anaemia?

options.t: Commence subcutaneous darbepoetin alfa and review Hb in 4 weeks
Give IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose) targeting TSAT 20-30% and ferritin 100-500, then reassess Hb
Trial oral ferrous sulfate 325 mg daily and recheck iron studies in 3 months
Transfuse 2 units of packed red cells today and arrange outpatient ESA
Investigate for occult GI malignancy with colonoscopy before any treatment

subtopic_detail: AEIOU + advance care planning in frail older adult
stem: An 82-year-old woman with CKD stage 5 (eGFR 8 mL/min/1.73m^2 due to renovascular disease and longstanding T2DM) is admitted with fatigue, anorexia and one week of intermittent confusion. She lives alone with package supports, has a Clinical Frailty Scale of 7, and a Charlson index of 9 (T2DM with retinopathy and neuropathy, IHD with PCI, HF with EF 35 percent, COPD). She has been counselled previously about home haemodialysis and peritoneal dialysis and has expressed a preference 'not to live on a machine' but has not formalised this. She is alert, oriented to person and place only. Bloods: K 5.4 mmol/L, HCO3 19 mmol/L, urea 38 mmol/L, creatinine 412 micromol/L, Hb 92 g/L, no acidaemia on VBG (pH 7.34), no pulmonary oedema on CXR, urine output 1100 mL/day. She has not been exposed to NSAIDs, contrast or new nephrotoxins.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.t: Insert a tunnelled vascath and commence urgent in-centre haemodialysis today
Hold a formal goals-of-care conversation and offer renal supportive (conservative) care
Commence inpatient peritoneal dialysis training and discharge home on PD
Insert an indwelling urinary catheter for accurate urine output then discharge with GP review
Transfuse 1 unit of packed red cells targeting Hb 110 g/L to relieve her fatigue

subtopic_detail: Pre-renal vs intrinsic - urine sodium and FENa interpretation
stem: A 68-year-old man presents to ED with five days of vomiting, diarrhoea and reduced oral intake after a community gastroenteritis outbreak. Past history: hypertension, T2DM, IHD with previous CABG. Medications: perindopril 8 mg, indapamide 2.5 mg, metformin 1 g BD, empagliflozin 10 mg (held 48 hours ago by his GP), atorvastatin 40 mg, aspirin 100 mg. He has not taken any NSAIDs or had recent imaging contrast. On arrival: BP 96/58 mmHg lying, 78/44 mmHg sitting, HR 112, dry mucous membranes, JVP not visible, no peripheral oedema, soft non-tender abdomen, bladder not palpable. Bloods: Na 148 mmol/L, K 4.0, urea 22 mmol/L, creatinine 198 micromol/L (baseline 88 three months ago), eGFR 28, HCO3 19, lactate 2.4, glucose 9 mmol/L, CRP 12. Urinalysis: SG 1.030, no blood, no protein, no nitrites, no casts. Spot urine: sodium 8 mmol/L, urine creatinine 12 mmol/L, urine osmolality 612 mOsm/kg. Renal tract US: normal-sized kidneys, no hydronephrosis.
lead_in: Which finding best supports a pre-renal aetiology over established acute tubular necrosis?

options.t: Postural hypotension on arrival with dry mucous membranes on examination
Creatinine has more than doubled from her three-month baseline value
Urine sodium 8 mmol/L with FENa under 1% and concentrated urine
Hypernatraemia (sodium 148 mmol/L) reflecting a free water deficit clinically
Absence of hydronephrosis on bedside renal tract ultrasound today

stem: A 71-year-old woman with CKD stage 3b (eGFR 38 mL/min/1.73m^2) and T2DM (HbA1c 56 mmol/mol) is scheduled tomorrow for an elective coronary angiogram for stable angina. She takes perindopril, atorvastatin, aspirin, metformin and empagliflozin. She is euvolaemic on examination with no peripheral oedema, JVP not raised, clear chest, BP 134/78 mmHg, K 4.1 mmol/L, creatinine 132 micromol/L. She has no decompensated heart failure or current sepsis. The cardiologist plans iso-osmolar low-volume iodinated contrast.
lead_in: Which combined peri-procedural plan most appropriately reduces her risk of contrast-associated AKI and metabolic complications?

options.t: Continue all current medications and prescribe IV 0.9 percent sodium chloride 1 mL/kg/h for 6 hours before and 6 hours after contrast
Withhold metformin and empagliflozin on the day of and 48 hours after contrast, give IV 0.9 percent sodium chloride 1 mL/kg/h pre and post, and recheck creatinine before reinstating
Commence oral N-acetylcysteine 1200 mg twice daily on the day of and after the procedure, continue all usual medications
Give IV sodium bicarbonate infusion in place of normal saline, with IV frusemide 40 mg before contrast to maintain urine output
Withhold all antihypertensives for 48 hours before angiogram and recheck creatinine on the day

stem: A 34-year-old man presents to his GP with two weeks of progressive ankle and periorbital swelling, weight gain of 6 kg, and frothy urine. He has no joint pains, rashes, recent sore throat, skin infection, or haemoptysis. He takes no regular medications. Examination: BP 138/86 mmHg, periorbital and bilateral pitting peripheral oedema to the knees, chest clear, no purpura. Bloods: albumin 22 g/L, cholesterol 8.8 mmol/L, creatinine 88 micromol/L, eGFR over 90, normal FBC and LFTs, complement C3 and C4 normal, ANA and ANCA negative, hepatitis B and C and HIV negative, HbA1c 38 mmol/mol. Urinalysis: protein 4+, blood trace, no leucocytes or nitrites, no red cell casts on microscopy. Spot urine protein-creatinine ratio 480 mg/mmol.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next investigation to establish the underlying diagnosis?

options.t: Renal biopsy with light microscopy, immunofluorescence and electron microscopy
Serum and urine electrophoresis with free light chain assay to exclude paraprotein
Anti-phospholipase A2 receptor (anti-PLA2R) antibody titre as a standalone diagnostic test
Renal tract ultrasound with Doppler to assess kidney size and vascular flow
Empirical trial of high-dose oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily and recheck in 8 weeks

subtopic_detail: ACR/UPCR treatment threshold and ACEi/ARB role
stem: A 27-year-old previously well man is referred to nephrology after two episodes of frank haematuria coinciding with a sore throat. Renal biopsy six weeks ago confirmed IgA nephropathy with mesangial proliferation and no crescents. He has no rash, joint pain or systemic symptoms. Family history of CKD is negative. Today, after 12 weeks of lifestyle advice and observation, his BP is 138/88 mmHg, eGFR is 78 mL/min/1.73m^2 and stable, ACR is 78 mg/mmol (approximately 0.78 g/g; estimated 24-hour proteinuria 1.1 g) on first-morning samples on two occasions. Microscopic haematuria persists. He takes no regular medications. Hepatitis serology is negative. He has no contraindication to ACE inhibitors, no recent pregnancy plans (not relevant - male) and potassium is 4.2 mmol/L. He has no signs of nephrotic syndrome and complement is normal.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in his management?

options.t: Continue lifestyle measures alone and recheck urine ACR in six months' time
Commence high-dose IV cyclophosphamide induction immediately without further investigation
Commence high-dose oral prednisolone daily as first-line agent today
Refer for elective tonsillectomy as the patient's definitive disease-modifying therapy
Maximise ACE inhibitor or ARB, targeting BP < 120/70 mmHg and proteinuria < 1 g/day

stem: A 64-year-old woman is admitted with three weeks of malaise, weight loss, sinus crusting and small-volume haemoptysis, on a background of two months of arthralgia. She has no history of smoking, no exposure to silica or solvents. Examination: BP 156/92 mmHg, saturation 95% on RA, scattered fine crackles bilaterally, mild ankle oedema, palpable purpura on the lower legs. Bloods on admission: creatinine 312 micromol/L (was 84 three months ago), eGFR 14, urea 28, K 4.8, Hb 96 g/L, platelets 380, WCC 11, CRP 124, ESR 92, albumin 28 g/L. Urinalysis: blood 3+, protein 2+, red cell casts seen on phase-contrast microscopy. UPCR 180 mg/mmol. Serology: MPO-ANCA positive, PR3-ANCA negative, ANA negative, anti-GBM negative, complement C3 and C4 normal, hepatitis B/C and HIV negative. CT chest: bilateral peripheral ground-glass opacities. Renal biopsy is being arranged urgently.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial induction regimen?

options.t: Oral prednisolone alone titrated to symptom control over weeks
IV methylprednisolone then oral prednisolone, plus rituximab (or cyclophosphamide), with PJP prophylaxis
Therapeutic plasma exchange alone as the sole intervention for induction
Oral mycophenolate mofetil 1 g twice daily as monotherapy for induction
Await renal biopsy result before starting any disease-modifying immunosuppression

subtopic_detail: Acute interstitial nephritis vs acute tubular necrosis
stem: A 62-year-old woman is referred to ED with a creatinine rise noted on routine bloods. Six weeks ago she was commenced on a proton pump inhibitor for reflux and three weeks ago she had a course of amoxicillin-clavulanate for sinusitis. She reports a non-pruritic maculopapular rash on her trunk over the last week, intermittent low-grade fever, and mild fatigue. She denies vomiting, diarrhoea, dehydration, NSAID use, or contrast exposure. Her usual medications are perindopril and metformin. Examination: BP 134/82, HR 84, T 37.6, mild non-blanching erythematous rash trunk and proximal limbs, no oedema, JVP not raised, clear chest. Bloods: Cr 198 micromol/L (baseline 70), eGFR 28, K 4.4, HCO3 21, eosinophils 0.8 x10^9/L (raised), CRP 24. Urinalysis: blood trace, protein 1+, leucocytes 2+, nitrites negative. Urine microscopy: white cells present, no bacteria, no red cell casts; eosinophiluria reported on Wright stain. Renal tract US: normal-sized kidneys, no hydronephrosis.
lead_in: Which is the most likely diagnosis and most appropriate next step?

options.t: Pre-renal AKI - hold the ACE inhibitor and rehydrate with IV crystalloid
Acute tubular necrosis - give IV crystalloid resuscitation and observe with serial bloods
Pyelonephritis - commence IV gentamicin and ceftriaxone after blood and urine cultures
Rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis - urgent biopsy and pulse IV methylprednisolone
Acute interstitial nephritis - withdraw the offending drug(s) and consider corticosteroids if no improvement

subtopic_detail: CK threshold, IV crystalloid, hyperkalaemia surveillance
stem: A 52-year-old man is brought to ED after being found on the floor of his home approximately 18 hours after a fall complicated by alcohol intoxication. He has no head injury but is unable to bear weight on the left side due to pain. Past history: hypertension on perindopril, gout on allopurinol, harmful alcohol use. Examination: GCS 15, BP 132/82, HR 104, RR 18, T 36.8, SpO2 97% RA, dependent oedema and skin tenderness over the left thigh and calf, dark tea-coloured urine when catheterised. Bloods on arrival: Cr 168 micromol/L (baseline 88), K 5.6, HCO3 19, Na 138, urea 12, glucose 6.0, CK 84,000 U/L, troponin not elevated, lactate 2.6, AST 380, ALT 110. Urinalysis: dipstick positive for blood 4+, but microscopy shows no red blood cells. ECG: sinus tachycardia, no peaked T waves. He has not taken statins recently and no other myotoxic drug exposure is identified.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.t: IV frusemide 80 mg bolus to force diuresis through depleted tubules
Urgent haemodialysis via a temporary vascath to clear circulating myoglobin
Aggressive IV isotonic crystalloid (1-2 L/h) titrated to urine output 200-300 mL/h
IV mannitol 1 g/kg bolus to scavenge oxygen free radicals and force diuresis
Restrict fluids and observe given a normal blood pressure on arrival

stem: A 74-year-old man with CKD stage 4 (eGFR 22 mL/min/1.73m^2) and heart failure attends ED with malaise and palpitations. Medications: irbesartan 300 mg, spironolactone 25 mg, frusemide 40 mg, metoprolol, atorvastatin. He was started on trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 5 days ago for a UTI. Examination: BP 138/82, HR 52 regular, alert. Bloods on arrival: K 7.3 mmol/L, Na 134, Cr 248 micromol/L (was 198 last month), HCO3 19, glucose 6.4. ECG shows sinus bradycardia with prolonged PR, broad QRS approximately 140 ms, and tall peaked T waves in V2-V5. You have given IV calcium gluconate 10 percent 10 mL over 2 minutes and the QRS narrows on repeat ECG.
lead_in: Which combination is the most appropriate next set of interventions?

options.t: Repeat IV calcium gluconate 10 percent 10 mL every 30 minutes while awaiting dialysis

IV actrapid insulin 10 units with 50 mL of 50 percent dextrose plus nebulised salbutamol 10 mg, stop irbesartan, spironolactone and trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, and arrange urgent renal review for dialysis
Oral sodium polystyrene sulfonate 30 g and IV frusemide 80 mg bolus then admit for telemetry overnight
IV sodium bicarbonate 100 mmol over 30 minutes plus continue irbesartan and spironolactone at half dose
Withhold further interventions and recheck potassium in 4 hours, planning shift therapy only if it remains above 6.5

stem: A 68-year-old woman is admitted from a residential aged care facility with two days of vomiting and reduced oral intake. She has long-standing heart failure on frusemide 80 mg daily and metolazone 2.5 mg three times weekly. Other medications include pantoprazole for several years, digoxin, perindopril and atorvastatin. Examination: BP 122/72, HR 96 with frequent ectopics, dry mucous membranes, no oedema. Bloods on admission: K 2.6 mmol/L, Mg 0.55 mmol/L, Ca 2.30 corrected, Na 132, HCO3 32, Cr 142, eGFR 38, digoxin level pending. ECG: sinus rhythm 96, prominent U waves, occasional ventricular ectopics, QT prolonged at 480 ms. She has received 40 mmol of oral KCl slow-release tablets six hours ago at the facility but the potassium has not improved on the repeat venous sample. She is not on insulin and has no diabetic ketoacidosis.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in correcting her electrolytes?

options.t: Start an IV potassium infusion at 40 mmol/hour via peripheral cannula
Increase oral potassium chloride slow-release tablets to 80 mmol immediately
Replace magnesium intravenously (e.g. magnesium sulfate 10 mmol) alongside potassium replacement
Stop pantoprazole and recheck potassium in 48 hours
Give IV sodium bicarbonate for the alkalosis

subtopic_detail: Outpatient SIADH workup - drug and paraneoplastic causes
stem: A 72-year-old man is referred from his GP after an asymptomatic sodium of 124 mmol/L on a routine pre-operative panel. He has a 50-pack-year smoking history and a chronic cough for three months with mild weight loss. Medications: amlodipine, atorvastatin, sertraline (started six months ago) and carbamazepine (long-standing for trigeminal neuralgia). He denies polydipsia and is otherwise well. Examination: BP 132/78 lying and standing, JVP not raised, mucous membranes moist, no peripheral oedema, normal CVS and respiratory exam. Bloods: Na 124, K 3.9, urea 3.0, creatinine 76 micromol/L, glucose 5.0, serum osmolality 258 mOsm/kg, urine osmolality 412 mOsm/kg, urine sodium 48 mmol/L, TSH 1.8 mIU/L, 9 am cortisol 380 nmol/L.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.t: Cease sertraline and carbamazepine simultaneously and recheck sodium in 2 weeks
Arrange CT chest with contrast and review medications with a structured taper plan
Commence tolvaptan 15 mg daily and titrate to sodium response over the next week
Empirical hypertonic 3 percent saline 100 mL bolus and admit for ongoing infusion
Restrict fluid intake to 800 mL daily and reassure that no further investigation is needed

subtopic_detail: Central vs nephrogenic - water deprivation + desmopressin
stem: A 45-year-old woman is referred to endocrine clinic with three months of polyuria and polydipsia (estimated 6 to 7 L per day), worse at night. She has bipolar disorder on lithium 750 mg daily for 12 years, stable, with no recent mood episodes. Medications: lithium, escitalopram. BP 116/72, weight 64 kg, mucous membranes slightly dry. Bloods: Na 148 mmol/L, K 4.2, creatinine 92 micromol/L, eGFR 64, corrected calcium 2.34 mmol/L, glucose 5.2, HbA1c 38, TSH 1.6, lithium 0.7 mmol/L. Plasma osmolality 308 mOsm/kg, urine osmolality 198 mOsm/kg. Water deprivation: urine osmolality 220 mOsm/kg after 6 hours, rising only to 245 mOsm/kg after subcutaneous desmopressin 2 microgram. Her psychiatrist has communicated that lithium remains the preferred mood stabiliser.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate ongoing management strategy?

options.t: Discontinue lithium immediately and switch to sodium valproate, then reassess polyuria in 6 weeks
Continue lithium, add amiloride 5 to 10 mg daily, ensure free access to water and monitor sodium and lithium levels
Commence intranasal desmopressin 10 microgram twice daily and continue lithium unchanged
Restrict fluid intake to 1.5 L daily and recheck sodium and lithium level in 4 weeks
Start a high-protein high-sodium diet to enhance free water clearance and continue lithium

stem: A 67-year-old man presents with two weeks of fatigue, anorexia, constipation and confusion. He has a history of 6 months of progressive low back pain attributed to musculoskeletal strain and a 4 kg unintentional weight loss. Examination: GCS 14 (oriented to person and place, not time), BP 134/78 lying, 110/68 standing, HR 100, dry mucous membranes, mild tenderness over lumbar spine, otherwise unremarkable. Bloods on admission: corrected calcium 3.42 mmol/L, phosphate 0.65 mmol/L (low), albumin 32 g/L, creatinine 152 micromol/L (baseline 84), eGFR 38, ESR 92, total protein 96 g/L with reversed albumin:globulin ratio, Hb 92 g/L, normal WCC and platelets, 25-OH vitamin D 62 nmol/L. PTH (intact) is 0.6 pmol/L (low). Urine dipstick: protein 2+. Skeletal survey shows lytic lesions in the lumbar spine.
lead_in: Which is the most likely cause of his hypercalcaemia?

options.t: Vitamin D toxicity
Primary hyperparathyroidism
Multiple myeloma
Granulomatous disease (sarcoidosis)
Thiazide-induced hypercalcaemia

subtopic_detail: Drug dose adjustment and avoidance in CKD
stem: A 78-year-old woman with CKD stage 4 (eGFR 24 mL/min/1.73m^2) is reviewed after a fall. Medications: perindopril 5 mg, metformin 1 g BD, gabapentin 600 mg three times daily, morphine MR 30 mg twice daily, ciprofloxacin 500 mg BD (day 2 of 5 for uncomplicated UTI started by her GP), and apixaban 5 mg BD for atrial fibrillation. She weighs 58 kg. She is drowsy and ataxic, but rousable, with no focal neurology and no head injury. BP 132/76. Bloods: Cr 220 micromol/L (was 195 last month), eGFR 24, K 4.8, HCO3 21, INR 1.1.
lead_in: Which combination of medication changes is most urgently required?

options.t: Reduce gabapentin dose, switch morphine to a less renally cleared opioid such as buprenorphine or fentanyl, and reduce apixaban to 2.5 mg BD given age over 80 and weight under 60 kg
Stop metformin permanently, continue gabapentin and morphine at current doses, and continue apixaban 5 mg BD
Switch apixaban to warfarin titrated to INR 2.0 to 3.0 and continue all other medications
Stop ciprofloxacin and switch to oral nitrofurantoin 100 mg BD for the remaining UTI course
Stop perindopril today and recheck creatinine and potassium in 1 week

stem: A 32-year-old G1P0 woman at 33 weeks and 2 days gestation attends day assessment with a two-day history of new frontal headache and intermittent right upper quadrant discomfort that has worsened over the last 24 hours. She denies visual disturbance, scotomata, abdominal pain in other regions, vaginal bleeding or reduced foetal movements. Her booking blood pressure at 12 weeks was 108/64 mmHg and all booking bloods (including renal, hepatic and platelet counts) were normal. Her dating ultrasound was reassuring and there is no personal or family history of hypertensive disease. On three repeated measurements 15 minutes apart today her blood pressure is 152/98 mmHg, 154/96 mmHg and 150/99 mmHg in the seated position with an appropriately sized cuff. A spot urinary protein/creatinine ratio is 18 mg/mmol, below the SOMANZ significant proteinuria threshold of 30 mg/mmol, and she has only trace blood on dipstick.
