The midwife notes hyperreflexia without clonus and asks the obstetric registrar whether the patient meets the diagnostic criteria for pre-eclampsia in the absence of significant proteinuria, given the bedside biochemistry shown below.
lead_in: Which feature in this presentation independently satisfies the SOMANZ diagnostic criteria for pre-eclampsia in the absence of significant proteinuria?

options.t: Serum urate above the trimester-specific reference range value in isolation alone here
Hyperreflexia on clinical examination without any ankle clonus present on today's review
Platelet count 92 x10^9/L with transaminases above twice the upper limit of normal
New right upper quadrant pain in clinical isolation without any other PET features
Urine protein/creatinine ratio of 18 mg/mmol on a spot midstream sample today

stem: A 28-year-old G2P1 woman at 35 weeks and 1 day gestation is admitted to the birth suite with severe pre-eclampsia. Her blood pressure is 168/112 mmHg confirmed on three measurements 15 minutes apart despite oral labetalol 200 mg taken two hours ago, she reports a brisk frontal headache and visual scotomata, platelets are 86 x10^9/L and ALT is 130 U/L (more than three times the upper limit of normal). There is no clonus and no focal neurology; reflexes are 3+. Renal function is normal with creatinine 68 micromol/L and urine output is 60 mL/h over the last four hours. CTG shows a reactive trace with no decelerations and the foetus is appropriately grown on recent ultrasound. The consultant decides to commence intravenous magnesium sulphate for seizure prophylaxis prior to expedited delivery, and asks the registrar to set up the SOMANZ/RANZCOG infusion regimen. The midwife asks specifically about the correct loading dose, the maintenance infusion rate, the monitoring required during the infusion, and what to do if a generalised tonic-clonic seizure occurs while the patient is already on the maintenance infusion.
lead_in: Which magnesium sulphate regimen most closely matches Australian (SOMANZ/RANZCOG) recommendations for this patient?

options.t: Load 2 g IV over 20 minutes, then 0.5 g/h maintenance infusion, and 1 g IV bolus only if a seizure occurs
Load 4 g IV over 15-20 minutes, then 1 g/h maintenance, with hourly monitoring of reflexes, respiratory rate and urine output; recurrent seizures treated with a further 2-4 g bolus
Load 6 g IV over 20 minutes, then 2 g/h maintenance infusion, with no monitoring required while urine output remains adequate hourly
Load 10 g IM (5 g into each buttock) without any IV loading dose, then 5 g IM every 4 hours as maintenance
Load 4 g IV over 5 minutes, then 1 g/h maintenance, ceasing the infusion if patellar reflexes are absent and giving calcium chloride only if respiratory rate falls below 12

stem: A 26-year-old G1P0 woman at 37 weeks and 4 days gestation, previously well with an uncomplicated pregnancy until last week when her blood pressure was first noted to be 142/88 mmHg, is brought to the birth suite by her partner after a witnessed generalised tonic-clonic seizure at home lasting two minutes followed by a period of unresponsiveness. She has had a worsening frontal headache, blurred vision and epigastric pain for 24 hours. On arrival she is post-ictal but rousable to voice. Her blood pressure is 175/115 mmHg and 172/110 mmHg on repeat 5 minutes apart with an appropriately sized cuff. She has 3+ proteinuria on dipstick, mild peripheral oedema and hyperreflexia with three beats of clonus. There is no focal neurology, no neck stiffness and no fever, making other intracranial causes less likely. Foetal heart rate on CTG is 145 bpm with reduced variability and no decelerations. The senior registrar is in the room, the anaesthetist and obstetric consultant are being paged, an IV line is in situ and bloods are being drawn for urgent FBC, coagulation, U&E and LFTs.
lead_in: Which sequence of immediate management steps is most appropriate?

options.t: Immediate caesarean section under general anaesthesia before commencing any magnesium sulphate or antihypertensive therapy at all
IV diazepam 10 mg as the first-line anticonvulsant, then a phenytoin loading dose, then expedite delivery in theatre
Sublingual nifedipine to rapidly reduce blood pressure, then immediate transfer directly to theatre without anticonvulsant cover at all
Left lateral with oxygen, magnesium sulphate 4 g IV over 15 minutes then 1 g/h, labetalol or hydralazine, then deliver
Continuous EEG monitoring at the bedside before commencing any anticonvulsant therapy or antihypertensive medication in this setting

subtopic_detail: Gestational diabetes - ADIPS 75 g OGTT thresholds
stem: A 30-year-old G2P1 with booking BMI 27, no diabetes history, no PCOS, no previous GDM has a 75 g OGTT at 27 weeks: fasting 5.3, 1 h 8.4, 2 h 7.1 mmol/L. The midwife asks whether this meets Australian (ADIPS) criteria for gestational diabetes, and if so, what the immediate next step is. The woman has no symptoms and her foetus is growing on the 50th centile.
lead_in: Which interpretation and next step are correct?

options.t: GDM diagnosed on the fasting >=5.1 mmol/L cut-off; commence dietitian and diabetes educator referral, capillary glucose monitoring 4 times daily, and review at the joint diabetes-in-pregnancy clinic within 1 week
Not GDM; only the fasting result is borderline; repeat OGTT in 4 weeks
GDM only if fasting >=5.5 mmol/L; her result is below threshold
Type 2 diabetes diagnosed (fasting >=7.0); commence insulin today
Borderline only; reassure with healthy-eating advice and re-test at 32 weeks

subtopic_detail: GDM - insulin titration after lifestyle failure
stem: A 33-year-old G2P1 woman at 30 weeks and 2 days gestation was diagnosed with GDM at 26 weeks (fasting 5.4 mmol/L, 2 h 9.1 mmol/L on 75 g OGTT). She has been on structured dietary modification with a credentialled dietitian and 30 minutes of daily walking for four weeks. Her four-times-daily capillary glucose diary now shows fasting values consistently 5.6-6.2 mmol/L (ADIPS target <5.1 mmol/L) and 2-hour postprandial values 5.8-6.8 mmol/L (target <6.7 mmol/L) after all three meals. There is no overnight hypoglycaemia, no symptomatic hypoglycaemia, no glycosuria and no ketonuria on bedside testing. Foetal ultrasound at 30 weeks shows estimated foetal weight on the 78th centile with appropriate liquor and normal umbilical and middle cerebral artery Dopplers. She has no contraindication to insulin or metformin, her renal function is normal (creatinine 58 micromol/L), and she has good health literacy and dexterity for self-injection. Her booking BMI was 31 and she has gained 6 kg in pregnancy.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next pharmacological step?

options.t: Commence basal-bolus insulin with rapid-acting analogue at meals plus bedtime NPH
Commence rapid-acting prandial insulin (aspart or lispro) before each meal only, no basal
Continue lifestyle measures only and review fasting glucose again in four weeks
Commence empagliflozin 10 mg orally daily as monotherapy in pregnancy
Commence isophane (NPH) insulin at bedtime, titrated to fasting capillary glucose target

stem: A 29-year-old G1P0 at 28 weeks with gestational hypertension (persistent BP 144/92 over a fortnight; no proteinuria; bloods normal; normal foetal growth and Dopplers) is asymptomatic. She has well-controlled severe asthma on inhaled budesonide/formoterol with two emergency presentations in the past year requiring oral prednisolone. She has no other medical conditions, no allergies, normal renal and liver function. The consultant is choosing the first-line oral antihypertensive.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line oral antihypertensive?

options.t: Labetalol 100 mg orally twice daily titrated to target
Slow-release nifedipine 20 mg orally twice daily titrated to target
Enalapril 5 mg orally once daily titrated to target
Methyldopa 250 mg orally three times daily titrated to target
Oral hydralazine 25 mg three times daily titrated to target

stem: A 30-year-old G1P0 at 28+3 weeks presents with 3 days of fever 38.6, myalgia, headache and mild non-bloody diarrhoea after eating unpasteurised soft cheese 1 week earlier. Several other guests have had similar symptoms. CTG reactive at 145 bpm baseline. Examination: looks unwell but not septic, chest clear, abdomen soft, uterus non-tender. Blood and urine cultures taken before any antibiotics. WCC 14.5, CRP 92, blood cultures pending. She has no penicillin allergy, normal renal function, takes only pregnancy supplements. The microbiology registrar advises empirical cover for the most likely food-borne organism in this setting.
lead_in: Which empirical antimicrobial regimen is most appropriate?

options.t: IV ampicillin 2 g 6-hourly plus IV gentamicin 4-5 mg/kg daily, pending cultures
IV ceftriaxone 2 g daily as monotherapy
Oral amoxicillin 1 g three times daily without IV cover
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole IV monotherapy
IV vancomycin monotherapy

stem: A 27-year-old G1P0 woman at 11 weeks and 2 days gestation has a routine booking infection screen which shows Toxoplasma gondii IgM positive and IgG positive with low avidity, a pattern strongly suggesting recent primary infection within the last 12-16 weeks. She has had a new indoor kitten at home since six weeks before conception and recalls cleaning the litter tray on multiple occasions without gloves. She also recalls eating undercooked lamb skewers at a barbecue 10 weeks ago. She is asymptomatic, has no lymphadenopathy on examination and her partner is well with no symptoms. Toxoplasma PCR on maternal blood is positive.. Her booking ultrasound today shows a viable singleton with normal anatomy for gestation and no calcifications.
lead_in: Which initial pharmacological strategy is most appropriate?

options.t: Spiramycin 1 g TDS, switching to sulphadiazine-pyrimethamine-folinic acid only if amniocentesis confirms foetal infection
Sulphadiazine plus pyrimethamine plus folinic acid commenced immediately for all women regardless of foetal status
Co-trimoxazole oral monotherapy without folinic acid or any foetal assessment as the definitive treatment
Azithromycin 500 mg orally daily as definitive monotherapy without spiramycin, folinic acid or referral
Termination of pregnancy without treatment given certain severe congenital toxoplasmosis at this gestation

subtopic_detail: CMV in pregnancy - primary vs non-primary infection
stem: A 29-year-old G2P1 woman at 14 weeks and 1 day gestation works as a long-day-care educator caring for children aged 12-24 months and presents to her GP with a fortnight of mild fatigue, low-grade fever, headache, mild cervical lymphadenopathy and arthralgia. She has no rash, no respiratory symptoms and no jaundice. Routine TORCH testing shows CMV IgM positive, CMV IgG positive with low avidity (consistent with infection within the past three months). Critically, her booking CMV IgG four weeks earlier was negative, providing serological evidence of seroconversion in early pregnancy. A maternal blood CMV PCR is positive at a moderate viral load. She has no prior infectious diseases history, no immunosuppression, and her previous pregnancy was uncomplicated with a healthy term infant. She asks the maternal-foetal medicine consultant about the difference between primary and non-primary (reactivation or reinfection) maternal CMV infection in pregnancy, the next investigation step including foetal surveillance and timing of any amniocentesis, and the role and evidence base for any antiviral therapy in pregnancy.
lead_in: Which statement most accurately describes the current Australian approach to maternal CMV infection in this case?

options.t: Non-primary CMV infection carries a higher vertical transmission rate than primary infection, so reassurance is appropriate here
Maternal intravenous ganciclovir is the established standard of care to prevent foetal CMV infection in primary seroconversion
Spiramycin reduces vertical transmission of CMV in the same way that it does for Toxoplasma gondii
CMV is a rare cause of congenital infection and routine foetal surveillance is unnecessary after maternal seroconversion
Serial foetal ultrasound, amniocentesis for CMV PCR after 21 weeks and 6 weeks post-seroconversion, and high-dose valaciclovir following MFM discussion

stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 woman at 24 weeks and 2 days gestation with a background of mild persistent asthma on budesonide-formoterol maintenance (one inhalation twice daily as preventer plus reliever) presents to the emergency department with 12 hours of progressive wheeze, dyspnoea and chest tightness after a viral upper respiratory infection. She is too breathless to speak in full sentences. Her best personal PEFR is 420 L/min; in ED it is measured at 210 L/min (50% of personal best). SpO2 is 93% on room air. Auscultation reveals widespread polyphonic expiratory wheeze with reduced air entry bilaterally and no focal crackles or signs of consolidation. She is afebrile at 37.1 degC, has no calf swelling, no chest pain, the foetus is moving normally and the CTG is reactive with a baseline of 140 bpm. She has stopped her preventer inhaler for the past four days because she was worried about possible effects on the baby and asks whether she should continue inhaled corticosteroids and whether oral prednisolone is safe in pregnancy, given a friend has told her to refuse steroids.
lead_in: Which management approach is most appropriate?

options.t: Continue maintenance inhaled corticosteroid but withhold all systemic corticosteroids in pregnancy due to perceived foetal teratogenic risk
Nebulised salbutamol/ipratropium if severe, continue ICS, oral prednisolone 37.5-50 mg for 5-10 days, plus controlled oxygen
Switch the patient to a leukotriene receptor antagonist (oral montelukast) alone as monotherapy maintenance throughout pregnancy
Intravenous magnesium sulphate 2 g over 20 minutes given as the immediate first-line therapy in pregnancy
Discharge home with a short-acting beta-2 agonist alone once PEFR has reached 60% of personal best

stem: A 27-year-old G1P0 at 7 weeks is referred for Graves disease. She was diagnosed 6 months ago with tremor, weight loss and palpitations; currently on carbimazole 15 mg daily with normal TFTs and TRAb 5.2 IU/L (positive). She has no goitre and no ophthalmopathy. She is asymptomatic. Her endocrinologist asks the obstetric medicine team to advise on antithyroid drug choice, dosing strategy by trimester, and foetal/neonatal surveillance given the positive TRAb.
lead_in: Which management plan is most appropriate?

options.t: Switch to propylthiouracil (PTU) in trimester 1 then back to carbimazole in trimester 2-3; monitor maternal TFTs monthly; arrange foetal growth and goitre surveillance scans and neonatal TFTs/TRAb at birth
Continue carbimazole at current dose throughout pregnancy; foetus is not at risk
Stop antithyroid drugs as pregnancy is naturally immunosuppressive and Graves usually remits
Switch to radioactive iodine ablation now
Switch to PTU throughout pregnancy without switching back

subtopic_detail: Combined first-trimester screen - next-step after high risk
stem: A 36-year-old G2P1 woman attends her general practitioner with the result of her combined first-trimester screen, performed at 12 weeks and 4 days gestation with the recommended ultrasound markers (crown-rump length 62 mm, nuchal translucency 3.2 mm, present nasal bone) and maternal serum PAPP-A and free beta-hCG. The laboratory adjusted risk for trisomy 21 has come back as 1 in 80, with trisomy 18 risk 1 in 950 and trisomy 13 risk 1 in 1100. Her first child is well at age 4 with no chromosomal abnormality. There is no family history of aneuploidy or congenital structural malformations. Her booking bloods are unremarkable. She and her partner have read about NIPT and diagnostic testing online and want to know the most appropriate immediate next step. She is keen to avoid procedure-related miscarriage risk if reasonable. The pregnancy is otherwise progressing well, foetal heart was 162 bpm on a brief ultrasound today and she has no bleeding, pain or symptoms of concern.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in counselling and investigation for this woman?

options.t: Reassure that the cFTS result represents a low-risk pregnancy and proceed to a routine 18 to 20 week morphology scan
Repeat the combined first-trimester screen at 13+6 weeks to see if the risk falls before any further action is taken
Proceed directly to chorionic villus sampling today without further screening, since maternal age above 35 makes diagnostic testing mandatory
Offer NIPT as a contingent screening test, or diagnostic testing (CVS now or amniocentesis from 15 weeks) after counselling about procedure-related miscarriage risk
Arrange amniocentesis today at 12+4 weeks, given the high-risk result and the woman's preference to avoid further screening tests

stem: A 41-year-old G3P2 woman attends a tertiary maternal foetal medicine clinic at 13 weeks and 2 days gestation. She declined combined first-trimester screening and instead self-funded non-invasive prenatal testing at 11 weeks. The result has been telephoned through by her general practitioner this morning as a high-probability positive for trisomy 21, with foetal fraction reported at 8.2% and no comment on sex chromosome or other autosomal findings. Trisomy 18 and 13 results were low risk. Her two previous children were chromosomally normal. She has hypothyroidism on thyroxine 100 micrograms daily, has had a previous lower-segment caesarean for breech, and there is no significant family history of inheritable disease. Her booking bloods were unremarkable. An ultrasound today shows a viable singleton pregnancy with CRL consistent with dates, a nuchal translucency of 2.1 mm, present nasal bone, no other structural concerns, and a normally implanted placenta on the posterior uterine wall. She is distressed and asks the maternal foetal medicine consultant for clear advice on what the NIPT result actually means and what the next step should be.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step for counselling and management?

options.t: Counsel that NIPT has very high positive predictive value at her age and proceed directly to termination of pregnancy if she wishes, without further confirmatory testing
Proceed directly to amniocentesis today at 13+2 weeks for the most accurate confirmatory result
Repeat the NIPT on a fresh maternal sample to confirm the result before any consideration of diagnostic testing
Reassure her because the nuchal translucency today is normal at 2.1 mm and there are no structural anomalies; defer further testing to the 20 week morphology scan
Counsel that NIPT remains a screening test and offer confirmatory diagnostic testing by chorionic villus sampling now, or amniocentesis from 15 weeks, before any irreversible decision is made

subtopic_detail: Diagnostic testing - CVS vs amniocentesis timing and risk
stem: A 33-year-old G2P1 woman is reviewed in a maternal foetal medicine clinic at 11 weeks and 5 days gestation by certain dates and a confirmatory ultrasound. Her combined first-trimester screen returned a risk of 1 in 60 for trisomy 21, with a nuchal translucency of 3.6 mm and PAPP-A of 0.3 MoM. The foetus has a present nasal bone and no structural anomaly is visible on this morning's ultrasound. She has reviewed both contingent NIPT and definitive diagnostic options with her midwife and consultant and has decided that she wants a definitive answer in the shortest time possible because she has finite leave from work and significant anxiety. Her previous child has Down syndrome diagnosed postnatally and she does not want to wait until 15 weeks. The placenta is anterior and easily accessible by transabdominal route. She is RhD positive, has no allergies, and is fit and well. She asks the maternal foetal medicine consultant which diagnostic procedure is most appropriate today and what its principal procedure-related risk is.
lead_in: Which diagnostic procedure is most appropriate today and what is the approximate procedure-related miscarriage risk in an experienced Australian unit?

options.t: Transcervical chorionic villus sampling today, with procedure-related miscarriage risk approximately 2 to 3%
Amniocentesis today at 11+5 weeks, with procedure-related miscarriage risk approximately 0.5 to 1%
Defer diagnostic testing and arrange NIPT today, with procedure-related miscarriage risk of zero
Transabdominal chorionic villus sampling today, with procedure-related miscarriage risk approximately 0.1 to 0.2%
Defer diagnostic testing until 18 to 20 weeks and proceed to morphology scan only, with procedure-related miscarriage risk of zero

subtopic_detail: Anti-D prophylaxis - sensitising event before 12 weeks
stem: A 24-year-old G2P0 woman presents to a regional Australian emergency department with a single episode of moderate per-vaginal bleeding requiring two pads in two hours, associated with mild lower abdominal cramping. Her last menstrual period dates her at 9 weeks and 1 day, and a transvaginal ultrasound today shows a single intrauterine pregnancy with CRL consistent with 8+6 weeks, present foetal cardiac activity at 158 bpm, no retained products in the uterus and a small subchorionic haematoma. There is no cervical motion tenderness, the os is closed and no products of conception are visible. Her booking blood group from the GP last week is documented as O RhD negative with a negative antibody screen, and she had no prior pregnancies carried beyond miscarriage at 6 weeks one year ago. The general practice doctor in ED asks the obstetric registrar whether routine anti-D Rh(D) immunoglobulin is indicated for this episode and at what dose, given Australian National Blood Authority guidance.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate anti-D Rh(D) immunoglobulin management today?

options.t: Administer 625 IU anti-D Rh(D) immunoglobulin IM now, since any bleeding in an RhD-negative woman after 9 weeks is a sensitising event
Administer 1500 IU anti-D Rh(D) immunoglobulin IM now as a single antenatal dose covering both 28 week and 34 week prophylaxis until delivery
Administer 250 IU anti-D Rh(D) immunoglobulin IM now and order a Kleihauer-Betke test today to titrate any further required dose
Anti-D is not indicated today; she continues routine 28 and 34 week prophylaxis of 625 IU IM with 625 IU post-event from 12 weeks onwards
Defer all anti-D decisions until 12 weeks gestation, then administer 625 IU IM only if vaginal bleeding persists or recurs at that time

subtopic_detail: GBS screening - intrapartum prophylaxis with penicillin allergy
stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 woman presents to the birth suite at 38 weeks and 5 days gestation in established labour with regular painful contractions every 3 minutes and intact membranes. Her vaginal-rectal swab taken at 36 weeks for universal group B Streptococcus screening was positive for Streptococcus agalactiae, sensitive to penicillin and reported as resistant to clindamycin and erythromycin. Her chart documents an immediate hypersensitivity reaction (generalised urticaria and tongue swelling without anaphylaxis) following oral amoxicillin in childhood, judged by the allergy team as high-risk for severe penicillin allergy. Her previous baby is well at age 3 with no neonatal sepsis. Vital signs are normal, the baby is cephalic and engaged, foetal heart rate is reassuring on intermittent auscultation, and the obstetric registrar wants to commence intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis. The midwife asks which antibiotic regimen is most appropriate given the patient's penicillin allergy and the documented GBS antibiotic sensitivities.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis regimen?

options.t: IV benzylpenicillin 3 g loading dose then 1.8 g every 4 hours until birth as standard first-line intrapartum prophylaxis
IV cefazolin 2 g loading dose then 1 g every 8 hours until birth as the recommended alternative for low-risk penicillin allergy
IV vancomycin 25 mg/kg (maximum 2 g) loading then 15 mg/kg every 12 hours until birth
IV clindamycin 900 mg every 8 hours until birth, given the documented high-risk penicillin allergy and inability to use beta-lactams
Oral erythromycin 500 mg every 6 hours until birth, given documented penicillin allergy and need to avoid intravenous antibiotic therapy

stem: A 31-year-old G1P0 woman attends her general practice for review of her 75 g oral glucose tolerance test performed at 26 weeks gestation as routine ADIPS-recommended screening. She has no prior diabetes and a body mass index of 28 kg/m2. Her parents both have type 2 diabetes on metformin. She has had no symptoms of polyuria, polydipsia or weight loss. Her morphology scan at 19 weeks was normal, with foetal biometry on the 60th centile. Her booking bloods were unremarkable. The OGTT was performed after an overnight 10-hour fast at a local pathology collection centre with serial venous plasma glucose samples at 0, 60 and 120 minutes after the 75 g glucose load. Her fasting venous plasma glucose was 5.3 mmol/L, the 1-hour value was 9.6 mmol/L and the 2-hour value was 7.9 mmol/L. She has no acute symptoms, blood pressure is 118/72 mmHg and the foetal heart rate today is 142 bpm on bedside auscultation. She asks the general practitioner whether the test indicates gestational diabetes by Australian (ADIPS) criteria.
lead_in: Which best describes the interpretation of this OGTT result against current ADIPS criteria?

options.t: Gestational diabetes is diagnosed because the fasting glucose of 5.3 mmol/L exceeds the ADIPS fasting threshold of 5.1 mmol/L
Gestational diabetes is not diagnosed because no single value reaches the WHO threshold of 7.0 mmol/L fasting or 11.1 mmol/L at 2 hours
Gestational diabetes is not diagnosed because at least two values must meet thresholds under ADIPS criteria
Gestational diabetes is not diagnosed because the 1-hour value of 9.6 mmol/L is below the 10.0 mmol/L threshold; the fasting value of 5.3 is within the normal pregnancy range
The diagnosis should be deferred and HbA1c used to confirm; HbA1c of 5.5% rules out gestational diabetes

stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 woman attends an early pregnancy assessment service with a referral from her general practitioner. She is at 10 weeks and 3 days gestation by certain last menstrual period, confirmed by a dating ultrasound at 7 weeks that showed CRL of 9 mm with present foetal cardiac activity. She has had minimal brown vaginal spotting for the last week but no heavy bleeding or pain, and has noticed that breast tenderness and nausea (previously prominent) have resolved over the past 10 days. She has no fever, no haemodynamic instability and no significant past medical history. Examination shows a soft, non-tender abdomen with a closed cervical os, no active bleeding and no products of conception visible. The transvaginal ultrasound today shows a single intrauterine gestational sac with mean sac diameter 28 mm, a foetal pole with crown-rump length of 12 mm and no foetal cardiac activity demonstrated on careful M-mode assessment by an accredited sonographer. There is no retained tissue, no subchorionic haematoma and the cervix is closed.
lead_in: Which classification best describes this clinical and ultrasound picture?

options.t: Threatened miscarriage - viable intrauterine pregnancy with bleeding and closed cervix
Missed miscarriage - foetal demise diagnosed on ultrasound with retained pregnancy tissue and a closed cervical os
Inevitable miscarriage - bleeding with open cervix and foetal demise on ultrasound
Incomplete miscarriage - retained products of conception following partial expulsion of pregnancy tissue
Complete miscarriage - all products of conception passed with an empty uterus and resolving symptoms

subtopic_detail: Hyperemesis - severity assessment and admission criteria
stem: A 24-year-old G1P0 woman at 10 weeks and 2 days gestation presents to an emergency department with three weeks of progressively worsening nausea and vomiting. Over the last 48 hours she has been unable to keep down oral fluids, has vomited at least eight times per day, and has lost 4 kg from her booking weight of 62 kg (>5%). She is exhausted, intermittently tearful, and has missed two days of work. Her partner reports she has had episodes of postural light-headedness. Examination shows dry mucous membranes, capillary refill 2 to 3 seconds, mild ketotic breath, HR 108 bpm, BP 100/62 mmHg lying and 88/55 mmHg standing, T 36.7 degC. Urinalysis shows ketones 3+, specific gravity 1.030, no nitrites or leucocytes. Her PUQE-24 score today is 14 out of 15 (severe). Bloods reveal sodium 132 mmol/L, potassium 3.1 mmol/L, urea 7.8 mmol/L, creatinine 92 micromol/L. Beta-hCG and TFTs are pending. A transabdominal scan shows a viable singleton pregnancy at 10+2 with no molar features. She has tried oral pyridoxine 25 mg TDS at home with no relief.
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management step?

options.t: Admit for IV normal saline rehydration, IV thiamine before any dextrose, IV potassium replacement, and stepwise antiemetics with serial electrolyte monitoring
Discharge with oral doxylamine-pyridoxine and a metoclopramide PRN script with a written safety-net to return for ongoing vomiting
Admit for total parenteral nutrition via a central venous catheter to bypass the gut entirely while severe vomiting resolves over coming days
Admit for IV dextrose 5% rehydration to provide immediate energy substrate and commence IV ondansetron monotherapy without giving prior thiamine
Arrange urgent termination of pregnancy at 10 weeks as the only definitive treatment for refractory severe vomiting unresponsive to oral therapy

stem: A 22-year-old G1P0 woman presents to her general practitioner at an estimated 11 weeks gestation by uncertain last menstrual period with three weeks of persistent nausea, intermittent vaginal spotting and one episode of passing what she describes as small grape-like tissue. She has no significant medical history. Examination shows a uterus palpable above the symphysis to a size consistent with 14 weeks, soft and non-tender. Blood pressure today is 138/86 mmHg (previously 110/70 at GP visit at 8 weeks). She has dry mucous membranes and reports mild postural symptoms. A transvaginal ultrasound at the local imaging service today shows a heterogeneous intrauterine mass with multiple small cystic spaces (described as 'snowstorm' appearance), no foetal pole, no foetal cardiac activity, and bilateral theca lutein cysts on the ovaries measuring 4 to 5 cm. The serum beta-hCG is 285 000 IU/L. Thyroid function tests show TSH 0.05 mU/L and free T4 22 pmol/L (mildly elevated). She has been referred to the local gynaecology team and her GP wants to confirm the recognition and what the next steps are.
lead_in: Which sequence of management most accurately reflects current Australian (RANZCOG) guidance for this presentation?

options.t: Single-dose IM methotrexate 50 mg/m2 as outpatient with weekly hCG until undetectable, then 6-monthly surveillance for the following 12 months
Suction curettage in theatre with histology, weekly hCG until three negative samples then monthly for 6 months (complete) or 1 month (partial), with reliable contraception
Expectant management with weekly serum beta-hCG only, given that many molar pregnancies will resolve spontaneously without surgical or medical intervention
Total abdominal hysterectomy as primary treatment given the high reported risk of choriocarcinoma associated with this histology
Medical evacuation of the mole with methylergometrine and misoprostol, followed by weekly serum beta-hCG until undetectable for three consecutive weekly samples

stem: A 24-year-old G1P0 at 12 weeks is asymptomatic with a normal dipstick today. The booking MSU MC&S sent a week ago reports pure growth of GROUP B STREPTOCOCCUS 10^8 CFU/L (sensitive to penicillin). No symptoms, no costovertebral tenderness, observations normal, no allergies. The GP asks the obstetric registrar what management this asymptomatic finding warrants.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management plan?

options.t: Treat with oral antibiotics now (penicillin V or amoxicillin) per Therapeutic Guidelines, and arrange intrapartum IV benzylpenicillin GBS prophylaxis at delivery
No treatment as patient is asymptomatic and dipstick is negative
Treat with antibiotics now but no intrapartum prophylaxis required
Repeat MSU in 4 weeks before any treatment to confirm the finding
Commence continuous suppressive nitrofurantoin until delivery

stem: A 28-year-old G2P1 attends her 28-week antenatal visit. Her partner attends every visit, answers questions for her, and asked the midwife last visit to keep him informed of all her results. Today she is quiet, has a fading bruise on her left forearm she attributes to a kitchen accident, and SFH/foetal heart are appropriate. The clinic has a private room, an interpreter line and an on-site social worker. The midwife steps out and asks the GP registrar what to do next; the partner is in the room with her.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step to enable safe domestic violence enquiry?

options.t: Ask the screening questions in front of the partner to demonstrate transparency and gather his perspective
Find a clinical reason to see her alone (e.g. urine sample, listen to baby in another room) and enquire privately using a validated framework such as HARK or RADAR
Defer the enquiry to the postnatal home visit, where the partner is less likely to attend
Avoid direct enquiry today and place a 1800RESPECT leaflet in her hand-held antenatal record for her to see
Contact NSW Police immediately because the forearm bruise constitutes evidence of an assault

subtopic_detail: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander antenatal care - culturally safe model
stem: A 22-year-old G1P0 Aboriginal woman from a regional NSW community books for antenatal care at 11 weeks. She has no medical conditions, BMI 24, supportive partner, lives with her mother and cousins. She tells the booking midwife she felt unsafe at a previous gynaecology appointment because she did not feel listened to and had to bring her own information; she has missed two prior follow-up appointments at other services for the same reason. The hospital offers: standard midwifery group practice (MGP); a local Aboriginal Maternal and Infant Health Service (AMIHS) co-led by an Aboriginal Health Worker and continuity midwife linked with the regional ACCHO; obstetrician-led clinic; GP shared care; and telehealth. All five pathways can access morphology USS, OGTT and specialist obstetric review.
lead_in: Which model of care is most likely to optimise engagement and outcomes for this woman?

options.t: AMIHS continuity-of-carer model integrating midwife and Aboriginal Health Worker, with ACCHO linkage
Standard midwifery group practice with referral to a separate Aboriginal Health Worker as needed
Obstetrician-led care because all Aboriginal pregnancies are inherently high-risk and require specialist supervision
GP shared care with the regional GP to minimise hospital contact
Telehealth-only antenatal care to reduce her travel burden regardless of her preference

subtopic: Indigenous health (Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander)
subtopic_detail: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander maternal health - RHD secondary prophylaxis in pregnancy
stem: A 26-year-old G2P1 Aboriginal woman from a remote NT community presents for booking at 12 weeks. She has a documented history of acute rheumatic fever aged 10 with subsequent moderate mitral regurgitation on echo last year and is on monthly intramuscular benzathine benzylpenicillin (BPG) 1.2 million units secondary prophylaxis through the local clinic. Her last dose was 2 weeks ago. She has had one previous uncomplicated SVD at term with no cardiac decompensation, breastfed for 12 months, and is currently well with no exertional dyspnoea, no orthopnoea, and NYHA class I. Examination: BP 108/64, HR 82, soft pansystolic murmur at apex unchanged from previous, no peripheral oedema, clear chest. Repeat booking echo shows unchanged moderate MR, mild LA dilation, normal LV function, and pulmonary artery pressure 28 mmHg. She asks whether she should continue the monthly injections during pregnancy because a friend told her to stop because the penicillin might harm the baby.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate advice regarding her secondary prophylaxis during pregnancy?

options.t: Continue 4-weekly IM BPG at the standard dose throughout pregnancy and the postpartum period
Switch to oral phenoxymethylpenicillin 250 mg BD for the duration of pregnancy to avoid IM injections
Cease benzathine benzylpenicillin prophylaxis until after delivery because penicillin readily crosses the placenta
Shorten the BPG interval to every 3 weeks because pregnancy increases drug clearance and lowers troughs
Convert to azithromycin 1 g monthly as an alternative because penicillin allergy is more common in pregnancy

subtopic_detail: Termination of pregnancy - Australian state-specific gestational limits
stem: A 24-year-old G2P0 woman in regional Victoria presents at 23+1 weeks by dating ultrasound requesting termination of pregnancy. Morphology ultrasound at 21 weeks showed a foetal cardiac anomaly initially read as a ventricular septal defect, but on tertiary review at 23 weeks was confirmed as hypoplastic left heart syndrome with associated coarctation. She has discussed the diagnosis with paediatric cardiology, including the staged Norwood pathway and its long-term survival and morbidity, and feels unable to proceed with the pregnancy. She has no medical comorbidities, her support network is intact, she has accessed counselling, and her first pregnancy ended in a first-trimester miscarriage. She specifically asks about the legal pathway in Victoria, whether she must travel interstate, and whether an ethics committee or panel needs to approve the procedure before she can be referred to the tertiary service She has no contraindication to mifepristone or prostaglandin induction.
lead_in: Which statement best describes the lawful pathway for termination of pregnancy in Victoria at this gestation?

options.t: Termination is lawful on request without any additional consultation up to 24 weeks gestation in Victoria
Termination is unlawful after 20 weeks in Victoria and the patient must travel interstate to obtain care
Termination is lawful: she is under the 24-week on-request limit and is referred to a tertiary service
Termination requires approval by a formal hospital ethics committee at any gestation in Victoria
Termination is only lawful where the foetal anomaly is incompatible with life as defined by statute

stem: A 27-year-old G1P0 presents at her general practice at 7+2 weeks by transvaginal ultrasound, having confirmed an unintended intrauterine pregnancy. She requests medical termination. She has no medical comorbidities, no allergies, BMI 23, takes no regular medications, has a confirmed intrauterine location with single live embryo CRL consistent with 7+2 weeks, has been screened and consented for medical TOP, and has discussed contraception. The GP is MS-2 Step certified. The patient lives 25 minutes from a hospital with 24-hour emergency services, has a supportive partner who can stay with her for 48 hours, and has clear arrangements for follow-up serum beta-hCG and a 2-week review. She understands the success rate, expected pain and bleeding, warning signs prompting ED return, and the need for confirmatory follow-up. She has read the MS-2 Step consumer information and asks the GP to confirm the exact dosing and interval she should expect.
lead_in: Which medical regimen is correct under the MS-2 Step Australian protocol for this gestation?

options.t: Mifepristone 200 mg oral, followed 36 to 48 hours later by misoprostol 800 microg buccal
Mifepristone 600 mg oral, followed 24 hours later by misoprostol 400 microg oral
Methotrexate 50 mg/m2 IM, followed 7 days later by misoprostol 800 microg vaginal
Misoprostol 800 microg vaginal alone, repeated every 3 hours up to 3 doses
Mifepristone 200 mg oral, followed immediately by misoprostol 800 microg buccal in the same visit

stem: A 30-year-old G3P2 woman presents to ED 10 days after a medical TOP at 8 weeks using mifepristone-misoprostol from her GP. The procedure was reported as successful with passage of products on day 1 and tapering bleeding for 6 days. Over the last 36 hours she has had heavy bleeding soaking 1 large pad per hour, lower abdominal cramping, low-grade fever (37.8 deg C), foul-smelling discharge, and feels lightheaded on standing. She has no significant past history, takes no medications, blood group O Rh positive, and breastfed her two prior children. On arrival: BP 96/58, HR 116, T 37.9, RR 18, SpO2 99%. Abdomen tender suprapubically with guarding but no peritonism. Speculum: open external os with brisk bleeding and brown malodorous discharge, products visible at the os. Bedside USS: heterogeneous endometrial collection 38 mm with vascularity on colour Doppler and no free fluid in the pouch of Douglas. Bloods: Hb 88 g/L, WCC 16.4 x 10^9/L, CRP 142 mg/L, lactate 1.6, group-and-hold sent.
lead_in: What is the single best next step in management?

options.t: Outpatient repeat misoprostol 800 microg vaginal plus doxycycline 100 mg BD for 14 days, review in 48 hours
Resuscitate with IV access, fluids, group-and-hold, and broad-spectrum IV antibiotics, then proceed to urgent surgical evacuation of retained products
Admit for IV oxytocin infusion with close observation, deferring evacuation pending blood culture results
Tranexamic acid 1 g IV with observation and serial Hb without antibiotic cover until culture results
Intramuscular methotrexate 50 mg/m2 for presumed ongoing intrauterine pregnancy and discharge home to GP

subtopic_detail: Domestic violence - mandatory reporting of child exposure
stem: A 34-year-old G4P3 woman in NSW discloses ongoing intimate partner violence during her postnatal home visit at day 14. There have been two physical assault episodes in the past 2 weeks, both witnessed by her 5- and 8-year-old children. The youngest child has begun bedwetting and the older child has been refusing school. The newborn is feeding well and gaining weight; there are no injuries on the infant. She is asking that nothing be reported to child protection because she fears the four children will be removed from her care. She has agreed to a safety plan, accepted a warm referral to a specialist DV service, and confirmed she is safe to remain at her sister's house tonight. The midwife is a mandated reporter under the NSW Children and Young Persons (Care and Protection) Act 1998, and exposure of children to intimate partner violence meets the threshold of Risk of Significant Harm under the NSW Mandatory Reporter Guide.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step regarding child protection reporting?

options.t: Honour the woman's request not to report because reporting would breach therapeutic trust and engagement
Report only the children's behavioural change to the GP and do not lodge any child protection report
Lodge a Risk of Significant Harm report to the NSW Child Protection Helpline and continue the safety plan
Contact NSW Police directly to seek an Apprehended Domestic Violence Order on behalf of the woman
Wait until the next clinic visit in a fortnight to allow the woman more time to consider

subtopic_detail: Domestic violence - cycle and safety planning
stem: A 26-year-old woman disclosed intimate partner violence 4 weeks ago and you drew up a written safety plan (code words, packed bag at her sister's, 1800RESPECT number). Today she reports her partner has been kind, brought flowers, and agreed to attend couples counselling; she is reconsidering whether she still needs the plan. No acute injuries. The previous incident involved choking, which she had not initially disclosed. She is calm, oriented, has no children, joint rental, independent income, family interstate. She asks whether the change means the cycle of abuse is over and whether joint counselling would now be safe.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate response?

options.t: Validate her experience, explain the reconciliation/honeymoon phase of the cycle, advise against joint couples counselling (which is unsafe in active IPV with prior strangulation), reinforce the safety plan and offer ongoing review
Reassure her that the apology phase usually means violence will not recur and discard the safety plan
Insist she leave the relationship today as the only safe option
Recommend joint couples counselling because the partner has agreed to attend
Discharge her from clinical follow-up because she now feels safe

subtopic_detail: Sexual assault - drug-facilitated and toxicology evidence
stem: A 19-year-old woman presents to ED 18 hours after a night out, with no memory of several hours of the evening, having woken in an unfamiliar bed beside someone she did not know, with disarranged clothing. She is concerned that her drink was spiked. She last urinated on waking 1 hour ago and has had three sips of water since. She has had no alcohol or illicit drugs to her knowledge other than two drinks she ordered and saw poured. She has no known medical conditions and takes no regular medications. She is alert, oriented, distressed but cooperative. The local sexual assault medical service is contactable and a forensic medical examiner is available within the hour. She has not decided whether to report to police. The forensic examiner will offer toxicology and asks which sample to collect first given the long window since exposure and the short half-lives of common drug-facilitated sexual assault agents such as GHB and benzodiazepines.
lead_in: Which sample provides the highest yield for detection of common drug-facilitated sexual assault agents at this presentation?

options.t: A clotted serum sample taken now for benzodiazepine and GHB quantification analysis
First-catch urine sample now and a repeat urine sample at the next void
Scalp hair sample taken now for later segmental analysis after a 4-week growth window
Gastric aspirate via nasogastric tube to recover any residual ingested drug from the stomach
Oral mucosal swab to recover any drug residues from saliva and the buccal mucosa surface

stem: A 33-year-old G1P1 is reviewed at 6 weeks postpartum following a left lower-limb deep vein thrombosis diagnosed 2 weeks ago and now on therapeutic warfarin (target INR 2 to 3) after initial enoxaparin. She is bottle feeding by choice (lactation suppressed), normotensive, BMI 28, non-smoker, with no history of migraine and no other medical history. She asks for the most reliable contraception she can use. She has decided she does not want further children at this stage but is not ready to consider permanent sterilisation. She is happy with all reversible options, including LARCs, and asks the GP to confirm which methods are unsafe given the recent DVT, particularly the combined hormonal options that her sister uses. She has read about UKMEC categories online and wants to understand which method she should avoid She is on warfarin for at least 3 more months and may extend to 6 months.
lead_in: Which contraceptive option is contraindicated (UKMEC category 4) in this woman?

options.t: Levonorgestrel 52 mg intrauterine device (Mirena) inserted at this 6-week review
Etonogestrel 68 mg subdermal implant (Implanon NXT) inserted in the inner upper arm
Progestogen-only pill (norethisterone 350 microg) taken daily at the same time
Combined hormonal contraception (COCP, vaginal ring, or transdermal patch) at any dose
Copper intrauterine device inserted as a non-hormonal LARC at this review

stem: A 30-year-old G1P1 woman with well-controlled HIV (CD4 720, viral load <50 copies/mL on tenofovir alafenamide-emtricitabine-bictegravir) is reviewed at 6 weeks postpartum. She is formula feeding per current Australian guidance, an option chosen after counselling once viral suppression was sustained, and the infant has completed post-exposure prophylaxis per ASHM. She is normotensive, BMI 25, non-smoker, no migraine, no VTE history, and no other comorbidity. She wants reliable contraception for at least 2 years, preferably long-acting and not requiring daily adherence. Her HIV physician confirms she will remain on bictegravir-based therapy and is not on any enzyme-inducing antiretroviral. She specifically asks whether the implant will still work on her HIV regimen and whether she needs efavirenz to be added for the implant to be effective Her infectious-diseases physician has cleared any contraceptive method Her HIV physician noted no enzyme-inducing antiretrovirals on her regimen.
lead_in: Which contraceptive option is most appropriate without significant interaction concerns?

options.t: Etonogestrel 68 mg subdermal implant (Implanon NXT) inserted in the inner upper arm
Combined oral contraceptive pill in the immediate postpartum period because she is not breastfeeding
Etonogestrel subdermal implant only if efavirenz is added to her antiretroviral regimen
Avoid all hormonal methods because of HIV-related immune effects on contraceptive efficacy
Permanent surgical sterilisation as the only safe contraceptive option in women with HIV

subtopic: Indigenous health (Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander)
subtopic_detail: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander maternal health - hyperglycaemia in pregnancy
stem: A 32-year-old G3P2 Aboriginal woman from a regional town presents at 11 weeks for booking with her local ACCHO. Her two previous pregnancies were complicated by gestational diabetes managed with diet and metformin, and both babies were macrosomic (birth weights 4.3 kg and 4.5 kg with shoulder dystocia in the second). Between pregnancies she lost touch with primary care and has not had her glucose checked. Her BMI is 32 and BP is 122/76. There is a strong family history of type 2 diabetes (mother, two siblings). At booking, the GP arranges fasting venous plasma glucose and HbA1c as part of the early antenatal panel recommended by ADIPS and RANZCOG for high-risk women. Results: fasting venous plasma glucose 6.4 mmol/L, HbA1c 6.7% (50 mmol/mol), urine ketones negative. She is otherwise well, on folic acid and iodine, and asks whether she has GDM or something different.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate interpretation and next step?

options.t: Reassure that values are normal in pregnancy and re-screen with 75 g OGTT at 24-28 weeks
Diagnose diabetes in pregnancy, commence lifestyle measures and metformin, refer to multidisciplinary clinic
Diagnose gestational diabetes on the FPG threshold of >=5.1 and commence dietary advice only
Repeat the fasting plasma glucose in 4 weeks before initiating any pharmacological intervention
Commence insulin glargine immediately as monotherapy without any metformin or lifestyle intervention being trialled

stem: A 25-year-old G1P0 woman in Perth, Western Australia presents at 24+2 weeks gestation by ultrasound requesting termination of pregnancy. She has no medical comorbidities and no foetal anomaly. The pregnancy is unwanted but she had previously been undecided. She is certain of her decision now and asks her GP to refer her. The GP knows the new WA Public Health Amendment (Abortion) Act 2023 was enacted in early 2024 with different rules above and below 23 weeks.
lead_in: Which statement most accurately reflects the legal pathway for her TOP in WA?

options.t: Lawful on request up to 23 weeks with one medical practitioner; at her current gestation (24+2) she needs assessment and agreement by two medical practitioners, at least one being an obstetrician/gynaecologist or in consultation with one
Unlawful in WA at this gestation under any circumstances; must travel interstate
Partner consent is required before the procedure
A mandatory 7-day waiting period applies from first consultation to procedure
Must be performed surgically; medical TOP is not available in WA

subtopic_detail: DV - DVO/AVO interaction with antenatal care planning
stem: A 27-year-old G2P1 woman is 34 weeks pregnant and has an active Apprehended Domestic Violence Order in NSW against her former partner, who is the father of the unborn child. The order prohibits him contacting her or approaching within 100 metres of her residence and workplace. She presents to the maternity day-assessment unit for routine review and is anxious because her former partner has been ringing the hospital switchboard asking for her appointment times and 'information about his child'. She is fully engaged in antenatal care, has a written safety plan, is supported by a hospital social worker, and has a planned hospital birth. She asks specifically whether the ADVO applies inside the hospital, whether her former partner has any right to be told her appointment times because he is the father, and what the hospital can do to ensure he cannot find out when she will be in the building.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next action with respect to her hospital information access and safety?

options.t: Provide the former partner with appointment times because he has a paternal right to information about the unborn child
Flag the medical record as confidential, instruct switchboard and reception not to release any information, and notify security
Transfer her care to a different hospital to break the trail without involving her in the decision
Tell the partner the ADVO does not apply to the hospital so he may attend appointments
Restrict her from public maternity care until the ADVO is formally upgraded by the court

stem: A 31-year-old G3P2 woman undergoes elective surgical TOP at 13 weeks by dilation and evacuation in a day-procedure unit. The procedure itself is uneventful but in recovery 30 minutes later she is found to be soaking the pad with bright red blood. She has no significant past medical history, is on no anticoagulation, has no known bleeding diathesis, BMI 26, and no prior caesarean section. On urgent review: BP 88/52, HR 122, RR 22, SpO2 99%, T 36.7, alert but pale and clammy. Abdominal palpation reveals a soft, poorly contracted, palpable uterus midway between the umbilicus and the symphysis pubis. Speculum: brisk red bleeding from a parous cervical os, no obvious cervical laceration, no visible retained products at the os. Estimated blood loss is already 800 mL. The major haemorrhage protocol is being activated, two large-bore IV cannulae are in situ, and a group-and-hold has been sent. The on-call gynaecologist is en route; pharmacy has misoprostol, ergometrine and carboprost available The anaesthetic team has secured the airway adequately for now.
lead_in: What is the single best initial uterotonic intervention while ongoing resuscitation continues?

options.t: IV oxytocin 5 IU slow bolus followed by oxytocin infusion 40 IU in 1 L saline
Misoprostol 800 microg per rectum as the first-line uterotonic, given before any IV oxytocin
Tranexamic acid 1 g IV as the sole haemorrhage agent, withholding any uterotonic for now
Carboprost 250 microg intramuscularly as the first-line uterotonic, given before any IV oxytocin
Methylergometrine 250 microg intramuscularly as the first-line uterotonic, given before any IV oxytocin

subtopic_detail: Heavy menstrual bleeding - investigation in a perimenopausal woman
stem: A 49-year-old G3P3 presents with a 9-month history of heavy menstrual bleeding. Cycles have shortened from 28 to 22 days, last 8 to 10 days, and she changes a fully soaked pad every 90 minutes on the worst two days with passage of 5-cent-piece-sized clots. She reports two episodes of intermenstrual bleeding in the last 3 months. Her BMI is 34, she has type 2 diabetes on metformin, and she has never been on hormonal contraception. There is no postcoital bleeding, no dyspareunia, and her last cervical screen 18 months ago was HPV negative. She is not sexually active currently and uses no contraception. On examination her abdomen is soft with no palpable mass; speculum shows a healthy cervix and no cervical lesion; bimanual reveals a bulky but mobile uterus, no adnexal mass. Pregnancy test is negative. FBC shows Hb 98 g/L with MCV 74 fL and ferritin 6 microg/L. TSH and coagulation screen are normal.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.t: Commence tranexamic acid 1 g tds during menses and review
Repeat the cervical screening test with HPV partial genotyping today
Pelvic MRI to characterise the bulky uterus before further management
Transvaginal ultrasound and outpatient endometrial biopsy at this visit
Outpatient hysteroscopy without prior endometrial sampling or pelvic imaging

subtopic_detail: Primary amenorrhoea with absent breast development - workup pathway
stem: A 16-year-old presents with primary amenorrhoea. She has never had a menstrual period. Her height is 148 cm (below 3rd centile), weight 48 kg, BMI 22. On Tanner staging her breast development is Tanner 1 and pubic hair is Tanner 3. She has a wide carrying angle at the elbows, a short fourth metacarpal, and a low posterior hairline. There is no anosmia and no visual field defect. She reports no cyclical pelvic pain, no abdominal mass, and no headaches. Her past medical history includes recurrent otitis media in childhood requiring grommets and a known bicuspid aortic valve picked up incidentally at age 8 on a school murmur referral. She has no sisters; her mother went through menarche at 13 and is 165 cm tall. There is no family history of consanguinity, autoimmune disease, or premature ovarian insufficiency. On examination her external genitalia are prepubertal Tanner stage; the vaginal opening is patent and a normal-appearing cervix is visible on careful inspection. Pelvic ultrasound shows a small infantile uterus and streak ovaries bilaterally with no follicular activity. A renal ultrasound performed in childhood was normal.
lead_in: Which is the single most useful next investigation to confirm the underlying diagnosis?

options.t: Karyotype to exclude Turner syndrome and structural abnormalities
Serum prolactin to exclude a pituitary cause
Pelvic MRI to characterise the uterus and adnexa
GnRH stimulation test to localise the axis defect
Olfactory testing for anosmia to identify Kallmann syndrome

subtopic_detail: Secondary amenorrhoea in an athlete - hypothalamic differential
stem: A 24-year-old marathon runner presents with 11 months of amenorrhoea. Her menarche was at 13 and cycles were regular at 28 days until she increased training to 90 km per week 18 months ago in preparation for an ultramarathon. Her BMI has dropped from 22 to 18.5 in the last 12 months and she reports restricting carbohydrates and skipping meals on training days. She works as a primary school teacher and has noticed fatigue, low mood and cold intolerance for the last 4 months. She has no galactorrhoea, no hirsutism, no acne, no hot flushes, no vaginal dryness, no headaches, no visual disturbance, no recent dilatation and curettage or termination of pregnancy, and no history of pelvic infection. She has had no live births and uses condoms with her long-term male partner. A pregnancy test is negative. Her FSH is 2.8 IU/L, LH 1.6 IU/L, oestradiol 110 pmol/L, prolactin 280 mIU/L, TSH 1.8 mIU/L, AMH 22 pmol/L, total testosterone 0.6 nmol/L. A bone density scan shows a Z-score of -2.3 at the lumbar spine and -1.8 at the femoral neck.
lead_in: Which is the most likely diagnosis?

options.t: Polycystic ovary syndrome with anovulation
Functional hypothalamic amenorrhoea from stress
Premature ovarian insufficiency before age forty
Hyperprolactinaemia from a microprolactinoma adenoma
Asherman syndrome from prior uterine instrumentation

stem: A 34-year-old woman presents with her fifth episode of vulvovaginal candidiasis in the past 12 months. Each episode features intense vulval pruritus, dyspareunia, thick white curd-like discharge, and erythematous vulval skin, and resolves with a single oral fluconazole 150 mg dose. Between episodes she is asymptomatic. She is in a stable monogamous relationship for 5 years, uses copper IUD for contraception, has no history of diabetes, and a recent HbA1c is 5.4% (35 mmol/mol). She does not use scented soaps, wears cotton underwear, and has not used antibiotics in the last 6 months. She has no immunosuppression. Today on speculum examination there is thick white adherent discharge with vulval erythema and excoriation. Vaginal pH is 4.2. KOH microscopy shows numerous pseudohyphae and budding yeast. A high vaginal swab for fungal culture and speciation is collected and confirms Candida albicans sensitive to fluconazole.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate ongoing management?

options.t: Single dose fluconazole 150 mg orally and reassure this is an isolated acute episode
Induction fluconazole 150 mg orally days 1, 4, 7, then suppressive fluconazole 150 mg weekly for 6 months
Empirical oral metronidazole 400 mg twice daily for a 7-day course without further investigation
Refer her for an oral glucose tolerance test before any further antifungal therapy
Long-term intravaginal boric acid 600 mg nightly indefinitely as continuous suppressive antifungal therapy regimen

subtopic_detail: Urinary incontinence - stress vs urge distinction and initial management
stem: A 55-year-old G3P3 presents with 18 months of urinary leakage. She leaks small volumes when she coughs, laughs, sneezes, or lifts her grandchildren, has no urgency, no nocturia, and no leakage on the way to the toilet. She empties her bladder 6 times a day, never wakes overnight to void, and does not feel incomplete emptying. There is no haematuria, no dysuria, no recurrent UTI, and no faecal incontinence. She had three vaginal births with two episiotomies and a forceps delivery for her second child. Her last period was 14 months ago. BMI 31. She smokes 10 cigarettes/day. On examination, abdomen soft, no palpable bladder. On speculum a first-degree anterior wall prolapse is noted. A cough stress test in semi-recumbent and standing positions reproduces leakage of urine from the urethral meatus. Urinalysis is clear and post-void residual on bladder scan is 35 mL.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line management?

options.t: Refer for a mid-urethral sling (transobturator tape) surgical procedure now
Commence oral oxybutynin 5 mg twice daily as first-line monotherapy for symptoms
Three-day bladder diary, pelvic floor training for 3 months, weight loss and smoking cessation
Insert a ring pessary for the management of stress urinary incontinence
Urodynamic studies as the first step before any conservative therapy is trialled

subtopic_detail: Pelvic organ prolapse - conservative management of symptomatic stage 2 cystocele
stem: A 68-year-old G4P4 woman presents with 6 months of a vaginal bulge worse at end of day. She also has bothersome stress urinary incontinence on coughing/sneezing and needs to splint to void fully. No faecal incontinence, no dyspareunia. Sexually active with husband, BMI 27, hypertension on perindopril, no other comorbidities. POP-Q: Ba 0, C -2, Bp -3 (stage 2 anterior compartment). Post-void residual 80 mL. Urodynamic studies confirm urodynamic stress incontinence with no detrusor overactivity and good bladder capacity. She wants the option with the best evidence for both prolapse and urinary symptoms; she is prepared to consider surgery but would also consider a pessary.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line management?

options.t: Trial vaginal ring pessary and supervised pelvic floor physiotherapy, with reassessment of urinary symptoms after prolapse reduction
Anterior colporrhaphy with concomitant retropubic midurethral sling
Sacrocolpopexy with synthetic mesh under general anaesthesia
Indwelling urinary catheter to manage the elevated post-void residual long-term
Topical oestrogen as monotherapy with no other intervention

stem: A 29-year-old G1P1 presents for preconception counselling. Her first child was born 18 months ago at 39 weeks via uncomplicated vaginal birth weighing 3450 g. She takes sodium valproate 1000 mg daily for generalised tonic-clonic epilepsy (last seizure 4 years ago) and is co-managed by neurology. She is otherwise well. She has no personal or family history of neural tube defects, no diabetes, no malabsorption, and a BMI of 24. She is up to date with her cervical screening (HPV negative 8 months ago) and her booking bloods from her last pregnancy showed she was rubella immune and varicella immune. She smokes 5 cigarettes a day, drinks 4 standard drinks twice a week on weekends, and does not use any other supplements. She would like to start trying for a second child in 3 months and asks what she should do to prepare.
lead_in: Which is the most important pre-conception intervention?

options.t: Folic acid 0.5 mg orally daily for 3 months pre-conception and the first trimester only
Iodine 150 microg daily plus vitamin D level testing only without any medication change overall
Reassure her that no change to current AED therapy is needed because she is presently seizure-free
Administer MMR and varicella booster immunisations now before any pregnancy attempt is made by this patient
Refer to neurology to switch to a safer anti-seizure medication and prescribe folic acid 5 mg daily

subtopic_detail: Infertility workup in a couple under 35
stem: A 30-year-old G0 and her 32-year-old male partner present after 13 months of trying to conceive with regular unprotected intercourse 2 to 3 times per week, including timed intercourse around predicted ovulation in the last 4 cycles. She has regular 29-day cycles with predictable ovulatory symptoms (mid-cycle mucus change and mittelschmerz). She has no dysmenorrhoea, no dyspareunia, no pelvic surgery, no PID, no STI history, and was up to date with cervical screening 6 months ago (HPV negative). BMI 24, non-smoker, no alcohol, no caffeine excess. She takes folic acid 0.5 mg daily and iodine 150 microg daily. Her partner is well, BMI 26, non-smoker, no known fertility issues, no previous children, no varicocele symptoms, no testicular trauma, no mumps orchitis, no inguinal surgery, no cryptorchidism, no chronic illness, no anabolic steroid use, takes no medications, drinks 6 standard drinks per week, and his work as an electrician does not involve heat exposure. She is concerned and asks what investigations should be done now.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial set of investigations?

options.t: Semen analysis, day-21 progesterone, TSH and transvaginal ultrasound; reserve HSG for after initial workup
Refer immediately for IVF without any further investigation of the couple
Continue trying for another 12 months before any investigation of the couple
Hysterosalpingogram and diagnostic laparoscopy as the first-line investigations of the couple
AMH for ovarian reserve only and reassure her she can wait given young age

stem: A 42-year-old G2P2 woman presents with 4 months of postcoital bleeding, small-volume bright red, after most episodes of intercourse. No intermenstrual bleeding, no abnormal discharge, no pelvic pain. Cycles regular, COCP for 5 years. Her last cervical screening test 9 months ago: HPV non-16/18 detected with reflex LBC showing pLSIL; she did not attend her recommended 12-month repeat. On speculum today: a 1.5 cm fungating lesion on the anterior cervical lip that bleeds on contact, with surrounding pale tissue. No cervical motion tenderness, no adnexal mass on bimanual examination.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Urgent referral to gynaecological oncology for biopsy of the cervical lesion
Repeat the cervical screening test in clinic today
Stop the COCP and review in 3 months as it may be the cause of bleeding
Take chlamydia and gonorrhoea NAAT swabs and treat empirically while awaiting results
Reassure and arrange clinical review in 6 months

stem: A 42-year-old Aboriginal woman attends an ACCHO. Her last Pap smear was 9 years ago (reported normal). She declines a clinician-collected speculum, citing childhood sexual trauma and discomfort with previous examinations. She has no postcoital bleeding, no intermenstrual bleeding, no abnormal discharge, and no pelvic pain. She is G3P3 (all vaginal), in a monogamous relationship, no immunosuppression, BMI 27. She received Gardasil as an adolescent via catch-up. The clinic is accredited to offer self-collection. She wants to participate in screening in a way she can tolerate. There is no visible lesion at external genital examination (which she has agreed to).
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Offer a self-collected vaginal HPV swab today as her routine cervical screening test
Refer to gynaecology for examination under anaesthesia and cervical sampling
Decline screening today and rebook for clinician-collected sample at a later visit
Send a self-collected urine sample for HPV testing in lieu of vaginal sampling
Reassure her that prior Gardasil vaccination removes the need for screening

subtopic_detail: NCSP - non-16/18 oncogenic HPV with pLSIL reflex cytology
stem: A 38-year-old G2P2 returns for her cervical screening result: HPV oncogenic non-16/18 detected; reflex liquid-based cytology shows possible HIGH-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (pHSIL). She is asymptomatic; cervix appeared normal at the time of the sample. She is in a monogamous relationship of 12 years, last screened 7 years ago (normal Pap), non-smoker, BMI 24, no immunosuppression. She received Gardasil as a teenager. She uses the LNG-IUS. She is anxious and asks specifically whether 'non-16/18' means she should just have another screen in 12 months rather than colposcopy.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Refer directly to colposcopy now
Repeat clinician-collected HPV test in 12 months from today
Repeat HPV test in 6 months as an interim safety check
Return to routine 5-yearly screening as cytology is described as only 'possible'
Refer to colposcopy at 6 months if she develops symptoms, otherwise routine screening

subtopic_detail: HPV vaccination - Australian schedule (Gardasil 9, single-dose schedule from 2023)
stem: A 14-year-old female adolescent attends a GP clinic with her mother for a general check after recently arriving in Australia from overseas. She has not received any HPV vaccination previously. She has no allergies, takes no regular medications, and is otherwise well, with menarche at age 12 and regular cycles. She has never been sexually active. Routine childhood vaccinations were completed in her country of origin but did not include HPV. She is up to date with school enrolment. She has no immunosuppression, no organ transplant, and is not on long-term corticosteroids. Her mother specifically asks about the HPV vaccine schedule because she has read conflicting information online about whether one or two doses are required. The GP confirms the patient is generally well, has had no recent febrile illness, and is up to date with all other Australian National Immunisation Program vaccines. She is keen to receive the vaccine today if it is offered for free.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate HPV vaccination plan for this patient under the National Immunisation Program?

options.t: Three doses of Gardasil 9 at 0, 2 and 6 months because she is starting late
Two doses of Gardasil 9 at 0 and 6 months because she is over 14
Single dose of Gardasil 9 today, funded under the National Immunisation Program
Defer HPV vaccination until age 18 when sexual debut is more likely
Single dose of bivalent Cervarix today as she is over 14 years old

subtopic_detail: Symptomatic cervical cancer - postcoital bleeding workup overrides routine screening
stem: A 35-year-old G1P1 woman attends her GP with a 4-month history of intermittent postcoital bleeding occurring after most episodes of intercourse, with one episode of intermenstrual bleeding lasting 2 days. She has had no abnormal discharge, no dyspareunia and no pelvic pain. Her last cervical screening test was 18 months ago and showed HPV not detected on a clinician-collected sample. She received Gardasil as a teenager and is otherwise well. She is a current smoker (15 pack-years) and uses the combined oral contraceptive pill. She has one child after an uncomplicated vaginal delivery. There is no significant family history. She has been treated empirically by another clinician for presumed chlamydial cervicitis with azithromycin 1 g stat 6 weeks ago, with chlamydia and gonorrhoea NAAT both negative; symptoms have persisted unchanged. BMI is 24. She has no fevers, no weight loss, no urinary symptoms and no bowel symptoms. She is asking for a 'repeat HPV swab to make sure'.
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.t: Repeat the routine NCSP HPV cervical screening test and act on the result
Empirical treatment for cervicitis with doxycycline 100 mg BD for 7 days
Reassure patient that symptoms will likely resolve and review in 6 months
Perform a co-test (HPV + LBC) and refer for colposcopy regardless of result
Discontinue the combined oral contraceptive pill as likely cause, review 3 months

subtopic_detail: Advanced cervical cancer - recognising locally invasive symptoms
stem: A 52-year-old woman who has never participated in cervical screening attends ED with a 6-week history of intermittent vaginal bleeding (now soaking through several pads daily), foul-smelling watery discharge, deep right-sided pelvic and lower-back pain, new right leg swelling extending to the thigh, and frank haematuria for the past week. She has lost 6 kg unintentionally over 3 months and feels exhausted. She is a former heavy smoker (30 pack-years, quit 2 years ago), G3P3 with all vaginal deliveries, and has had three lifetime partners. She received no HPV vaccination. She has no significant past medical history. She emigrated to Australia from a country without an organised cervical screening program at age 30 and has had no screening since. She takes no regular medications and has no allergies. Bowels are regular and there is no rectal bleeding. She is otherwise afebrile.
lead_in: Which combination of clinical features most strongly suggests locally advanced cervical cancer with parametrial / pelvic sidewall involvement?

options.t: Heavy menstrual bleeding with iron-deficiency anaemia and a bulky uterus
Postcoital bleeding with a small, mobile cervix and normal renal function
Unilateral lower-limb oedema, haematuria, rising creatinine and a fixed parametrial mass
Postmenopausal bleeding with an endometrial thickness of 5 mm on TVUS
Bilateral lower abdominal pain with adnexal tenderness, pyrexia and elevated WCC

subtopic_detail: Postmenopausal bleeding - TVUS endometrial thickness >4 mm threshold
stem: A 64-year-old G3P3 woman attends her GP with two episodes of fresh vaginal bleeding over the past 3 weeks. She experienced natural menopause at age 51 with no further bleeding until now. She has no postcoital bleeding, no discharge and no pelvic pain. Her last cervical screening test was 4 years ago, HPV not detected. She has type 2 diabetes (metformin 1 g BD, last HbA1c 7.4%), essential hypertension on perindopril 5 mg daily, BMI 34, and a 25-year history of regular menses prior to menopause. She has never used hormone therapy or tamoxifen. Family history is unremarkable for gynaecological malignancy and Lynch syndrome screening criteria are not met. She does not smoke. She walks 3 km daily and has no urinary or bowel red flags. She has had no recent intercourse and is not on anticoagulation. She is not anaemic on point-of-care testing. She is anxious because her neighbour was recently diagnosed with endometrial cancer.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.t: Refer for outpatient hysteroscopy with directed and Pipelle endometrial biopsy
Trial topical vaginal oestrogen for presumed atrophic vaginitis and review in 6 weeks
Reassure as a single episode and review in 3 months if symptoms persist
Order a serum CA-125 to risk-stratify endometrial pathology before further imaging
Repeat the transvaginal ultrasound in 6 weeks to assess for any change

subtopic_detail: Endometrial hyperplasia with atypia - management in postmenopausal woman
stem: A 58-year-old G2P2 postmenopausal woman is referred to gynaecology after 4 weeks of intermittent vaginal bleeding. TVUS demonstrated an endometrial thickness of 12 mm with a heterogeneous appearance and no focal mass. Outpatient hysteroscopy with directed and Pipelle biopsies reports endometrial hyperplasia with atypia (also called endometrial intraepithelial neoplasia, EIN); no invasive disease seen on the available tissue. She has type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 7.6%, metformin 1 g BD), hypertension treated with perindopril 5 mg daily, BMI 38, and natural menopause at age 53. She has no family history of Lynch-associated cancers. She has completed her family. She has no contraindications to surgery, ASA 2, and is keen to 'sort this out properly'. She does not wish to continue hormonal therapy long term. She has no thromboembolic history and a normal cardiac assessment on preoperative review. She asks specifically about the risk that this finding already represents cancer.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate definitive management?

options.t: Total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy as definitive surgical management
Levonorgestrel intrauterine system with 6-monthly endometrial sampling for surveillance
Oral medroxyprogesterone acetate with repeat biopsy in 12 months
Endometrial ablation to control bleeding and reduce cancer risk
Watchful waiting with repeat TVUS and endometrial biopsy in 6 months

subtopic_detail: Ovarian cancer recognition - vague abdominal symptoms + CA-125
stem: A 62-year-old G2P2 postmenopausal woman attends her GP with a 3-month history of progressive abdominal bloating, early satiety, mild lower abdominal discomfort, increased urinary frequency without dysuria, and unintentional 4 kg weight loss. She initially thought she had developed IBS. There is no rectal bleeding, no change in bowel habit beyond mild constipation, and no postmenopausal bleeding. She has no significant past medical history, no family history of ovarian, breast or bowel cancer, and is not known to carry BRCA1/2. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol minimally. She has had no abdominal surgery. Her last cervical screening test was 2 years ago, HPV not detected. She has not used hormone therapy. Examination reveals mild abdominal distension and a vaguely palpable left adnexal fullness. There is no overt ascites but a low-volume effect cannot be excluded clinically. She is afebrile, with stable observations. The GP has already started her on a fibre supplement which has not relieved symptoms.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.t: Colonoscopy as the initial investigation for presumed bowel pathology and constipation
Serum HE4 alone as a more specific tumour marker than CA-125
Trial of antispasmodic and follow up if symptoms persist beyond 6 weeks
CT abdomen and pelvis with IV contrast as first-line imaging investigation
Pelvic transvaginal and transabdominal ultrasound with serum CA-125 for RMI/IOTA risk stratification

subtopic_detail: Test of cure after LLETZ for HSIL
stem: A 34-year-old G0 woman had a LLETZ procedure 11 months ago for biopsy-proven HSIL (CIN3) detected after a screening HPV test reported HPV 16 with possible HSIL on reflex cytology.
