The LLETZ histology confirmed CIN3 with clear excision margins (ectocervical, endocervical and deep margins all clear of dysplasia, no glandular involvement, no microinvasion). Post-LLETZ recovery was uncomplicated with no secondary haemorrhage and no cervical stenosis on subsequent review. She is asymptomatic, with no postcoital or intermenstrual bleeding and no discharge. She is in a monogamous relationship of 2 years and is using the etonogestrel implant for contraception. She is a non-smoker, BMI 24, has no immunosuppression, takes no regular medications and has no family history of cervical or other gynaecological malignancy. She received Gardasil as a teenager and completed her course. She attends her GP today asking what surveillance she now needs and when she can return to routine 5-yearly NCSP screening.
lead_in: Which surveillance pathway should she follow under the Australian NCSP?

options.t: Annual HPV cervical screening for 5 years, then return to routine 5-yearly screening
Single co-test at 24 months and return to routine 5-yearly screening if negative
Return to routine 5-yearly NCSP screening now because excision margins were clear
6-monthly colposcopy for 2 years regardless of cytology or HPV result
Co-test at 12 and 24 months; both negative returns to 5-yearly screening

subtopic_detail: Heavy menstrual bleeding - structured PALM-COEIN workup
stem: A 44-year-old G2P2 woman attends her GP with an 8-month history of progressively heavier menstrual bleeding. Cycles are regular at 28 days but bleeding now lasts 9 days with flooding through super tampons hourly on days 2-3 and passage of large clots up to 3 cm in size. She reports increasing tiredness, palpitations on minor exertion, and exertional shortness of breath climbing one flight of stairs. Her work as a primary school teacher has been disrupted and she has had to change pads during teaching sessions. She is not currently sexually active, has had no postcoital bleeding, no intermenstrual bleeding, and no postcoital spotting. There is no abdominal or pelvic pain. There is no easy bruising, no gum bleeding, no epistaxis, no family bleeding history and her last cervical screening test was negative for oncogenic HPV 18 months ago. BMI is 34. She has hypertension treated with perindopril 5 mg daily and takes no anticoagulants, no antiplatelets and no over-the-counter NSAIDs. She has had two normal vaginal deliveries with no postpartum haemorrhage. She has not yet had pelvic imaging or endometrial sampling in this presentation.
lead_in: In addition to a transvaginal ultrasound and full blood count with ferritin, which further investigation is most important at this visit?

options.t: FSH and oestradiol to assess for perimenopausal anovulation as cause
Coagulation screen and von Willebrand panel as the first-line bleeding work-up
Endometrial Pipelle biopsy at this visit, with TSH and coagulation screen as adjuncts
Hysteroscopy with directed biopsy as the sole investigation in place of TVUS today
Serum CA-125 to screen for occult ovarian malignancy in this patient

subtopic_detail: Uterine fibroids - choice of medical therapy
stem: A 39-year-old G1P1 woman has a 2-year history of heavy regular menstrual bleeding with cycle-related bulk symptoms including urinary frequency, mild lower-abdominal pressure, and occasional nocturia. She passes large clots during menses and floods through super tampons every 1-2 hours on the heaviest days. Pelvic ultrasound shows a 4 cm intramural fibroid (FIGO type 4, not distorting the cavity) and a 3 cm subserosal fibroid; the endometrial cavity is regular and the endometrium is 6 mm. Endometrial biopsy returns benign proliferative endometrium with no atypia. She has completed her family, is in a monogamous relationship using condoms for contraception, and would prefer to avoid surgery if possible. She does not want to lose her fertility surgically and is open to a long-acting hormonal device. She has tried tranexamic acid 1 g three times daily during menses with only modest improvement and has not tolerated mefenamic acid. BMI 26. She has no contraindications to oestrogen or progestogen, no migraines and no history of VTE.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line medical therapy?

options.t: GnRH agonist (e.g. goserelin 3.6 mg SC monthly) with add-back oestrogen
Levonorgestrel 52 mg intrauterine system (Mirena) insertion now
Tranexamic acid 1 g orally three times daily during menses only
Mefenamic acid 500 mg orally three times daily during menses only
Total abdominal hysterectomy with bilateral salpingectomy under GA

stem: A 27-year-old nulligravid woman presents with 4 years of cyclical pelvic pain (peaks day 1-2, deep dyspareunia, dyschezia during menses, cyclical back pain). She has tried regular ibuprofen and heat-pack regimen with minimal benefit. She is not planning conception in the next 18-24 months but does want children eventually. She has no migraine with aura, no smoking, no VTE history, BMI 24. Pelvic USS normal (no endometrioma). Bimanual examination shows tender uterosacral nodularity. She is now keen for the most evidence-based next step that does not commit her to surgery if it can be avoided.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Trial continuous combined oral contraceptive pill with NSAID for breakthrough pain, with review at 3-6 months and laparoscopy if refractory or fertility planning advances
Diagnostic laparoscopy with peritoneal biopsy before any medical therapy
Serum CA-125 to support the working diagnosis
MRI pelvis as the next investigation to confirm the working diagnosis
GnRH agonist with add-back HRT as first-line therapy

subtopic_detail: Premature ovarian insufficiency - diagnosis and HRT
stem: A 33-year-old nulligravid woman attends her GP with 6 months of secondary amenorrhoea following a 12-month history of progressively irregular cycles. She reports new hot flushes (around 6-8 per day), troublesome night sweats with disturbed sleep, vaginal dryness with deep dyspareunia, low mood, reduced libido and increased fatigue. She is in a stable long-term relationship and is open to future fertility consideration, although they have not actively been trying to conceive. There is no recent significant weight change, no excessive exercise, no medication changes, no eating disorder behaviours, no galactorrhoea, no headaches or visual field symptoms. She has had no chemotherapy, no pelvic radiotherapy and no previous ovarian surgery. She had menarche at age 12 with previously regular 28-day cycles. Family history: her mother had menopause at age 41 and her maternal aunt had menopause in her early forties; one cousin has Hashimoto's thyroiditis. BMI 23. She is not using contraception currently. Beta-hCG is negative today. Initial bloods 5 weeks ago: FSH 56 IU/L, oestradiol low, prolactin normal, TSH normal, total testosterone normal. Today's repeat FSH and oestradiol have been collected and are pending.
lead_in: If today's repeat FSH is similarly elevated (>25 IU/L), which is the most appropriate management?

options.t: Reassure and recheck FSH in 6 months as a single elevated FSH is non-diagnostic alone
Commence the combined oral contraceptive pill solely for menopausal symptom control without further workup needed
Bone density scan and fertility counselling referral, but defer hormonal therapy until age 40 years
Refer to endocrinology for an adrenal failure work-up before initiating any hormonal therapy in her
Systemic HRT, fertility counselling, screen for adrenal/thyroid antibodies and karyotype, plus baseline bone densitometry

stem: A 22-year-old nulligravid woman presents to ED with sudden severe right iliac fossa pain that began 90 minutes ago while she was walking briskly home. The pain is constant, 9/10 in severity, sharp, with intermittent stabbing exacerbations, and is associated with two episodes of bilious vomiting and one episode of diarrhoea-free loose stool. She is sexually active with her regular partner of 18 months, uses condoms consistently, and her last menstrual period began 14 days ago and was normal in flow and duration. Her cycles are regular at 28 days and she has no abnormal vaginal bleeding, no postcoital bleeding and no abnormal discharge. There is no fever, no rigors, no dysuria, no frequency, no haematuria, no diarrhoea, no recent travel and no recent change in bowel habit. She had a similar but milder episode three months ago that resolved spontaneously after 4 hours; at that time she did not present for medical review. She has no significant past medical history, no previous abdominal surgery, no allergies, and takes no regular medications.
lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.t: Ruptured ectopic pregnancy with haemoperitoneum and shock
Ovarian torsion of a right ovarian cyst
Acute pelvic inflammatory disease with tubo-ovarian inflammation
Ruptured haemorrhagic ovarian cyst with pelvic pain
Acute appendicitis with right iliac fossa peritonism

stem: A 21-year-old sexually active woman presents with 4 days of lower abdominal pain, deep dyspareunia, mucopurulent vaginal discharge, fever 38.4 C, HR 110, BP 100/64. Examination: bilateral lower-quadrant tenderness, cervical motion tenderness, mucopurulent endocervical discharge, AND a 6 cm tender right adnexal mass with bedside USS demonstrating a multilocular cystic mass with internal debris consistent with tubo-ovarian abscess. She is unable to tolerate oral intake due to nausea and vomiting. Urine beta-hCG negative. WCC 18, CRP 180. NAAT swabs sent. She has no allergies.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management?

options.t: Admit and commence IV ceftriaxone, doxycycline (or azithromycin if intolerant) and metronidazole, with surgical/interventional drainage if no improvement at 48-72 hours
Outpatient ceftriaxone 500 mg IM + oral doxycycline + oral metronidazole for 14 days
Single-dose IM ceftriaxone and discharge home with safety-net advice
Proceed immediately to laparoscopy and salpingectomy as the primary intervention
Oral amoxicillin monotherapy for 14 days

subtopic_detail: Contraception - migraine with aura (UKMEC 4 for COCP)
stem: A 28-year-old nulligravid woman attends her GP for contraceptive counselling. She has been using condoms only since her last relationship ended and would now like a more reliable method as she is in a new monogamous relationship of 9 months. She has a 6-year history of migraine with aura: a visual scotoma with shimmering edge develops in one visual field for 20-30 minutes preceding a moderate-to-severe unilateral throbbing headache that lasts up to 24 hours. These attacks occur 1-2 times per month and are usually associated with sleep deprivation or menstruation. She has been reviewed by a neurologist who confirmed the diagnosis of migraine with aura. She is otherwise well, non-smoker, BMI 24, blood pressure 118/76 mmHg, and has no personal or family history of VTE, ischaemic heart disease or stroke. She has had no episodes of focal neurology between migraine attacks. She has not had children and is open to long-acting reversible methods. She would prefer a method she does not need to remember daily. She has no allergies and takes sumatriptan 50 mg as needed for acute migraine attacks; she is not on any prophylactic agent.
lead_in: Which contraceptive method is contraindicated in this patient?

options.t: Levonorgestrel 52 mg intrauterine system (Mirena) for 5 years
Etonogestrel subdermal implant (Implanon NXT) for 3 years
Copper intrauterine device for up to 10 years
Combined oral contraceptive pill (ethinyloestradiol 30 mcg with levonorgestrel 150 mcg)
Progestogen-only desogestrel 75 mcg pill taken once daily continuously

subtopic_detail: Postnatal contraception in a breastfeeding woman
stem: A 31-year-old G2P2 woman attends her 6-week postnatal check with her local GP. She had an uncomplicated spontaneous vaginal birth of a healthy term infant 6 weeks ago and is exclusively breastfeeding on demand, day and night, with no formula supplementation and no expressed-milk bottle feeds. The baby is feeding 8-12 times in 24 hours and weight gain is on track. She has not yet had a return of menses. She wishes to space pregnancies for at least 2 years and would prefer the most reliable method that is compatible with breastfeeding. She has no history of VTE, no thrombophilia, no migraines and has a BMI of 25. She does not smoke. She has not used the lactational amenorrhoea method as her sole strategy and is concerned about pregnancy.
lead_in: Regarding contraception, which statement is most accurate?

options.t: The combined oral contraceptive pill is the first-line choice as BP has normalised and gestational hypertension has resolved
Lactational amenorrhoea method (LAM) is acceptable as a sole contraceptive method indefinitely while she remains amenorrhoeic
Levonorgestrel intrauterine system or copper IUD can be inserted today, and etonogestrel implant or progestogen-only pill are also suitable
Etonogestrel implant should be delayed until 6 months postpartum because of an adverse effect on milk supply
Depot medroxyprogesterone acetate is contraindicated during breastfeeding and should not be used at all here

subtopic_detail: Cervical screening - oncogenic non-16/18 HPV management
stem: A 38-year-old woman has her routine 5-yearly cervical screening test through her general practice. She is not pregnant, has had two previous CSTs at ages 28 and 33 which were both negative for oncogenic HPV, and has had no abnormal vaginal bleeding, no postcoital bleeding, no intermenstrual bleeding and no pelvic pain. She is in a stable monogamous relationship of 11 years and uses the LNG-IUS 52 mg (Mirena, inserted 4 years ago for both contraception and HMB control) and is currently amenorrhoeic. She has had a total of 4 lifetime sexual partners and has never had a sexually transmitted infection identified. She received the quadrivalent HPV vaccine series at age 14 through the school-based program. Her practitioner-collected sample returns: oncogenic HPV detected (non-16/18 type); reflex liquid-based cytology performed automatically by the laboratory shows no intraepithelial lesion or malignancy. She is immunocompetent and has no history of cervical intraepithelial neoplasia or cervical excisional treatment. She does not smoke.
lead_in: Under the Australian National Cervical Screening Program, what is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Direct referral for colposcopy with cervical biopsy now
Repeat HPV testing in 12 months (intermediate-risk pathway)
Return to routine 5-yearly cervical screening immediately
Repeat HPV test for partial genotype in 6 months
Refer for excisional treatment (LLETZ) without further investigation

subtopic_detail: Postmenopausal bleeding - TVUS endometrial thickness threshold
stem: A 62-year-old G3P3 woman presents to her general practice with a 6-week history of intermittent painless vaginal bleeding. She has been menopausal since age 51 (last menstrual period at 51) and has had no episodes of bleeding in the intervening 11 years until now. The current bleeding is light to moderate, requires a pad most days, and has been ongoing intermittently for the last 6 weeks. There is no associated abdominal or pelvic pain, no urinary symptoms, no bowel change and no weight loss. She has never been on menopausal hormone therapy, has never used tamoxifen, and has no family history of endometrial, ovarian, breast or bowel cancer (specifically no features of Lynch syndrome on family history). Her BMI is 33, she has type 2 diabetes managed with metformin 1 g twice daily (HbA1c 6.6%), and her hypertension is well controlled on perindopril 5 mg daily. She has had three previous spontaneous vaginal births, none complicated, and no postpartum haemorrhage. Her last cervical screening test was negative for oncogenic HPV 18 months ago. She is on no anticoagulants and no antiplatelets. A transvaginal ultrasound is being arranged today.
lead_in: Regarding TVUS endometrial thickness in this clinical setting, which statement is most accurate?

options.t: If TVUS endometrial thickness is <=2 mm then endometrial sampling is mandatory regardless of other factors
A TVUS endometrial thickness of 3 mm essentially excludes endometrial cancer and no further investigation is needed even if recurrent bleeding
Tamoxifen use makes TVUS thickness measurement unreliable due to subepithelial cysts; saline sonohysterography or hysteroscopy is preferred
She should proceed directly to hysteroscopy without prior TVUS, given her constellation of risk factors
Endometrial thickness >=4 mm on TVUS warrants endometrial sampling (Pipelle), with hysteroscopy if focal lesions or non-diagnostic sampling

subtopic_detail: Failure to progress in active first stage - oxytocin augmentation
stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 at 40+1 in spontaneous labour, admitted 6 hours ago at 5 cm, is re-examined and is now 6 cm at +0 station in direct OP position with mild caput and no moulding. Membranes ruptured spontaneously 4 hours ago with clear liquor. Contractions: palpate 3 in 10 lasting 40 seconds, mild intensity. CTG normal. Hb 124. Her mother is reminding the registrar that her own labour had to be 'started with the drip' and asks them to do the same.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Commence IV oxytocin infusion starting at 1-2 mU/min, titrated every 30 minutes to achieve 4-5 contractions per 10 minutes
Perform ARM and reassess in 4 hours before considering oxytocin
Proceed to category 2 caesarean section for failure to progress
Commence IV oxytocin infusion starting at 6 mU/min with no titration interval shorter than 60 minutes
Site an epidural and re-examine her in 6 hours without commencing any augmentation now

subtopic_detail: Shoulder dystocia - third-line manoeuvre after McRoberts and suprapubic pressure
stem: A 33-year-old G2P1 at 40+5 with a previous unassisted vaginal birth (3.6 kg, no shoulder dystocia documented) is now in second stage. She has gestational diabetes diagnosed at 26 weeks and managed on metformin 1 g twice daily with a last HbA1c of 5.6% and recent fasting BGLs in target range. Her booking BMI was 31. The estimated foetal weight at the 38-week growth scan was 4.1 kg with a normal amniotic fluid index. After 35 minutes of active pushing the head delivers with a turtle sign immediately recognised on the perineum. The midwife immediately calls for help, places the mother in McRoberts position with her hips hyperflexed and applies suprapubic pressure from the side of the foetal back for two consecutive contractions. The anterior shoulder remains impacted behind the symphysis pubis. The obstetric registrar arrives within one minute and the multidisciplinary team is now organised at the bedside, including neonatal staff. The mother has an epidural in situ with adequate block, the bladder is empty after recent in/out catheter, no episiotomy has been performed yet, and there is no PV bleeding.
lead_in: After McRoberts and suprapubic pressure have failed, what is the most appropriate next manoeuvre?

options.t: Perform Zavanelli manoeuvre with cephalic replacement and proceed to immediate emergency caesarean section in theatre
Perform internal rotation (Rubin II or Woods screw) or delivery of the posterior arm, with episiotomy if access is inadequate
Apply firm fundal pressure during the next contraction to dislodge the impacted anterior shoulder now
Deliberately fracture the foetal clavicle by direct outward pressure on the mid-clavicle bone immediately now
Cut a generous mediolateral episiotomy alone and ask the mother to push harder with the next contraction

subtopic_detail: Cord prolapse - immediate management at full dilatation
stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 at 38+2 in spontaneous labour at 10 cm dilatation has just had a spontaneous rupture of membranes with a large gush of clear liquor. The midwife performing a vaginal examination immediately feels a pulsating loop of cord in the vagina at +0 station. The foetal head is at -2 station and direct OA, with 3/5 of the head still palpable abdominally. The CTG was previously reassuring (baseline 140, variability 10, accelerations present, no decelerations) but now shows a prolonged deceleration to 70 bpm starting within the last minute. The mother is comfortable on her right side with an epidural in situ; her observations are BP 122/78, HR 88, T 36.9, SpO2 99%. She is otherwise well, has no obstetric or medical comorbidities, and her 36-week growth scan was unremarkable. The emergency buzzer has been pulled and the obstetric, anaesthetic and neonatal teams are en route. She has had no previous abdominal surgery, no caesarean section, and is otherwise low risk for surgical complications.
lead_in: Which immediate set of actions is most appropriate?

options.t: Reduce the cord back into the uterus through the cervix and proceed to instrumental vaginal delivery in the room
Continue oxytocin augmentation, reposition the mother to left lateral and reassess CTG again in 5 minutes
Administer terbutaline 250 microg subcutaneously and proceed immediately to assisted vaginal birth using a ventouse now
Manually elevate the presenting part off the cord, knee-chest or exaggerated Sims, fill bladder 500 mL if delay, category 1 caesarean
Wait for the next contraction at full dilatation and perform a fundal pressure delivery to expedite vaginal birth

subtopic_detail: Instrumental birth - forceps vs ventouse selection
stem: A 24-year-old G1P0 at 40+0 with an epidural is in active second stage. She has been pushing for 110 minutes without descent of the presenting part beyond +1 station. The foetal head is at +1, direct OA, with no caput or moulding palpable, and 0/5 of the head is palpable abdominally on Crichton assessment. The maternal pelvis was clinically adequate on assessment by both registrar and consultant. The CTG shows a baseline of 145 with variability 5-10 bpm, no accelerations and variable decelerations with most contractions but no late or prolonged decelerations and no sinusoidal pattern. Membranes are ruptured with clear liquor of normal volume. Contractions are 4 in 10 with oxytocin running at 16 mU/min. The mother is exhausted, asking for help to deliver, and consented antenatally to instrumental birth in the room with the obstetric registrar present. The bladder is empty after recent catheter in/out. Adequate epidural top-up has been given and the block is dense to ice. The neonatal team has been notified.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate instrument and approach for this delivery?

options.t: Kielland's rotational forceps for a deep transverse arrest under regional anaesthesia
Low-cavity non-rotational outlet forceps (Neville-Barnes or Simpson) under regional anaesthesia
Sequential ventouse then forceps if the initial vacuum extraction attempt fails to descend
Kiwi OmniCup soft ventouse with traction synchronous with maternal contractions only and pulls
Proceed directly to a category 2 caesarean section now without attempted instrumental delivery

stem: A 30-year-old G2P1 at 39+4 having a trial of labour after caesarean (one previous LSCS for breech) is in active labour at 7 cm with epidural. The CTG develops a sudden prolonged deceleration to 60 bpm, not recovering after 4 minutes despite stopping oxytocin, left lateral, 500 mL crystalloid and exclusion of hypotension. Fresh PV bleeding develops, scar tenderness, and the previously palpable head becomes ballotable abdominally (suspected uterine rupture). The registrar calls a category 1 caesarean section.
lead_in: Which combination most accurately describes the standards for category 1 caesarean and the immediate priorities?

options.t: Decision-to-delivery interval target 30 minutes; concurrent MTP activation, theatre transfer, anaesthetic preparation; do not delay for re-examination
Decision-to-delivery 15 minutes; defer MTP activation until intraoperative blood loss is confirmed
Decision-to-delivery 60 minutes; obtain confirmatory bedside ultrasound before transfer to theatre
Decision-to-delivery 75 minutes if epidural is already sited and theatre is in-house
Decision-to-delivery 45 minutes; abandon TOLAC pathway only after delivery is achieved

stem: A 32-year-old G3P2 presents at 32+1 weeks with a 30-minute history of moderate fresh vaginal bleeding. She had spontaneous rupture of membranes 20 minutes before the bleeding started, with a sudden gush of clear liquor followed almost immediately by bright red blood. She has not had abdominal pain at any point and reports no abdominal trauma in the preceding week. Her two previous births were both lower-segment caesarean sections (the most recent 18 months ago and the first 4 years ago), both for failure to progress in the first stage; she has not been counselled about VBAC this pregnancy and the antenatal plan was for an elective repeat CS at 39 weeks. Her 20-week morphology scan showed a low-lying anterior placenta (3 cm from the os) and a velamentous cord insertion with a vessel coursing close to the internal os, but a repeat 32-week transvaginal scan to characterise the cord vessel and re-measure placental edge was scheduled for next week. On arrival she is mildly tachycardic at 102 with a normal BP of 124/76. The CTG shows a sinusoidal pattern with a baseline of 145 bpm and reduced variability. The uterus is soft and non-tender between contractions and there is no maternal pain on speculum examination.
lead_in: What is the most likely cause of the antepartum haemorrhage in this woman?

options.t: Placenta praevia with marginal sinus bleeding from the lower segment
Placental abruption secondary to undisclosed cocaine use or trauma
Vasa praevia with ruptured foetal vessel at the internal os
Uterine rupture along the previous lower-segment caesarean scar from prior delivery
Heavy bloody show from established early preterm labour onset now

subtopic_detail: Postpartum haemorrhage - second-line uterotonic after oxytocin
stem: A 32-year-old G3P3 has just had a spontaneous vaginal birth at 40+2 of a 4.0 kg neonate after a 9-hour first stage and a 45-minute second stage with oxytocin augmentation for slow progress in the first stage. Active management of the third stage with IM oxytocin 10 units has been given and the placenta is delivered intact 8 minutes after the baby. There is now ongoing brisk PV bleeding estimated at 1200 mL over 10 minutes with a soft, boggy uterus that is poorly contracted at the umbilicus. The bladder has been catheterised and is empty. Bimanual compression has been commenced. IV oxytocin 40 units in 1 L Hartmann's at 250 mL/h is already running through a 16G cannula. Her observations are BP 96/58, HR 118, T 36.7. She has a history of asthma (mild, intermittent, no recent exacerbations) but no hypertensive disorder of pregnancy and no other medical comorbidities. Blood has been sent for FBC, coagulation, fibrinogen and group and crossmatch.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next pharmacological step in addition to ongoing IV oxytocin infusion?

options.t: PR misoprostol 800 microg with antiemetic precaution and continue the IV oxytocin infusion at current rate
IM carboprost 250 microg, repeated every 15 minutes to a maximum of 8 doses in total
IM ergometrine 250 microg (no hypertension contraindication) plus IV tranexamic acid 1 g over 10 minutes
Increase IV oxytocin infusion to 60 units in 1 L Hartmann's at 500 mL/h and observe response for 15 minutes
Insert an intrauterine balloon tamponade with 500 mL warmed saline immediately and withhold further uterotonics

subtopic_detail: Twin delivery - second twin with non-cephalic lie
stem: A 31-year-old G2P1 with a dichorionic diamniotic twin pregnancy at 37+3 has just had a spontaneous vaginal birth of Twin 1 (cephalic, 2.6 kg) with intact membranes of the second sac. She has an epidural in situ. After delivery of Twin 1 her uterus relaxes and bedside ultrasound now shows Twin 2 in a transverse lie with the back inferior and the membranes still intact. The estimated foetal weight of Twin 2 at the 36-week scan was 2.8 kg. The CTG of Twin 2 is reassuring with a baseline of 140 bpm, variability 10-15, accelerations present, no decelerations. The cervix remains fully dilated. The obstetric consultant is in theatre attached to the room, and the anaesthetist confirms a working epidural with a dense block. There is no PV bleeding and the uterus is currently soft. The team has been briefed and the mother consented antenatally to internal manoeuvres and breech extraction for a non-cephalic second twin.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate management of Twin 2 at this point?

options.t: External cephalic version under tocolysis followed by ARM when cephalic and longitudinal
ARM immediately to allow the head to descend by gravity
Immediate emergency caesarean section for delivery of the second twin
Wait 60 minutes for spontaneous version before any intervention
Internal podalic version with breech extraction performed under continued epidural anaesthesia

stem: A 29-year-old G2P1 at 39+0 was admitted in spontaneous labour 22 hours ago with intact membranes; she had a SROM 20 hours ago and is now at 7 cm with oxytocin augmentation for slow progress. She has just developed a temperature of 38.4 degC sustained on two readings 30 minutes apart, with a maternal tachycardia of 122 bpm and a foetal tachycardia on CTG of 175 bpm with reduced variability. She reports a mild uterine ache between contractions and the liquor on examination is now offensive and meconium-stained (Grade 2). She has no productive cough, no dysuria, no headache and no diarrhoea. Her GBS swab at 36 weeks was negative. She has no penicillin allergy. Her observations are BP 102/64, HR 122, RR 22, T 38.4. The decision is made to commence empirical intrapartum antibiotics for suspected chorioamnionitis after IV access, two sets of peripheral blood cultures, a high vaginal swab and a midstream urine sample are obtained.
lead_in: Which empirical antibiotic regimen is most appropriate per Australian Therapeutic Guidelines?

options.t: IV benzylpenicillin 1.8 g 4-hourly alone as monotherapy until delivery
IV piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g 8-hourly as broad-spectrum monotherapy until delivery
IV ceftriaxone 2 g daily plus IV metronidazole 500 mg 12-hourly
IV ampicillin 2 g 6-hourly plus IV gentamicin 5 mg/kg daily
IV vancomycin 25 mg/kg loading dose plus IV gentamicin 5 mg/kg daily

subtopic_detail: Failed induction - unfavourable cervix after prostaglandins
stem: A 33-year-old G1P0 at 41+5 is undergoing induction of labour for prolonged pregnancy. She has received two doses of dinoprostone 2 mg vaginal gel 6 hours apart, the second 8 hours ago. Cervix today: 1 cm long, posterior, firm, 1 cm dilated, head at -3 (Bishop score 3). No regular contractions. CTG reassuring, membranes intact. EFW 3.4 kg, no contraindications to mechanical methods, no previous caesarean, no antepartum haemorrhage, no foetal compromise. Local protocol permits maximum 2 doses of dinoprostone gel in 24 hours and prefers mechanical ripening when prostaglandins fail. She has no allergies.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Insert a transcervical Foley catheter (16 Fr) with balloon inflated to 30-60 mL for up to 24 hours for mechanical cervical ripening
Administer a third dose of dinoprostone 2 mg vaginal gel today
Perform artificial rupture of membranes and commence IV oxytocin infusion
Perform a membrane sweep at the bedside and reassess in 24 hours
Proceed directly to category 4 elective caesarean section for failed induction of labour

stem: A 38-year-old G5P4 has had a postpartum haemorrhage after spontaneous vaginal birth of a 4.2 kg neonate following oxytocin augmentation. Despite 40 IU oxytocin infusion, IM ergometrine, three doses IM carboprost, IV tranexamic acid 1 g, and a Bakri balloon at 500 mL, she continues to bleed. Total EBL 3500 mL. BP 88/52, HR 134, peripheries cool. Fibrinogen 1.4 g/L, platelets 68. MTP active, packed cells/FFP/platelets running. She has completed her family. Consultant obstetrician is at the bedside, gynae-oncology is in-house, interventional radiology is on site, anaesthetic team is in theatre.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Proceed to laparotomy with uterine-sparing measures first (B-Lynch compression suture +/- stepwise uterine artery ligation), escalating to peripartum hysterectomy if bleeding persists
Proceed directly to peripartum hysterectomy without trialling uterine-sparing surgery
Insert a second balloon alongside the Bakri and arrange transfer to interventional radiology for embolisation
Re-inflate Bakri to 750 mL and observe for 30 minutes before further intervention
Bilateral internal iliac artery ligation as the first surgical step

subtopic_detail: Normal labour - duration of second stage in nulliparous woman with epidural
stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 at 39+5 in spontaneous labour has been at full dilatation with a working epidural for 2 hours. She has been pushing actively for 90 minutes. The foetal head is at 0 station in occiput transverse with moderate caput and 1+ moulding. CTG: baseline 145, variability 5-10, no accelerations, occasional early decelerations. Contractions 3 in 10 lasting 45 seconds, palpate moderate. Liquor clear. EFW 3500 g, gynaecoid pelvis. She is exhausted but cooperative.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Continue active pushing for a further hour as delay is defined only after 3 hours in nulliparous women with epidural
Obstetric review for assessment of arrest of descent with consideration of rotational instrumental or caesarean
Immediate Kielland's rotational forceps in the room without senior review
Commence oxytocin augmentation and continue pushing for another 2 hours
Stop pushing and observe passively for spontaneous descent for the next 4 hours

subtopic_detail: Bishop score and selection of induction agent in an unfavourable cervix
stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 at 41+2 with an uncomplicated singleton pregnancy attends the day assessment unit for induction of labour for post-dates. Her previous birth was a spontaneous vaginal delivery at 39+4 weeks with a normal third stage and no perineal trauma beyond a small first-degree tear. She has no medical comorbidities, no allergies, no uterine surgery and no previous caesarean. The foetus is cephalic with a reassuring CTG today, an estimated foetal weight on the 60th centile at her 36-week scan and normal umbilical and middle cerebral artery Dopplers at that time. Liquor volume is normal. Membranes are intact and there has been no PV bleeding, no PV loss and no offensive odour. On vaginal examination the cervix is 1 cm dilated, 1 cm long, firm in consistency, posterior in position, and the presenting part is at -3 station, giving a Bishop score of 3. She has not had any contractions and reports normal foetal movements. She is keen to maximise her chance of vaginal birth and to remain as ambulant as possible, and she asks about the safest first agent to use to begin induction at her cervical state.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial induction method?

options.t: Artificial rupture of membranes followed by an IV oxytocin infusion
Mechanical cervical ripening with a transcervical Foley balloon catheter as priming
Oxytocin infusion commenced at 2 mU/min and titrated to contractions
Misoprostol 200 microg oral every 2 hours until contractions established
Stretch and sweep at the bedside and discharge home for review in 48 hours

subtopic_detail: Indications for induction of labour - post-dates threshold in Australian practice
stem: A 39-year-old G2P1 at 39+0 with a BMI of 34 attends antenatal clinic. EDD has been confirmed by an 8-week scan. The foetus is cephalic, growth on the 50th centile, normal AFI and Dopplers, reassuring CTG today. Bishop score 4 (cervix 1 cm, 30%, firm, posterior, station -2). Booking BP 118/74, today 122/76 with no proteinuria. She has gestational diabetes diet-controlled with normal home glucose monitoring. Her previous birth was a spontaneous vaginal delivery at 40 weeks with no complications. She is anxious about a long pregnancy but feels well. She asks about timing of induction.
lead_in: Which evidence-based threshold best supports a recommendation to induce labour for this woman?

options.t: Maternal age >=35 alone independently justifies induction at 39 weeks regardless of other factors
Diet-controlled GDM with appropriate growth supports induction at 39-40+6 weeks to reduce stillbirth risk
BMI 34 alone is an evidence-based indication for induction before 39+0 weeks
Bishop score >=8 is required before induction can be offered at any gestation
Induction at 39+0 reduces caesarean section rates compared with expectant management to 41+0

subtopic_detail: CTG terminology - distinguishing variable from late decelerations
stem: A 30-year-old G2P1 at 40+1 in spontaneous labour at 6 cm has a continuous CTG sited for thick meconium-stained liquor at ARM 90 minutes ago. She has no medical comorbidities, the foetus is appropriately grown with normal Dopplers, and oxytocin has not been used. The foetus is cephalic, head at -1 station, direct OA. Contractions are 4 in 10 lasting 50 seconds, palpating moderate with normal resting tone. Maternal observations are stable. Over the last 30 minutes the CTG shows a baseline of 135 bpm, variability of 10 to 15 bpm, occasional accelerations and recurrent decelerations occurring with most contractions. The decelerations have a sharp drop from the baseline beginning at the start of the contraction, reach a nadir of 90 bpm within 20 seconds, last around 40 seconds, often have a small acceleration immediately before the drop and a small overshoot before returning to baseline, and recover by the end of the contraction. There are no decelerations between contractions.
lead_in: Which type of deceleration is most consistent with these features and what is the underlying mechanism?

options.t: Early decelerations from foetal head compression
Late decelerations from uteroplacental insufficiency
Typical variable decelerations from umbilical cord compression
Atypical variable decelerations indicating foetal hypoxia
Prolonged decelerations from acute foetal compromise

subtopic_detail: Management of recurrent late decelerations with intrauterine resuscitation
stem: A 26-year-old G1P0 at 39+6 is in spontaneous labour, currently 7 cm dilated, with oxytocin augmentation at 8 mU/min for slow progress between 5 and 7 cm. Contractions are 5 in 10 lasting 60 seconds, palpate strong with no tachysystole. She has an epidural with good analgesia. Maternal BP 110/68, HR 88, T 36.8. Liquor clear, ARM 4 hours ago. The 36-week scan was unremarkable and GBS swab was negative. The CTG over the last 25 minutes shows a baseline of 150 bpm, variability of 5 to 8 bpm, no accelerations, and recurrent decelerations occurring with more than 50% of contractions. The decelerations begin gradually after the peak of each contraction, reach a nadir 60 seconds after the contraction peak and recover slowly after the contraction ends. There has been no prolonged deceleration and no vaginal bleeding. The obstetric registrar is at the bedside and theatre is available within 15 minutes.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate next step?

options.t: Continue oxytocin at current rate and repeat assessment in 30 minutes
Proceed immediately to a category 1 caesarean section in theatre now
Increase oxytocin to expedite delivery before further foetal compromise worsens further now
Stop oxytocin, left lateral, 500 mL IV crystalloid, exclude reversible causes, consider foetal scalp lactate
Administer terbutaline 250 microg subcutaneously for acute uterine tocolysis in this setting now

subtopic_detail: Management of a tight nuchal cord at delivery of the head
stem: A 31-year-old G2P1 at 40+0 is in the active second stage at full dilatation following a 25-minute push, with an epidural providing good analgesia. The previous birth was an uncomplicated SVD. The foetus is cephalic with normal growth, and the GBS swab was negative. The CTG shortly before crowning showed a baseline of 140 bpm, variability 10, and mild variable decelerations recovering well. Liquor is clear. The foetal head crowns and is delivered slowly with perineal support. A cord is palpated wrapped tightly around the neck. An attempt to slip the cord over the head fails because it is too tight and the loop cannot be loosened either way. The shoulders have not yet delivered and the head is in restitution to LOT. No shoulder dystocia is recognised and the woman is pushing effectively with the next contraction expected within 30 to 60 seconds. The midwife asks the obstetric registrar what to do.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Double-clamp and divide the umbilical cord immediately before delivery of the foetal shoulders begins
Apply firm traction to the foetal head to deliver the shoulders quickly before the cord tightens further
Ask the woman to stop pushing now and prepare for an emergency caesarean section
Cut the cord between two clamps only when shoulder dystocia is clearly diagnosed clinically
Perform somersault manoeuvre, keep the head close to maternal thigh as the body delivers, then unwind the cord

stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 at 40+2 is at full dilatation, head at +3 station, direct OA, distending the perineum. CTG has developed recurrent late decelerations with reduced variability for 12 minutes. The consultant has decided to perform a non-rotational Neville-Barnes forceps delivery in the room. Bladder empty, epidural topped up, consent obtained, paediatric team notified. EFW 3500 g, pelvis gynaecoid, head fully engaged. The registrar suggests cutting a midline episiotomy at the 12 o'clock position to maximise space.
lead_in: Which response is most appropriate regarding episiotomy in this birth?

options.t: Decline midline episiotomy; cut a mediolateral episiotomy at 60 degrees from the midline at crowning
Cut the midline episiotomy as the registrar suggests to maximise outlet space
Cut a mediolateral episiotomy at 30 degrees from the midline before crowning
Avoid episiotomy entirely as routine episiotomy is not recommended
Cut a J-shaped episiotomy starting midline then curving laterally at crowning

subtopic_detail: Active management of the third stage of labour
stem: A 33-year-old G3P2 at 39+5 has just had an uncomplicated spontaneous vaginal birth of a 3500 g infant. The baby is on the maternal chest, Apgar 9 at 1 minute, with delayed cord clamping at 90 seconds. Booked BP at 36 weeks was 138/86 and her booking BP today 1 hour ago was 145/92; she has been on labetalol for gestational hypertension since 32 weeks. She has no proteinuria and was not on magnesium sulfate. Maternal observations are now BP 150/95, HR 90, EBL so far 200 mL, uterus palpable at the umbilicus. Active management of the third stage is planned per unit protocol.
lead_in: Which uterotonic is the most appropriate component of active management for this woman?

options.t: Oxytocin 10 IU IM with delivery of the anterior shoulder, controlled cord traction and uterine massage
Syntometrine (ergometrine 500 microg + oxytocin 5 IU) IM with controlled cord traction and uterine massage
Ergometrine 500 microg IM with delivery of the anterior shoulder and uterine massage
Misoprostol 600 microg orally with controlled cord traction and uterine massage
Carbetocin 100 microg IV bolus with immediate cord clamping and uterine massage

subtopic_detail: Retained placenta - timing and approach to manual removal
stem: A 30-year-old G2P1 at 40+1 has had a spontaneous vaginal birth of a 3400 g infant 45 minutes ago after a 7-hour labour with no oxytocin augmentation and no instrumental delivery. Active management of the third stage was performed with oxytocin 10 IU IM at delivery of the anterior shoulder, and controlled cord traction has been attempted multiple times by the experienced midwife and registrar without placental delivery. There is no significant ongoing bleeding, total EBL is 350 mL. The uterus is palpable and contracted at the umbilicus and there is no apparent uterine inversion. The cord is intact, appears at the introitus, and shows no signs of having broken on traction. The woman has an epidural in situ that is providing good analgesia and remains haemodynamically stable. Her previous birth was an uncomplicated SVD with a normal third stage 3 years ago. She had one D&C for a missed miscarriage at 10 weeks two years ago and an LLETZ for CIN2 four years ago. No previous caesarean. The placenta is therefore retained at 45 minutes despite active management with no third-stage haemorrhage. The midwife asks the obstetric registrar what should happen next, and the on-call consultant has been informed.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Continue expectant management for a further 30 minutes given no significant bleeding evident
Inject 20 IU oxytocin diluted in 20 mL saline into the umbilical vein
Administer ergometrine 500 microg intramuscularly now and reattempt controlled cord traction in theatre
Perform manual removal at the bedside without anaesthesia since the woman has a working epidural
Transfer to theatre for manual placental removal under regional or general anaesthesia with IV antibiotics and oxytocin infusion

subtopic_detail: Intrapartum GBS prophylaxis - regimen and penicillin allergy
stem: A 27-year-old G2P1 at 39+0 attends in spontaneous labour at 5 cm. Her vaginorectal GBS screening swab at 36+2 was positive. Her previous birth was an uncomplicated spontaneous vaginal delivery at 39+5 and the infant had no early-onset GBS sepsis and was discharged home well. She has a documented allergy to penicillin recorded in detail in her record as widespread urticarial rash starting 2 days into a course of amoxicillin for a dental infection three years ago, without angioedema, without wheeze, without hypotension and without anaphylaxis or hospitalisation. Susceptibility testing on the GBS isolate from this pregnancy's swab is documented in the pathology report as susceptible to clindamycin and erythromycin. She has no other comorbidities, is afebrile, has a reassuring CTG, normal observations and no other risk factors for intrapartum infection. Membranes ruptured 30 minutes ago with clear liquor and CTG is reassuring with baseline 138, variability 10-15, accelerations and no decelerations. The midwife asks the resident which antibiotic regimen should be commenced now for intrapartum GBS prophylaxis.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate intrapartum antibiotic regimen for GBS prophylaxis?

options.t: Benzylpenicillin 3 g IV loading then 1.8 g IV every 4 hours until delivery as prophylaxis
Clindamycin 600 mg IV every 8 hours until delivery as monotherapy intrapartum
Vancomycin 1 g IV every 8 hours until delivery as monotherapy intrapartum
Cefazolin 2 g IV loading then 1 g IV every 8 hours until delivery as prophylaxis
Oral amoxicillin 1 g every 8 hours until delivery as intrapartum prophylaxis

subtopic_detail: Lactational mastitis - antibiotic choice in penicillin allergy
stem: A 30-year-old G2P2 woman, three weeks postpartum and exclusively breastfeeding her well term infant, presents to her GP with a 24-hour history of a tender, hot, wedge-shaped erythematous area in the upper outer quadrant of the left breast. She feels feverish and shivery, has been continuing frequent feeds and effective expression after feeds, and the area has continued to enlarge over the last 12 hours. There is no fluctuance, no skin breakdown and no axillary adenopathy. She is otherwise well. Her recorded drug allergy is to phenoxymethylpenicillin during childhood, when she developed an urticarial rash and lip swelling within 1 hour of a dose; she has never tolerated a penicillin or cephalosporin since. She has no other allergies, has not had antibiotics in the past 12 months, has no recent hospital contact and is not from a community with known high MRSA prevalence. She wishes to continue breastfeeding throughout treatment and her infant has no medical conditions and was born at term with no neonatal complications.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate oral antibiotic regimen?

options.t: Flucloxacillin 500 mg orally four times daily for 7 days
Cefalexin 500 mg orally four times daily for 7 days
Clindamycin 450 mg orally three times daily for 7 days
Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg orally twice daily for 7 days
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 160/800 mg orally twice daily for 7 days

subtopic_detail: Blocked duct vs inflammatory mastitis vs bacterial mastitis discrimination
stem: A 27-year-old primipara, 6 weeks postpartum, exclusively breastfeeding her thriving term infant, attends with a 14-hour history of a discrete tender lump in the lower inner right breast. Overlying skin has faint pink mottling but no defined erythema. T 36.8, no rigors, no flu-like symptoms, infant feeding effectively. Recent change: baby has slept a longer 5-hour stretch the past two nights. No fluctuance, no axillary adenopathy. She has read online that 'aggressive deep massage' and 'pump-and-dump' are recommended.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management?

options.t: Continue feeding from both breasts on demand, apply cold packs between feeds, ibuprofen for pain, and review in 24 hours; avoid deep massage and pump-and-dump
Commence oral flucloxacillin 500 mg qid for 7 days now
Aggressive deep breast massage every 2 hours with hot compresses for 48 hours
Cease feeding from the affected breast and pump-and-dump for 48 hours
Urgent breast ultrasound and surgical referral for incision and drainage

stem: A 32-year-old G3P3 woman, 4 weeks postpartum and exclusively breastfeeding, was diagnosed with right-breast bacterial mastitis 5 days ago and commenced flucloxacillin 500 mg orally four times daily with continued feeding and expression. Her fevers settled within 48 hours but the erythematous area has now developed a discrete, fluctuant tender lump over the past 24 hours despite ongoing antibiotics and effective drainage. She is afebrile today, haemodynamically stable, and the skin overlying the lump is intact, non-necrotic and not under tension. Bedside ultrasound performed by the GP demonstrates a well-defined, unilocular hypoechoic collection measuring 38 mm x 24 mm at 1.5 cm depth, with no overlying skin necrosis. She has no recurrent abscess history, no immunosuppression, no diabetes and wishes to continue breastfeeding. She is comfortable being managed in primary care if the lesion is amenable to office-based drainage and the local Breast Centre offers ultrasound-guided procedures on a same-day basis.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Continue current oral flucloxacillin and review in 5 days without drainage
Refer for ultrasound-guided needle aspiration with culture; continue antibiotics, reaspirate if needed
Refer for theatre incision and drainage with packing under general anaesthetic
Stop breastfeeding from the affected breast and continue antibiotics only
Switch to oral clindamycin 450 mg three times daily and observe without drainage

subtopic_detail: Low milk supply - first step in assessment
stem: A 28-year-old primiparous woman attends with her 3-week-old term infant for a same-day appointment because she is convinced her milk supply has dropped. The baby is now feeding for 60-80 minutes per feed, 10-12 times in 24 hours, and is unsettled between feeds. She produces only 30 mL when she expresses after a feed and feels her breasts are softer than they were at 10 days. The infant had a birthweight of 3.4 kg, was discharged at 3.15 kg on day 4 (regained birthweight by day 12), and today weighs 3.55 kg on the practice scales. The infant is alert, has 6-8 heavy wet nappies and 3-4 mustard-yellow stools per day, has no jaundice, and feeds with audible swallowing during feeds. She has been advised by family to start formula top-ups. She is not on any medications, has no breast surgery history, has normal-appearing breasts that grew through pregnancy, and breastfed her older nieces' children briefly without difficulty.
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.t: Commence formula top-ups of 60 mL after each breastfeed to meet caloric requirements
Reassure: growth, nappy output and feeding behaviour indicate adequate transfer; review in 1 week
Commence domperidone 10 mg three times daily as a pharmacological galactagogue
Arrange measured pre-feed and post-feed test-weighs over 24 hours at home
Refer for maternal pituitary MRI to exclude Sheehan syndrome as the cause

subtopic_detail: Tongue tie - indications for frenotomy referral
stem: A 30-year-old primiparous woman attends the GP at 3 weeks postpartum with her term infant. She has persistent bilateral cracked, bleeding nipples that have not improved despite three lactation consultant reviews focused on positioning and attachment; the latch has been optimised across multiple holds and she has tried nipple shields without lasting benefit. Feeds last 50-70 minutes and the infant frequently breaks suction with a clicking sound. Infant weight gain has fallen below the expected trajectory: birthweight 3.5 kg, discharge weight 3.25 kg, today's weight 3.4 kg at 3 weeks (mean gain ~10 g/day from nadir, below the 20-30 g/day threshold). On examination by an experienced lactation consultant, the infant has a thin anterior tongue frenulum that restricts tongue elevation to less than midline when crying, a heart-shaped tongue tip when the tongue is lifted, and a Hazelbaker tongue-function score that meets criteria for ankyloglossia with impaired function. The infant has no other oral, craniofacial or neurological abnormalities and is otherwise well.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Refer to an experienced clinician for assessment and frenotomy of the anterior tongue tie
Continue positioning and attachment optimisation for another 4 weeks before considering frenotomy
Commence formula top-ups and discontinue breastfeeding given the persistent nipple trauma
Refer for posterior tongue tie and upper-lip tie release out of hours regardless of findings
Prescribe long-term nipple shields and review at the 6-week postnatal check

stem: A 33-year-old G2P2 woman is reviewed in the GP-IBCLC combined clinic at 5 weeks postpartum with confirmed insufficient milk supply contributing to faltering infant growth despite four weeks of structured input. Positioning and attachment have been optimised, frequency of feeds increased, post-feed expression added, and a structural cause has been excluded (no tongue tie, no breast surgery, no retained placenta, normal thyroid). Infant weight gain remains below 20 g/day with documented inadequate transfer on test-weighs over 24 hours. The mother wishes to use a galactagogue as adjunctive therapy to maintain breastfeeding while continuing the non-pharmacological measures. She has no personal cardiac history, but on review of her medications she takes citalopram 20 mg daily for anxiety. Her resting baseline ECG today shows sinus rhythm at 78 bpm with QTc 460 ms (Bazett) and normal electrolytes including K+ 4.4 mmol/L and Mg2+ 0.85 mmol/L. She has no family history of sudden cardiac death and no syncope. The IBCLC asks specifically whether domperidone is appropriate in her situation.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step regarding domperidone?

options.t: Commence domperidone 10 mg three times daily for 7 days as a galactagogue
Avoid domperidone; review citalopram with her psychiatrist and intensify non-pharmacological measures
Increase domperidone dose to 20 mg four times daily to maximise the milk yield
Substitute metoclopramide 10 mg three times daily as a long-term galactagogue
Commence fenugreek 600 mg three times daily as a herbal galactagogue without other changes

subtopic_detail: Vault prolapse after hysterectomy - surgical choice
stem: A 64-year-old G3P3 postmenopausal woman attends urogynaecology with 12 months of progressive vaginal vault prolapse 9 years after a total abdominal hysterectomy for benign menorrhagia. She has previously trialled a ring pessary for 6 months with recurrent expulsion despite refit, and supervised pelvic floor physiotherapy for 4 months with no symptomatic benefit. POP-Q examination shows vaginal vault descent to +4 with anterior wall to +2 and posterior wall to 0; TVL is 8 cm. She is sexually active and wishes to preserve coital function; she has no obesity-related surgical risk (BMI 24), no significant cardiopulmonary disease, no diabetes and is otherwise healthy with no contraindication to general anaesthetic and abdominal surgery.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate surgical procedure?

options.t: Laparoscopic sacrocolpopexy with type 1 polypropylene mesh fixed to the anterior sacral ligament
Transvaginal mesh kit repair of the vaginal vault using a synthetic mesh device
Sacrospinous ligament fixation of the vaginal vault through a vaginal surgical approach
Le Fort colpocleisis with obliteration of the vagina for definitive vault support
Repeat pessary trial with a Gellhorn pessary plus indefinite topical oestrogen therapy

stem: A 58-year-old G3P3 postmenopausal woman presents with 6 months of a vaginal bulge worse at end of day and a need to splint to defaecate. She also reports new-onset stress urinary incontinence on coughing, urgency without leakage, and one episode of incomplete bladder emptying requiring her to bear down. No faecal incontinence, no dyspareunia. BMI 26, postmenopausal 8 years. Speculum and POP-Q: Ba 0 (anterior at hymen on Valsalva), Bp +1 (posterior beyond hymen), C -5 (cervix well supported), Aa 0, Ap +1. Post-void residual 90 mL. Bowel motions Bristol 4 on fibre.
lead_in: Which compartment defect best explains her splinting-to-defaecate symptom specifically?

options.t: Posterior vaginal compartment defect (rectocele), evidenced by Bp +1 and need to splint posteriorly to evacuate stool
Anterior vaginal compartment defect (cystocele), causing her splinting symptom
Apical compartment defect of the uterine cervix, causing her splinting symptom
Enterocele of the pouch of Douglas, causing her splinting symptom
Post-hysterectomy vault prolapse defect

subtopic_detail: Urge incontinence pharmacological choice in older woman
stem: A 78-year-old woman with mild cognitive impairment (MoCA 24) presents to her GP with 9 months of urinary urgency, urge incontinence approximately twice daily and nocturia 3 times. She has had a structured trial of bladder training and supervised pelvic floor physiotherapy for 12 weeks under a continence nurse, with caffeine and evening fluid restriction implemented, and reports modest but inadequate improvement. There is no stress component on her bladder diary, no haematuria, and PVR is 30 mL. Urinalysis is clear; recent FBC, UEC, glucose and Ca2+ are normal. She has well-controlled hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg, no untreated narrow-angle glaucoma, no severe constipation and her resting heart rate is 72 bpm with BP 134/82 mmHg. She does not have severe uncontrolled hypertension. She is concerned about adding medications that might worsen her memory. Her geriatrician and GP agree to add pharmacotherapy to her ongoing conservative measures.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate pharmacological agent?

options.t: Oxybutynin immediate-release 5 mg orally twice a day
Mirabegron 25 mg orally daily, titrating to 50 mg if needed
Solifenacin 5 mg orally daily as a long-acting antimuscarinic
Duloxetine 40 mg orally twice daily for the urinary symptoms
Desmopressin 0.1 mg orally at night for the urge incontinence

stem: A 55-year-old G3P3 woman is referred to urogynaecology after 12 months of stress urinary incontinence symptoms refractory to a documented 6-month programme of supervised pelvic floor muscle training and weight loss (lost 7 kg, BMI now 28). Her bladder diary shows leakage with cough/sneeze/exertion only, no urgency or urge episodes, no voiding difficulty. PVR is 20 mL and a positive cough stress test reproduces leakage in clinic. There is no haematuria, no recurrent UTI, no prior pelvic surgery, no neurological disease and no prolapse beyond the hymen (POP-Q Ba -1). She has been counselled about a mid-urethral sling and an autologous fascial sling and is keen to proceed surgically. She has not had urodynamic studies. The clinical fellow asks whether urodynamics should be performed before sling surgery. She has not had any previous incontinence surgery, no pelvic radiotherapy and no relevant neurological disease.
lead_in: Which approach to urodynamics is most consistent with current guidance?

options.t: Routine urodynamic studies in every woman before any incontinence surgery
Urodynamics not required before primary sling here; reserve for mixed or complex
Urodynamic studies only after a failed primary mid-urethral sling procedure
Urodynamic studies required only when the post-void residual exceeds 100 mL
Urodynamics is mandatory whenever any pharmacotherapy is being considered

stem: A 48-year-old G3P3 woman attends the gynaecology outpatient clinic 4 weeks after a total laparoscopic hysterectomy for symptomatic fibroids. The procedure was complicated by adhesions at the vesicouterine peritoneum requiring sharp dissection from the bladder; the surgeon documented bladder integrity at the end of the case using bladder filling without leak observed. For the past 8 days she has had continuous painless watery leakage into her underwear regardless of position or activity, with no urge, no stress trigger, no incomplete emptying sensation, and unchanged daytime voids of normal volume. There is no fever, no flank pain, no haematuria, no vaginal bleeding. Urinalysis is negative for blood, nitrites and leucocytes. Speculum examination reveals clear fluid pooling in the vaginal vault but no visible defect on direct inspection. The vault is well healed; there is no apparent vault dehiscence. She is on no medications other than recent post-operative paracetamol and is otherwise well.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next investigation to confirm the diagnosis?

options.t: Three-swab dye test: instil dilute methylene blue into the bladder and inspect vaginal swabs
Empirical oral antibiotics for 7 days then outpatient review of the leakage pattern
Urodynamic studies as first-line to characterise the pattern of continuous leakage
MRI pelvis with rectal contrast to define the suspected genitourinary tract fistula
Outpatient pelvic floor physiotherapy with re-review in 3 months without imaging

subtopic_detail: VIN - management of HSIL (uVIN) vs differentiated VIN
stem: A 38-year-old woman with HIV (CD4 540, viral load undetectable on ART) is referred to a vulval clinic with 9 months of mild vulval pruritus. Examination shows two well-demarcated, slightly raised, pigmented and acetowhite plaques on the right labium minus measuring 8 mm and 12 mm, with no ulceration, induration or vascular atypia. There is no clitoral hood resorption, no labial fusion and no porcelain-white skin. Vulvoscopy-directed punch biopsy from each lesion shows high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (vulvar HSIL, also called usual-type VIN) with full-thickness dysplasia, koilocytotic change and p16 block-positive immunohistochemistry consistent with HPV-driven disease; there is no invasion, no microinvasion and no features of differentiated VIN (no basal atypia on a background of lichen sclerosus). She has had previous CIN 2 treated by LLETZ 5 years ago and is up to date with HPV-based cervical screening. She is sexually active with one partner, both fully HPV-vaccinated, no immunosuppressive medications other than ART, no other comorbidities and is keen to preserve vulval anatomy if possible.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management?

options.t: Topical imiquimod 5% three times weekly for up to 16 weeks
Watchful waiting with annual vulvoscopy and no active treatment
Radical vulvectomy with bilateral inguinofemoral lymphadenectomy for vulval HSIL
Topical clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment to lesions for 12 weeks
Topical 5-fluorouracil cream applied daily indefinitely as primary therapy

stem: A 28-year-old nulliparous woman presents with a 3-day history of progressively painful right vulval swelling, now interfering with walking. T 37.1, no rigors, no STI symptoms. Examination: 4 cm fluctuant tender mass at the right posterolateral introitus (5 o'clock) consistent with a Bartholin abscess; intact non-necrotic skin, no cellulitis spreading beyond. This is her THIRD Bartholin abscess at the same site in the last 12 months. The two previous episodes were treated with simple incision and drainage with packing, and both recurred within 3 months. She is otherwise well, on no medications, and wants definitive treatment that minimises further recurrence.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management?

options.t: Simple incision and drainage with packing under local anaesthetic
Marsupialisation under general anaesthetic
Word catheter insertion under local anaesthetic for 4-6 weeks
Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg twice daily for 7 days without drainage
Excision of the Bartholin gland as primary management

stem: A 27-year-old woman with focal epilepsy attends her GP for preconception counselling. She has been seizure-free for 4 years on sodium valproate 1000 mg daily as monotherapy, prescribed by her neurologist who has been unable to wean her without seizure recurrence. She is in a stable relationship, drinks no alcohol, BMI 24, no other medications. There is no personal or family history of neural tube defects. She wishes to conceive within the next 6 months and has not yet started any vitamins. She has read online that 'folic acid 5 mg' is recommended in epilepsy.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate preconception advice?

options.t: Continue valproate, start folic acid 5 mg daily and aim to conceive in 6 months
Refer to neurology for transition to a less teratogenic antiepileptic (e.g. lamotrigine or levetiracetam) and start folic acid 5 mg daily, deferring conception until stable on the new agent
Stop valproate today without neurology input and start folic acid 400 mcg daily
Continue valproate and add lamotrigine to allow pregnancy on combination therapy
Continue valproate and start folic acid 400 mcg daily, the standard dose for low-risk women

subtopic_detail: Epilepsy - valproate switch and neonatal vitamin K
stem: A 31-year-old G2P1 woman with generalised tonic clonic epilepsy is reviewed by the obstetric medicine clinic at 34 weeks. She has been on sodium valproate 1000 mg daily since adolescence and has had no seizures since her late teens. Her previous pregnancy four years ago ended in a healthy term infant who is meeting all developmental milestones, although she was not taking folate at conception that time. She booked for this pregnancy at 9 weeks and her neurologist discussed switching to a less teratogenic antiepileptic but she elected to remain on valproate given her excellent control and the late presentation; she did commence folic acid 5 mg daily from that visit.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate additional measure to reduce neonatal haemorrhagic disease in this pregnancy?

options.t: Intramuscular vitamin K 1 mg to the neonate at birth as routine prophylaxis
Maternal oral vitamin K 10 mg daily from 36 weeks until delivery
Switch valproate to lamotrigine now at 34 weeks gestation here
Elective caesarean section at 39 weeks to reduce bleeding risk
Fresh frozen plasma transfusion to the neonate within 6 hours of birth

stem: A 24-year-old G1P0 at 9 weeks gestation has had asthma since childhood, well-controlled on budesonide 200 mcg bd plus salbutamol PRN. Over the past month she has needed reliever 4-5 times per week with two episodes of nocturnal cough. Spirometry today: FEV1 78% predicted (baseline 95% pre-pregnancy). No infective symptoms, no exposure changes. She tells you a friend advised her to stop the budesonide because 'steroids cross the placenta'. She is up to date with influenza vaccination and folic acid.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate management of her asthma now?

options.t: Continue current budesonide 200 mcg bd and reassess in 4 weeks
Step up inhaled corticosteroid (e.g. budesonide-formoterol combination) per the Australian Asthma Handbook and continue throughout pregnancy
Stop budesonide and use salbutamol alone for the duration of pregnancy
Switch to oral prednisolone 10 mg daily for the duration of pregnancy
Switch to montelukast 10 mg daily as monotherapy

subtopic_detail: Hypothyroidism - thyroxine dose escalation on confirming pregnancy
stem: A 33-year-old woman with primary hypothyroidism after thyroidectomy for benign multinodular goitre two years ago presents at 6 weeks of a confirmed singleton pregnancy. She has been stable on levothyroxine 100 micrograms daily for 18 months with TSH consistently between 1.2 and 1.8 mIU/L. Her last TSH four months ago was 1.4 mIU/L. She conceived spontaneously after stopping the combined oral contraceptive eight weeks ago and reports nausea but no vomiting. She has no other medical problems and is on no other regular medications. She has commenced folic acid 500 micrograms daily and iodine 150 micrograms daily as advised by her GP. She has not yet had pregnancy bloods drawn and asks whether she should change her thyroxine dose now or wait for the booking blood results, which she will have done in the next few days. She has not had any change in body weight, energy, cold tolerance or constipation. There is no family history of thyroid autoimmunity.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate adjustment to her levothyroxine?

options.t: Maintain 100 micrograms daily and recheck TSH in 4 weeks
Stop iodine supplementation because she is taking levothyroxine
Double the dose to 200 micrograms daily and recheck in 6 weeks
Switch to liothyronine 20 micrograms three times daily here
Increase to approximately 125 micrograms daily and recheck TSH in 4 weeks

stem: A 34-year-old woman is referred to the early pregnancy clinic at 6 weeks gestation. She has had three consecutive first-trimester miscarriages at 8, 9 and 10 weeks, all spontaneous and chromosomally untested. Six months ago she was investigated for recurrent pregnancy loss and found to have persistently positive lupus anticoagulant on two occasions 12 weeks apart, with negative anticardiolipin and beta-2-glycoprotein-I antibodies. She has no personal history of thrombosis, no history of pre-eclampsia, no known autoimmune disease and no family history of VTE. Her BMI is 26 kg/m2 and she does not smoke. She is on folic acid 500 micrograms daily but is not yet on any other medication. Both she and her partner are otherwise well. The clinic discusses her diagnosis of obstetric antiphospholipid syndrome based on her clinical pregnancy loss history and persistent lupus anticoagulant, and offers a treatment plan to reduce miscarriage risk in this pregnancy. She has no contraindications to anticoagulation.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate treatment to start this pregnancy?

options.t: Therapeutic-dose enoxaparin alone without aspirin for the whole pregnancy
Aspirin 100 mg daily alone with no anticoagulant in this pregnancy
Warfarin 5 mg daily titrated to INR 2.0-3.0 throughout pregnancy
Aspirin 100 mg daily plus prophylactic dose enoxaparin from a positive pregnancy test
Rivaroxaban 10 mg orally daily as a once daily anticoagulant option

subtopic_detail: HIV - mode of delivery decision by viral load
stem: A 30-year-old G1P0 woman with HIV diagnosed three years ago attends her 36 week obstetric review. She has been on combination antiretroviral therapy (tenofovir disoproxil, emtricitabine and dolutegravir) since diagnosis and has been virologically suppressed throughout this pregnancy. Her viral load four weeks ago was undetectable (<20 copies/mL) and her CD4 count was 620 cells/microlitre. She has no opportunistic infections, no co-infections (HBV and HCV negative) and no other comorbidities. Her pregnancy has been uncomplicated with a normally grown cephalic foetus on the 50th centile and reassuring serial umbilical artery Dopplers. She wishes to plan a vaginal birth and asks the obstetric registrar what the recommendation is regarding mode of delivery given her status and her preference. She has no obstetric contraindications to vaginal birth and there are no concerns on CTG today. She has not yet ruptured membranes and is not in labour. The infectious diseases physician has been co-managing her care.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate recommendation regarding mode of delivery?

options.t: Plan an elective caesarean section at 38 weeks regardless of viral load
Plan a vaginal birth at term with continuation of antiretroviral therapy
Stop antiretroviral therapy two weeks before planned vaginal birth
Plan an elective caesarean section at 39 weeks plus intravenous zidovudine
Induction of labour at 37 weeks to minimise duration of ruptured membranes

subtopic_detail: HIV - infant prophylaxis and feeding choice in Australia
stem: A 26-year-old G1P0 woman with HIV diagnosed prior to pregnancy is reviewed postnatally on the maternity ward six hours after a normal vaginal birth at 39+2 weeks of a 3.2 kg infant. She has been on combination antiretroviral therapy throughout pregnancy and her most recent HIV viral load at 36 weeks was <20 copies/mL. The infant is well and feeding cues are emerging. She is hepatitis B surface antigen negative and hepatitis C negative. She has no other comorbidities and her partner is HIV negative and on PrEP. She lives in a major Australian city, has stable housing, a refrigerator, reliable formula supply and supportive family. She asks the midwife about safe feeding choices and what medication, if any, the baby needs. The neonatologist is also reviewing the infant and confirms the baby is well, term and not requiring resuscitation. The infectious diseases physician has been involved throughout pregnancy and is available to discuss the neonatal plan.
lead_in: Which combination of advice and prophylaxis is most appropriate for this infant in an Australian setting?

options.t: Formula feeding plus oral triple antiretroviral therapy to the infant for 6 weeks
Exclusive breastfeeding for six months plus oral zidovudine for the infant
Exclusive formula feeding plus oral zidovudine monotherapy for 2-4 weeks
Mixed feeding (breast and formula) plus zidovudine to the infant for 2 weeks
Exclusive formula feeding alone with no antiretroviral prophylaxis required

subtopic_detail: Hepatitis C - antenatal counselling and breastfeeding
stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 woman with chronic hepatitis C diagnosed two years ago presents at her booking visit at 10 weeks gestation. She acquired HCV through past injecting drug use and has been abstinent for three years with current support from an addiction medicine service. Her HCV RNA is 5.2 x 10^5 IU/mL with genotype 1a and ALT 32 U/L. She has no co-infection with HIV or HBV. Her liver synthetic function is preserved with INR 1.0, albumin 38 g/L, platelets 220 x10^9/L and a FibroScan of 5.1 kPa nine months ago indicating minimal fibrosis. She has not commenced direct-acting antiviral therapy. She has no other medical issues, takes folic acid 500 micrograms daily and has no allergies. She is in stable accommodation with her supportive partner who is HCV antibody negative. She asks the obstetric team about treatment in pregnancy, vertical transmission risk and whether she will be able to breastfeed her baby.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate advice to give now?

options.t: Defer direct-acting antiviral therapy and plan breastfeeding once nipples intact
Start sofosbuvir/velpatasvir at 12 weeks and continue for 12 weeks total
Plan elective caesarean section at 39 weeks to reduce vertical transmission
Advise no breastfeeding because of detectable HCV RNA in her serum
Test the newborn with HCV antibody within 48 hours of delivery here

subtopic_detail: ITP - platelet threshold for neuraxial anaesthesia
stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 woman with ITP diagnosed three years ago presents to obstetric day assessment at 36 weeks gestation. Her ITP has previously responded to oral prednisolone but she has been off all therapy for the past 12 months with platelet counts between 90 and 110 x10^9/L. Today her routine bloods show a platelet count of 72 x10^9/L, down from 95 x10^9/L two weeks ago. She has no bleeding, no petechiae and no bruising, and the rest of her FBC and a peripheral blood film show isolated thrombocytopenia consistent with her known ITP. She has no signs of pre-eclampsia, no other medical problems and no allergies. She is on iron supplementation only and her booking HIV, HBV and HCV serology was negative. She is keen to have an epidural in labour. The obstetric anaesthetist and obstetric haematologist are reviewing her at 36 weeks and discussing a plan to optimise her platelets prior to expected delivery.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management plan with respect to neuraxial anaesthesia and delivery?

options.t: No treatment required because platelets above 50 x10^9/L allow epidural use
Commence oral prednisolone aiming for platelets above 80 x10^9/L by labour
Plan immediate platelet transfusion to bring the count above 100 x10^9/L
Start eltrombopag 50 mg orally daily and continue throughout pregnancy
Splenectomy after delivery is no longer indicated for chronic ITP

stem: A 29-year-old G1P0 woman with Crohn disease presents for preconception counselling. She has been in clinical and biochemical remission for 18 months on azathioprine 150 mg daily and adalimumab 40 mg subcutaneously every two weeks. Three years ago, before stable remission, she had a flare during which she received a 6-month course of methotrexate that was subsequently stopped. She is now planning pregnancy in the next three months. She has no other medical problems and no allergies. Her gastroenterologist is part of the discussion and her preconception bloods (FBC, U&E, LFTs, CRP and faecal calprotectin <50 micrograms/g) are reassuring. She has commenced folic acid 5 mg daily. She asks specifically which of her current and past medications might need to change before conception. Her vaccinations including MMR, varicella and pertussis are up to date and her partner is well with no relevant medical history.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate medication advice for preconception?

options.t: Stop azathioprine preconception and switch to oral budesonide as her sole maintenance therapy for the pregnancy
Continue azathioprine and adalimumab; ensure methotrexate has been stopped for at least 3-6 months
Stop adalimumab at conception and avoid all biologic therapy for the duration of the pregnancy
Restart methotrexate 15 mg weekly if any biochemical evidence of flare during this pregnancy emerges
Switch azathioprine to mycophenolate mofetil preconception for stronger maintenance immunosuppression during pregnancy

stem: A 26-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes diagnosed at age 11 attends preconception clinic with her partner. She uses multiple daily injections (insulin glargine 18 units nocte and insulin aspart with meals) with continuous glucose monitoring. Her most recent HbA1c is 8.4% (68 mmol/mol), down from 9.2% (77 mmol/mol) six months ago. She has background diabetic retinopathy on screening last year but no proliferative changes, urine ACR 1.8 mg/mmol, normal renal function and no neuropathy.
