She has no other medical problems, no allergies and is not on any other regular medication. She is taking a standard prenatal multivitamin but no separate folic acid supplement. She has been smoke free for two years and drinks no alcohol. She tells you she would like to start trying to conceive in the next six months and asks whether her current glucose control and supplementation are adequate. Her partner is well.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in her preconception care?

options.t: Switch from insulin to metformin 1 g twice daily before conception and continue throughout the pregnancy
Reassure that the current HbA1c of 8.4% is acceptable for proceeding immediately to conception attempts
Intensify glucose management aiming for HbA1c below 6.5% and start folic acid 5 mg daily
Advise indefinitely against pregnancy because of background diabetic retinopathy and the current HbA1c level
Start a SGLT2 inhibitor such as empagliflozin preconception to reduce HbA1c rapidly before conception attempts

subtopic_detail: Intrapartum glucose targets in pre-existing diabetes
stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 woman with type 1 diabetes for 15 years is admitted in spontaneous labour at 38+3 weeks. She has been on insulin pump therapy throughout pregnancy with an HbA1c at 36 weeks of 5.9% (41 mmol/mol). Growth has tracked along the 80th centile with an estimated foetal weight 3.7 kg one week ago. There is no polyhydramnios. She has no diabetic complications other than background retinopathy and no pre-eclampsia. She is currently 4 cm dilated, contracting regularly and requesting an epidural. On admission her capillary blood glucose is 8.2 mmol/L and she has trace ketones on urine dip. The midwifery team is asking the obstetric registrar about the appropriate glucose target during labour and the optimal way to manage her insulin from now on. She has no allergies and no other regular medications. The neonatal team has been notified to attend delivery.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate intrapartum glucose management?

options.t: Continue subcutaneous insulin pump unchanged with 4-hourly capillary glucose monitoring
Switch to variable-rate IV insulin infusion targeting CBG 4-7 mmol/L
Stop all insulin in labour and recommence after delivery of the placenta
Aim for capillary glucose 8-10 mmol/L to reduce maternal hypoglycaemia risk
Give a single dose of long-acting insulin glargine 20 units now

subtopic_detail: Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy - UDCA and delivery timing
stem: A 30-year-old G2P1 woman at 35+2 weeks presents with two weeks of progressive generalised itching that is worst on the palms and soles and worse at night. She has no rash, no jaundice and no right upper quadrant pain. She has no significant past medical history and her booking bloods including viral screen were normal. Her previous pregnancy was uncomplicated. Her LFTs today show ALT 110 U/L (ULN 35), AST 88 U/L (ULN 35), bilirubin 12 micromol/L, GGT 60 U/L, ALP 280 U/L, and total fasting bile acids 56 micromol/L. Her INR is 1.0 and platelets are 220 x10^9/L. She has no signs of pre-eclampsia, urine PCR is 4 mg/mmol and her CTG is reassuring. Liver ultrasound shows no gallstones, normal hepatic parenchyma and patent portal vessels. She has had no other liver insults and is on no regular medications other than a pregnancy multivitamin.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate combined plan for medication and timing of delivery?

options.t: Ursodeoxycholic acid 500 mg twice daily and plan delivery at 37-38 weeks
Topical emollients only and plan delivery at term 39-40 weeks
Cholestyramine 4 g three times daily and plan immediate delivery now
Oral dexamethasone 12 mg daily for 7 days as definitive symptomatic therapy
Antihistamine therapy with chlorphenamine and expectant management to 40 weeks

stem: A 29-year-old G1P0 has her first ultrasound at 9+4 by certain LMP, showing two viable intrauterine pregnancies. The report describes two gestational sacs, two yolk sacs, a thick echogenic inter-twin membrane and a wedge of placental tissue extending into the base of the membrane (lambda sign). Both crown-rump lengths are concordant. The next appointment with maternal-foetal medicine is in 5 weeks. The community midwife asks the registrar what additional management is required as a direct consequence of this chorionicity assignment, beyond singleton routine care.
lead_in: Which management plan correctly reflects the chorionicity and the implications for care?

options.t: Dichorionic diamniotic twins: scan every 4 weeks from 24 weeks for growth and Dopplers, plan delivery by 37+6 weeks if uncomplicated
Monochorionic diamniotic twins: scan every 2 weeks from 16 weeks for TTTS, deliver by 36+6 weeks
Monochorionic monoamniotic twins: admit from 24 weeks for daily CTG and deliver by 32-34 weeks
Chorionicity cannot be assigned until 11-14 weeks gestation
Dichorionic monoamniotic twins exist and require shared monitoring; plan delivery by 34 weeks

stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 with a confirmed MCDA twin pregnancy at 19+5 weeks attends fortnightly surveillance. The 16-week scan was unremarkable. Today's ultrasound: twin 1 (donor) EFW 22nd centile, DVP 1.6 cm, bladder not seen on three sweeps over 30 minutes; twin 2 (recipient) EFW 56th centile, DVP 9.2 cm, distended bladder, normal MCA-PSV. Umbilical artery Dopplers normal both twins, no foetal hydrops, cervix 38 mm. She has no contractions, no bleeding, no symptoms of pre-eclampsia. The tertiary foetal medicine unit is 2 hours away and offers fetoscopic laser ablation daily. She wants the option that maximises the chance of two healthy survivors.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Urgent referral to a tertiary foetal medicine unit for fetoscopic laser ablation of the placental anastomoses
Reassure and continue routine fortnightly surveillance for 2 more weeks
Amnioreduction of the recipient sac in the local maternity unit
Admit for antenatal corticosteroids and plan delivery this week
Selective foetal reduction of the donor twin by cord occlusion now

stem: A 30-year-old G1P0 RhD-negative woman with a DCDA twin pregnancy (two viable embryos at 7+1) re-presents at 13+0 with a single episode of pink spotting yesterday, now resolved. She is haemodynamically stable. Ultrasound today: twin A viable, CRL appropriate for 13+0; twin B sac collapsed, embryo CRL 7+5 with no cardiac activity (demised). Antibody screen at booking and today: negative. She has not previously received anti-D in this pregnancy. She is otherwise well.
lead_in: Which combination of management is most appropriate?

options.t: Reassure regarding minimal increased risk to the surviving twin from the early loss; administer anti-D 625 IU IM as the bleeding episode is at 13+0; continue routine DCDA twin surveillance
Administer anti-D 250 IU IM as the dose for sensitising events before 20 weeks
Withhold anti-D until 28 weeks routine prophylaxis as the antibody screen is negative
Surgical evacuation of the demised gestational sac to prevent later miscarriage of the surviving twin
Admit for serial coagulation studies given the increased maternal risk of disseminated intravascular coagulation

subtopic_detail: Mode and timing of delivery - MCDA twins
stem: A 31-year-old G2P1 with an uncomplicated MCDA twin pregnancy is reviewed at 35+4. Her previous birth was an uncomplicated spontaneous vaginal birth of a singleton at term. Both twins have grown along the 40th to 55th centiles with concordant growth, normal Dopplers, normal amniotic fluid, and no evidence of TTTS or sIUGR throughout the pregnancy. Both twins are cephalic on today's presentation scan. Estimated foetal weights are 2.45 kg and 2.38 kg. The cervix on speculum is closed; she has no contractions. Her booking BMI was 24. She has expressed a preference for vaginal birth and has no contraindications to labour. She has been counselled antenatally about the option of vaginal birth versus elective caesarean for diamniotic twins with a cephalic-cephalic presentation, and the unit has a 24-hour anaesthetic and theatre service with experienced operators for internal podalic version if the second twin changes lie after the first is born.
lead_in: Which delivery plan is most consistent with current RANZCOG and KEMH guidance?

options.t: Planned vaginal birth at 38 to 39 completed weeks of gestation
Planned vaginal birth between 36+0 and 36+6 weeks
Elective lower segment caesarean section at 36+0 weeks of gestation
Elective caesarean section at 32+0 weeks with corticosteroid cover
Elective caesarean at 37+0 weeks regardless of foetal presentation

subtopic_detail: Mode and timing of delivery - MCMA twins
stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 with a confirmed MCMA twin pregnancy is reviewed at 31+4. Booking confirmed a single placental mass, no inter-twin membrane on serial scans, and entangled cords on colour Doppler. Growth has tracked the 35th to 45th centiles concordantly, Dopplers are normal, and there is no evidence of TTTS. She has been an inpatient since 26 weeks for daily CTG monitoring. Both twins are presently non-cephalic on today's presentation scan. The cervix is closed at 32 mm on TVS. She received a complete course of antenatal corticosteroids at 26 weeks and an additional rescue course was given two weeks ago. She has received daily inpatient CTG surveillance without abnormal traces, no episodes of bleeding or rupture of membranes, and a recent maternal infection screen is unremarkable. The neonatal unit on site routinely supports infants from 28 weeks. She is keen to proceed with planned delivery once the team agrees timing.
lead_in: Which delivery plan is most appropriate?

options.t: Planned caesarean between 32+0 and 34+0 weeks after corticosteroids
Planned caesarean at 36+0 to 36+6 after steroids
Planned vaginal birth at 32+0 to 34+0 with continuous CTG
Continue inpatient monitoring and aim for vaginal birth at 37+0
Immediate caesarean today at 31+4 regardless of stability

stem: A 32-year-old G3P2 at 33+1 presents with painful tightenings every 6 minutes for 4 hours. She had a previous preterm birth at 31 weeks. She has no rupture of membranes or bleeding. Examination shows a cephalic presentation, cervix 1 cm dilated and 30 percent effaced; CTG is reactive. She has no maternal medical comorbidities and is on no regular medication. Her registrar suggests indomethacin tocolysis to allow corticosteroid effect, citing its rapid onset. She has no rupture of membranes, no antepartum haemorrhage and no fever. Maternal observations are normal, urine dipstick is clear and the foetal heart on continuous CTG is reactive with normal variability. Booking scans were unremarkable and a recent foetal anatomy review at 31 weeks was normal. The local maternity unit has nifedipine, atosiban, indomethacin and magnesium sulfate available. The team is balancing tocolytic options to allow corticosteroids to take effect, and the registrar has specifically asked about indomethacin given its rapid onset.
lead_in: Which is the strongest reason to avoid indomethacin in this patient?

options.t: Risk of foetal ductus arteriosus constriction and oligohydramnios at this advanced gestation
Substantially increased risk of maternal pulmonary oedema compared with other tocolytics
Indomethacin causes significant maternal hypotension via systemic vasodilation in pregnancy
Indomethacin is absolutely contraindicated whenever there is any prior preterm birth
Indomethacin significantly reduces lung-maturation efficacy of antenatal corticosteroids in pregnancy

stem: A 30-year-old G2P1 at 36+2 presents with regular contractions and a Bishop score of 8 with the cervix 3 cm dilated. The presentation is cephalic and CTG is reassuring. Her previous birth was at term. She has no other risk factors. The covering registrar asks whether antenatal corticosteroids should be administered before delivery. The pregnancy has been low-risk with appropriate growth and reassuring Dopplers throughout. She has had no episodes of bleeding, no rupture of membranes, no infections and no symptoms of pre-eclampsia. The unit offers routine intrapartum care from 36 weeks gestation on site with experienced neonatal support. The covering registrar is unfamiliar with the upper gestational limit for antenatal corticosteroid use and wants RANZCOG-aligned guidance before signing the order. She has no medical comorbidities, is on no regular medications and has no contraindication to corticosteroids. She has no contraindication to either betamethasone or dexamethasone.
lead_in: Which statement most accurately reflects current RANZCOG guidance?

options.t: Give betamethasone 11.4 mg IM now, then repeat in 24 hours, regardless of timing of birth
Do not give antenatal corticosteroids; her gestation is beyond the recommended window for routine use
Give dexamethasone 6 mg IM every 12 hours for four doses if birth is expected within 7 days
Give a single dose of betamethasone only if birth is genuinely expected within the next hour
Give a single rescue dose of dexamethasone 12 mg IM at her current gestation

stem: A 29-year-old G1P0 at 29+4 with a singleton pregnancy has progressed in spontaneous preterm labour despite atosiban tocolysis. She is now 7 cm dilated, contracting every 2 minutes, and birth is expected within the next 2 hours. She has had a complete course of betamethasone 36 hours ago. She has no contraindication to magnesium sulfate, has normal renal function, and no neuromuscular disease. She has no antepartum haemorrhage, no rupture of membranes, no fever and no symptoms of pre-eclampsia. The CTG is reactive and maternal observations are normal. Her booking BMI was 23, her booking bloods were unremarkable and she has not received magnesium sulfate at any earlier point in this pregnancy. The unit routinely uses MgSO4 for neonatal neuroprotection in line with RANZCOG and Australian Antenatal Magnesium Sulphate Neuroprotection guidance. Birth is now genuinely expected within 1 to 2 hours given her rapid cervical progress.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate plan for foetal neuroprotection?

options.t: Commence intravenous MgSO4 loading and maintenance now, aim for at least 4 hours before birth
Do not give magnesium sulfate, as it is only indicated in pre-eclampsia for seizure prophylaxis
Give a single intramuscular dose of magnesium sulfate now for foetal neuroprotection
Withhold magnesium sulfate because a complete course of antenatal corticosteroids has been given
Defer magnesium sulfate now as the gestation has progressed beyond 28 weeks

stem: A 33-year-old G2P1 at 22+0 with a singleton pregnancy attends for a planned TVS cervical length assessment because of one prior spontaneous preterm birth at 32 weeks. She has no symptoms today: no bleeding, no fluid loss, and no contractions. Anatomy scan was normal at 20+1. TVS today shows a cervix of 18 mm with no funnelling. Foetal fibronectin is not routinely used at her unit at this gestation. She has no contraindications to progesterone. Her previous birth was a spontaneous preterm vaginal birth at 32 weeks with no recognised infection, no antepartum bleeding and no overt cervical instrumentation. She has no other significant medical or obstetric history, takes no medications other than folate and iodine, and has no contraindications to vaginal progesterone. Her unit follows current RANZCOG guidance recommending vaginal progesterone in women with a singleton pregnancy and short cervix on TVS prior to viability.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate intervention?

options.t: Commence vaginal progesterone 200 mg nightly continued until 36 weeks gestation
Intramuscular 17-hydroxyprogesterone caproate weekly until 34 weeks gestation
Rescue cervical cerclage placement now via vaginal approach
Strict bedrest at home until 34 completed weeks gestation
Foetal fibronectin testing now to guide subsequent management

subtopic_detail: Foetal fibronectin in threatened preterm labour
stem: A 26-year-old G1P0 at 29+2 attends pregnancy assessment with intermittent abdominal tightening over 8 hours and pelvic pressure. She has no bleeding, no rupture of membranes, no fever, and a reassuring CTG. Examination shows no fluid pooling on speculum and the cervix appears long and closed; TVS measures the cervix at 22 mm. Quantitative foetal fibronectin (qfFN) returns at 12 ng/mL. She has no obstetric history of preterm birth and no infections. She has no past history of preterm birth, no late miscarriage, no cervical surgery and no documented uterine anomaly. There is no antepartum bleeding, no offensive vaginal discharge and a urine dipstick is unremarkable. The maternity unit uses quantitative foetal fibronectin combined with TVS cervical length as the basis for triage of suspected preterm labour, with a published unit cut-off of 50 ng/mL above which admission and steroids are considered.
lead_in: Which management approach best reflects current evidence?

options.t: Reassure, discharge home with clear safety-netting advice and outpatient follow-up
Admit, give a course of antenatal corticosteroids and commence tocolysis
Admit and commence prophylactic magnesium sulfate for foetal neuroprotection
Repeat quantitative foetal fibronectin in 24 hours and admit until then
Insertion of emergency rescue cervical cerclage now in theatre

stem: A 31-year-old G1P0 at 37+1 with well-controlled gestational diabetes on diet alone is reviewed at her routine antenatal clinic. Foetal growth is on the 50th centile, amniotic fluid is normal, CTG is reactive, and self-monitored capillary glucose has stayed within target. She has no hypertension, no IUGR, and no other complication. She has booked into a private hospital and asks whether elective induction at 37 to 38 weeks would be safer than waiting.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate plan?

options.t: Continue routine antenatal care with planned delivery around 40+0 to 40+6 weeks
Elective induction of labour at 37+0 weeks gestation now
Elective lower segment caesarean section at 38+0 weeks of gestation
Planned induction at 39+0 with cervical ripening regardless of Bishop
Defer delivery until 41+5 weeks with daily CTG from 39 weeks

stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 at 39+3 presents 2 hours after a gush of clear fluid followed by ongoing leakage. Not contracting; speculum confirms a pool of clear amniotic fluid and a closed soft cervix. CTG reactive. GBS swab at 36 weeks was POSITIVE. She has no penicillin allergy. Booking BMI 24, otherwise uncomplicated pregnancy. She asks about waiting at home for spontaneous labour; she has no childcare for her older child and would prefer to wait if it is safe.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate plan in this Australian setting?

options.t: Admit for immediate induction of labour and commence intrapartum IV benzylpenicillin GBS prophylaxis now
Admit for expectant management up to 24 hours with no antibiotics unless fever develops
Wait at home for spontaneous labour for up to 5 days with daily review
Mandatory immediate lower-segment caesarean to prevent ascending infection
Commence oral erythromycin to prolong latency to delivery at term

stem: A 29-year-old G2P2 attends her 6-week postnatal review. She delivered vaginally and is exclusively breastfeeding on demand, day and night, no formula or solids. She has had no return of menstruation since delivery, and a urine beta-hCG is negative today. She and her partner do not wish to conceive again for at least 2 years. She has heard friends say 'breastfeeding stops periods so I don't need contraception' and asks whether that is true. She has no UKMEC category 3-4 contraindications.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate advice and recommendation?

options.t: Counsel that LAM provides about 98% efficacy only up to 6 months post partum and only while she is amenorrhoeic and exclusively/near-exclusively breastfeeding; offer a parallel reliable method (LARC or POP) to avoid the gap when LAM expires
Reassure that breastfeeding alone provides reliable contraception for up to 12 months post partum
Reassure that any breastfeeding plus amenorrhoea provides reliable contraception regardless of feeding pattern or solids
Counsel that LAM is reliable until 6 months only if one normal menstrual period has occurred to confirm cycles have restarted
Recommend commencing the combined oral contraceptive pill today as the most reliable bridging method

stem: A 32-year-old G3P3 woman presents to the emergency department on day 4 following an emergency lower segment caesarean section performed at 39+2 for failure to progress. She received cefazolin 2 g IV at skin incision intraoperatively. Over the last 24 hours she has developed fevers to 38.7 degC with rigors, lower abdominal pain, and increasingly offensive lochia which has changed from rubra to brown with a foul odour. She is breastfeeding with no breast engorgement, no breast erythema, no localised breast tenderness, no dysuria, no flank pain, no calf pain or swelling, and her caesarean wound looks clean and dry with no surrounding cellulitis. On examination her uterus is tender on bimanual palpation and is 4 cm above the symphysis. Bedside ultrasound shows an empty uterine cavity with no retained products. Cultures including blood, urine and high vaginal swab have been collected.
lead_in: Which empirical intravenous antibiotic regimen is most appropriate according to current Australian Therapeutic Guidelines?

options.t: Ceftriaxone 1 g IV daily as monotherapy without any anaerobic or aminoglycoside cover today
Flucloxacillin 2 g IV every 6 hours plus metronidazole 500 mg IV every 12 hours
Ampicillin 2 g IV every 6 hours plus gentamicin 5 mg/kg IV daily plus metronidazole 500 mg IV 12-hourly
Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV every 8 hours as broad-spectrum monotherapy without aminoglycoside or aerobic cover today
Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg twice daily and discharge home today with GP follow-up tomorrow in clinic

stem: A 28-year-old G1P1 exclusively breastfeeding her 3-week-old infant presents with a 36-hour history of a painful hot wedge of erythema in the upper outer right breast, fever 38.4, rigors, and infant feeding poorly from the affected side. Examination: 6 cm wedge erythema, warm, tender, NO fluctuance. Bedside USS shows no abscess. She has continued frequent feeding and warm compresses without improvement. Importantly, she has a documented IgE-mediated immediate hypersensitivity to penicillin (rash, urticaria and lip swelling 30 minutes after amoxicillin 5 years ago, confirmed by allergy clinic).
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line oral antibiotic regimen?

options.t: Flucloxacillin 500 mg orally every 6 hours for 5 days
Cefalexin 500 mg orally every 6 hours for 5 days
Clindamycin 450 mg orally every 8 hours for 5-10 days
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 160/800 mg orally every 12 hours for 7 days
Continue conservative measures alone for a further 48 hours

subtopic_detail: Postpartum VTE thromboprophylaxis - RANZCOG risk-stratified dosing
stem: A 34-year-old G3P3 woman has just had an emergency caesarean section at 38+5 weeks for foetal distress. Her pre-pregnancy BMI was 36 and her booking weight was 102 kg; she now weighs 108 kg on day 1 postpartum. She has a past history of one provoked deep vein thrombosis 8 years ago after a long-haul flight, was treated with warfarin for 6 months at that time, and has been off anticoagulation since. Her current pregnancy was uncomplicated apart from gestational diabetes managed with diet. She is a non-smoker, has no thrombophilia testing on record, no family history of unprovoked VTE, and her observations are stable. The estimated blood loss at caesarean was 600 mL with no further bleeding postoperatively. Her platelet count is 198 and her creatinine is 62 micromol/L. She is mobilising with help and is keen to breastfeed.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate postpartum thromboprophylaxis regimen?

options.t: Mechanical prophylaxis (TEDS plus early mobilisation) alone with no pharmacological prophylaxis
Enoxaparin 20 mg subcutaneously daily for 10 days as postpartum thromboprophylaxis
Enoxaparin 80 mg subcutaneously daily for 10 days only as postpartum prophylaxis
Enoxaparin 80 mg subcutaneously daily for 6 weeks as postpartum thromboprophylaxis
Warfarin 5 mg daily for 6 weeks targeting INR 2.0 to 3.0 with bridging LMWH

subtopic_detail: Postnatal contraception - timing of method initiation in a partially breastfeeding woman
stem: A 33-year-old G2P2 woman attends a postnatal review at 5 weeks following an uncomplicated spontaneous vaginal birth at 39+4 weeks gestation. She is partially breastfeeding (approximately 60% breast milk, 40% formula because she returns to her finance role in 4 weeks and the workplace lacks lactation facilities) and has had no return of menses since delivery. Her booking BMI was 31, she is a lifelong non-smoker, her blood pressure today is 132/82 mmHg, and her booking and 36-week antenatal BP readings were both normotensive. She has no personal or family history of VTE or thrombophilia, no migraine with or without aura, no current or prior breast cancer, no hepatic disease, no diabetes, and no autoimmune disease. She and her partner want effective contraception starting as soon as possible today and. She is comfortable returning at any time for a procedure if that is the most appropriate option after counselling.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate contraceptive option to initiate today?

options.t: Combined oral contraceptive pill containing ethinyloestradiol 30 microg/levonorgestrel 150 microg, starting today
Defer all contraception until 6 weeks postpartum and rely on LAM in the interim
Insertion of a copper intrauterine device at 5 weeks postpartum after speculum examination
Insertion of a 52 mg levonorgestrel intrauterine system today at 5 weeks postpartum
Etonogestrel 68 mg subdermal implant inserted today during the consultation

subtopic_detail: Obstetric anal sphincter injury repair - principles of management
stem: A 26-year-old G1P1 woman has just delivered a 4.1 kg infant by spontaneous vaginal birth with a 30-minute active second stage. The delivery was attended by a midwife with an obstetric registrar called for shoulder dystocia which was resolved with McRoberts and suprapubic pressure within 90 seconds. There was a head-to-body interval of 80 seconds. After delivery of the placenta, careful inspection of the perineum demonstrates a tear extending through the perineal skin, perineal muscles, and partial-thickness laceration of the external anal sphincter involving approximately 40% of its thickness; the internal anal sphincter and rectal mucosa appear intact on careful per rectum examination. The registrar grades the injury as 3a. Maternal observations are stable, the infant is in good condition with Apgars 9 and 9, and the estimated blood loss is 350 mL with no ongoing bleeding. The hospital has a theatre available now and a senior obstetrician on site.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management approach for repair and after-care?

options.t: Repair on the labour ward bed under local anaesthesia using 2-0 polyglactin (Vicryl) for all layers, no postoperative antibiotics, encourage normal diet, no physiotherapy referral
Repair on the ward without anaesthesia, give one course of oral metronidazole, no laxative and no perineal clinic follow-up

Repair in theatre under regional or general anaesthesia, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics intraoperatively then oral course, lactulose for 10 days, pelvic floor physiotherapy referral, perineal clinic follow-up
Repair in theatre, oral cephalexin only, no stool softener (constipation will protect the repair), no physiotherapy referral, GP review at 6 weeks
Defer repair for 24 hours to allow tissue oedema to settle, then repair in theatre, no antibiotics, lactulose for 3 days, physiotherapy referral

subtopic_detail: Postpartum iron deficiency anaemia - transfusion vs IV iron vs oral iron thresholds
stem: A 28-year-old G2P2 on day 2 post-vaginal-birth (PPH 1100 mL controlled with uterotonics and tranexamic acid; no transfusion) feels tired but not breathless, ambulates without symptoms, breastfeeds well. Booking Hb 124 g/L. Today: Hb 78 g/L, MCV 76, ferritin 12 mcg/L, transferrin saturation 9%, platelets 240, normal renal function. No active bleeding, no chest pain, no syncope. She wishes to be discharged tomorrow to care for her toddler. Local protocol follows the National Blood Authority Patient Blood Management Module 5 (Obstetrics).
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management of her postpartum iron deficiency anaemia?

options.t: Single dose IV ferric carboxymaltose 1000 mg before discharge with review at 4 weeks
Transfuse 2 units of packed red cells before discharge
Oral ferrous sulphate 325 mg three times daily for 3 months
Oral ferrous fumarate 200 mg three times daily with no scheduled follow-up
Erythropoietin-stimulating agent subcutaneously now plus oral iron for 3 months

subtopic_detail: Postpartum urinary retention - identification and management
stem: A 24-year-old G1P1 woman is reviewed 7 hours after a forceps-assisted vaginal birth at 40+1 with a mediolateral episiotomy. She had an epidural sited in labour and her last top-up was 5 hours ago; sensation in her legs has now fully returned. The estimated blood loss was 450 mL and her perineal tear is a grade 2 episiotomy repair with no extension into the sphincter complex. She last passed urine spontaneously 9 hours before the start of pushing, which was 11 hours ago in total. She has been encouraged to attempt voiding three times over the last 4 hours but each attempt has produced less than 50 mL despite a sensation of fullness. Bladder ultrasound at the bedside shows 720 mL of urine in the bladder. She has had no haematuria, no flank pain, no abdominal distension causing pain, and her observations are stable. She is keen to mobilise to the shower and start skin-to-skin.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.t: Encourage further oral fluids now and then re-scan her bladder again in 4 hours
Insert in-out catheter, drain bladder once, then discharge to postnatal ward without further plan
Refer her urgently to urology for cystoscopy under general anaesthesia today in theatre setting
Administer IV furosemide 20 mg and then re-scan her bladder again in 1 hour
Insert indwelling urinary catheter on free drainage for 24-48 hours, then trial of void with bladder scans

subtopic_detail: Postpartum thyroiditis - biphasic course and management
stem: A 33-year-old G2P2 woman presents to her general practitioner 4 months postpartum with a 6-week history of palpitations, heat intolerance, tremor, anxiety and a 3 kg weight loss despite an increased appetite. She is breastfeeding her infant exclusively. She has no neck pain, no recent viral illness, no goitre on examination, no eye signs, no family history of thyroid disease, and no past psychiatric history. Her pregnancy was uncomplicated, the delivery was a spontaneous vaginal birth at 39+4, and she had no obstetric or postpartum complications. Her bloods today show TSH less than 0.01 mIU/L, free T4 32 pmol/L, free T3 9.2 pmol/L, anti-thyroid peroxidase antibodies strongly positive at 480 IU/mL, and thyroid receptor (TRAb) antibodies negative. A radioiodine uptake scan would be contraindicated because of breastfeeding but a thyroid ultrasound shows a diffusely heterogeneous gland with reduced vascularity on Doppler.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management of her current thyrotoxic phase?

options.t: Carbimazole 30 mg orally daily, titrated to repeat thyroid function tests every 4 weeks
Radioiodine ablation as soon as breastfeeding can be temporarily ceased entirely
Propranolol 10-40 mg three times daily for symptom control, monitoring thyroid function every 6-8 weeks
Levothyroxine 50 microg orally daily and review her thyroid function tests in 8 weeks
Lugol iodine and oral dexamethasone for treatment of thyroid storm now

subtopic_detail: Postpartum fever differential - hooks for endometritis, wound infection, mastitis, UTI, DVT/PE
stem: A 27-year-old G1P1 woman presents to the postnatal ward on day 5 following an elective lower segment caesarean section at 39+0 for breech presentation. She has had three temperatures recorded above 38 degC over the last 12 hours with associated rigors. Her caesarean wound is closed with absorbable sutures and on inspection appears clean, dry and intact without erythema, fluctuance or discharge. She is breastfeeding her infant from both breasts; both breasts feel soft, are not erythematous and have no localised tenderness or fluctuance. Her uterus is mildly tender on palpation in the lower abdomen and her lochia is heavier than expected and faintly offensive in odour. She has no flank pain, no dysuria, no frequency, and a urinalysis is negative for leucocytes and nitrites. Her calves are soft, non-tender and symmetrical with no swelling. She is not breathless and her SpO2 is 99% on room air. Bloods show WCC 16.4 with neutrophilia and CRP 124.
lead_in: Which is the most likely diagnosis given the bedside findings?

options.t: Caesarean wound infection with cellulitis and fever
Lactational mastitis with breast abscess and fever
Urinary tract infection with pyelonephritis and fever
Postpartum endometritis with secondary bacteraemia and fever
Deep vein thrombosis with low-grade thrombotic fever

stem: A 32-year-old woman attends her GP for contraception. She is a non-smoker, BMI 24, BP 122/76 mmHg, with regular 28-day cycles and a normal cervical screening 12 months ago. Over the past year she has had three episodes of unilateral throbbing headache lasting 24 hours, each preceded by a 20-minute episode of zigzag visual lines in one visual field followed by partial right-sided pins and needles. Headaches are now occurring monthly. There is no personal or family history of venous thromboembolism, ischaemic heart disease, breast cancer or liver disease. She is not breastfeeding. She has heard friends recommend the combined oral contraceptive pill and asks specifically about that option.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in contraceptive counselling?

options.t: Commence a combined oral contraceptive pill with 20 mcg ethinyloestradiol to minimise vascular risk
Commence a combined oral contraceptive pill with 30 mcg ethinyloestradiol and levonorgestrel
Recommend a progestogen-only or non-hormonal method as combined hormonal contraception is contraindicated
Continue current condom-only contraception and only review if she develops new neurological deficit
Refer to neurology before any contraceptive can be safely commenced

stem: A 29-year-old nulligravid woman presents with 7 months of secondary amenorrhoea, intermittent bilateral galactorrhoea and reduced libido. She has had no recent pregnancy or breastfeeding, no headaches and no visual symptoms. She started risperidone 4 mg daily 9 months ago for chronic schizoaffective disorder, prescribed by her psychiatrist, and is responding well with no recent admissions. She takes no other regular medications and uses no recreational drugs. BMI 24, BP 116/70. Visual fields are full to confrontation. Bloods: beta-hCG negative, repeat mid-morning serum prolactin 1900 mIU/L (reference <500) on two occasions one week apart, TSH and free T4 normal, U+Es normal. She is keen to conceive in the next 12 months.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Request urgent MRI pituitary with gadolinium before any further investigation
Liaise with the treating psychiatrist about switching to a prolactin-sparing antipsychotic such as aripiprazole, then re-check prolactin
Commence cabergoline 0.25 mg twice weekly empirically and reassess prolactin in 6 weeks
Macroprolactin screen with polyethylene glycol precipitation as the next test
Stop risperidone abruptly without psychiatric consultation to allow prolactin to normalise

stem: A 32-year-old woman is reviewed 8 days after IVF oocyte retrieval (21 oocytes). She reports 48 hours of worsening abdominal distension, nausea and reduced oral intake. BP 108/64, HR 108, RR 18, SpO2 98% on room air. Abdomen distended, tense ascites palpable to the costal margin, mild generalised tenderness without peritonism. Pelvic ultrasound: ovaries 11 cm right and 10 cm left with multiple cysts; large-volume ascites extending above the umbilicus; small right pleural effusion. Bloods: haematocrit 0.48 (baseline 0.38), WCC 13.2, sodium 132, potassium 4.6, creatinine 96 micromol/L (baseline 62), albumin 27 g/L, ALT 78 U/L, urine output 25 mL/h for the past 4 hours.
lead_in: Which classification and management plan best fit her ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome?

options.t: Moderate OHSS: discharge home with daily phone review and oral fluids
Severe OHSS: admit for IV crystalloid, VTE prophylaxis with LMWH, strict fluid balance and consider paracentesis
Critical OHSS: transfer to ICU now for invasive ventilation and renal replacement
Severe OHSS: admit but give large-volume normal saline boluses until haematocrit normalises
Severe OHSS: admit but withhold thromboprophylaxis until haemoconcentration resolves

stem: A 38-year-old woman and her 40-year-old male partner have been trying to conceive for 18 months. The woman has regular ovulatory cycles, mid-luteal progesterone confirms ovulation, AMH 6 pmol/L (low for age), antral follicle count 6, TSH and prolactin normal, BMI 24, HyCoSy confirmed bilateral tubal patency. The partner has had two normal semen analyses by WHO 2021 criteria 4 weeks apart. Neither has had pelvic surgery, endometriosis or STI. They are non-smokers and drink within recommended limits. They have not had any prior pregnancies. They ask which ART pathway gives the highest live-birth chance given her age and ovarian reserve.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Continue timed intercourse for a further 12 months before referral to a fertility specialist
Proceed to in vitro fertilisation given her age and low ovarian reserve
Three cycles of intrauterine insemination with clomiphene before considering IVF
Commence clomiphene citrate alone with timed intercourse for six cycles
Recommend donor oocyte IVF as her age and reserve preclude autologous IVF success

stem: A 38-year-old G2P2 woman has 9 months of progressively heavy menstrual bleeding and intermenstrual spotting. Hb 102 g/L (microcytic) responsive to iron, TSH and beta-hCG normal, endometrial biopsy benign, cervical screening up to date. Pelvic ultrasound and saline-infusion sonography demonstrate a single 3 cm fibroid lying entirely within the endometrial cavity on a narrow stalk (FIGO type 0), with no other fibroids and a normal myometrium. She has completed her family, wishes to preserve her uterus, and prefers the most targeted intervention with the fastest return to normal activity. She has no contraindication to anaesthesia.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate treatment for her fibroid-related heavy menstrual bleeding?

options.t: Hysteroscopic resection of the submucosal fibroid
Uterine artery embolisation by interventional radiology
Levonorgestrel intrauterine system (Mirena) insertion at next routine appointment
Total abdominal hysterectomy as definitive treatment
Three-month course of GnRH agonist (goserelin) as definitive management

subtopic_detail: Infertility - couple workup timing and content
stem: A 36-year-old woman and her 38-year-old male partner attend the GP after 8 months of regular unprotected intercourse without conception. She has had regular 28 to 30 day cycles since menarche, no dysmenorrhoea, no abnormal bleeding and no pelvic pain. She has had no pregnancies and her partner has no children from previous relationships. Her past history includes appendicectomy at age 16 without complications. She has had no pelvic infections, no chlamydia exposure, and her cervical screening is up to date. Her partner has no chronic disease, takes no regular medications, is a non-smoker and works as a teacher. They have intercourse 2 to 3 times per week. BMI 23 (female) and 25 (male). She is on folic acid 0.5 mg daily. Pelvic examination is unremarkable. They are aware of her age and ask what investigations should be commenced now rather than waiting to the conventional 12-month threshold for younger couples.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.t: Reassure the couple and review in 4 months if there is no conception
Commence parallel investigation: semen analysis, mid-luteal progesterone, ovarian reserve and tubal patency
Order AMH and pelvic ultrasound only and defer semen analysis until a second visit
Refer immediately for IVF without baseline investigations given her age
Diagnostic laparoscopy first to exclude endometriosis as a contributing factor

subtopic_detail: Ovarian reserve - AMH and AFC interpretation
stem: A 34-year-old nulligravid woman is being assessed prior to commencement of IVF for tubal factor infertility (bilateral tubal occlusion confirmed on HyCoSy and laparoscopy). She has regular 28-day cycles with confirmed ovulation, BMI 22, normal TSH and prolactin, and her partner has a normal semen analysis. Her AMH is 4 pmol/L (low for age) and her antral follicle count is 5 (total both ovaries). Day-3 FSH is 11 IU/L with oestradiol 180 pmol/L. She has had no chemotherapy, no pelvic surgery beyond diagnostic laparoscopy, no autoimmune disease and no family history of premature ovarian insufficiency. She is anxious about the implications of these results and asks what they mean for her IVF cycle planning and her chance of natural conception. She has no menopausal symptoms and her BP today is normal. She is anxious about the implications for her treatment and asks specifically what these numbers mean for her IVF stimulation response.
lead_in: Which statement most accurately interprets her ovarian reserve markers in the context of planning IVF?

options.t: Low AMH and AFC predict poor ovarian response and low oocyte yield, not per-cycle spontaneous conception
AMH below 5 pmol/L confirms premature ovarian insufficiency, so IVF with her own oocytes is inappropriate
Her elevated day-3 FSH means she will achieve a high oocyte yield on standard stimulation protocols
AMH and AFC accurately predict the absolute number of years remaining until menopause for an individual
An antral follicle count above 4 excludes POSEIDON poor-responder classification regardless of the AMH

stem: A 37-year-old woman presents to her GP after 8 months of unprotected intercourse without conception. Her partner has had a normal semen analysis. Her cycles are 26 to 28 days and she has confirmed ovulation on mid-luteal progesterone. The GP arranges day-3 cycle bloods which return as follows: FSH 9 IU/L, LH 5 IU/L, oestradiol 350 pmol/L (high), AMH 6 pmol/L, TSH 1.8 mIU/L, prolactin within reference range. AFC is 7 total. She has no abnormal bleeding, no significant dysmenorrhoea, no hot flushes and no medications. Pelvic ultrasound shows a normal-sized uterus, no fibroids, normal-appearing ovaries with the AFC noted, and no endometrioma. She has had no chemotherapy, no pelvic surgery, no smoking and no autoimmune disease. She asks how to interpret the day-3 FSH given that it appears reassuring. Her BMI is 23 and she is otherwise well, with no symptoms suggesting menopause and no contraindication to ovulation induction.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate interpretation of these ovarian reserve markers?

options.t: Day-3 FSH within reference range reliably excludes diminished ovarian reserve in this patient
Elevated day-3 oestradiol may falsely suppress FSH; reserve is better assessed by AMH and AFC here
Her LH-to-FSH ratio of less than 1 confirms PCOS as the dominant cause of her subfertility
An AMH of 6 pmol/L with AFC of 7 confirms premature ovarian insufficiency, excluding IVF with own oocytes
Repeating day-3 bloods next cycle without changing strategy will give a definitive answer

stem: A 25-year-old nulligravid woman has just been diagnosed with PCOS by her GP (Rotterdam criteria: oligomenorrhoea, biochemical hyperandrogenism with elevated free androgen index, polycystic ovarian morphology on ultrasound with 24 follicles per ovary). Her exclusion bloods (TSH, prolactin, 17-OHP, beta-hCG) were all normal. She has acne, mild hirsutism (Ferriman-Gallwey 9) and recent 5 kg weight gain over 12 months. BMI 29. Family history: mother with type 2 diabetes diagnosed at 52, paternal uncle with myocardial infarction at age 60. She has no contraindications to combined hormonal contraception. She is not planning pregnancy in the next 2 years. Her GP plans the recommended metabolic screen at diagnosis per the 2023 international PCOS guideline. Blood pressure today is 124/78 mmHg. She has no symptoms of obstructive sleep apnoea, no chest pain and no peripheral oedema, and her cardiovascular examination today is unremarkable. The GP is using the 2023 international evidence-based PCOS guideline as the framework for baseline assessment.
lead_in: Which combination of investigations represents the most appropriate metabolic screen at the time of PCOS diagnosis in this woman?

options.t: Fasting lipid panel and a 75 g oral glucose tolerance test plus blood pressure measurement
Fasting glucose and HbA1c alone are sufficient screening in women under 35 with PCOS
Annual fasting insulin level to monitor for insulin resistance and guide treatment
Cardiac MRI with stress testing given her family history of cardiovascular disease at this age
Liver biopsy because PCOS is associated with non-alcoholic fatty liver disease

stem: A 28-year-old nulligravid woman has been on continuous combined oral contraceptive pill (30 mcg ethinyloestradiol + levonorgestrel) plus naproxen as needed for 8 months for clinically suspected endometriosis. Her cyclical pelvic pain has not improved (still requires time off work 2 days each month with pain rated 7/10), her deep dyspareunia persists, and a repeat transvaginal ultrasound now demonstrates a 3 cm right ovarian endometrioma not previously seen. She has had no abnormal bleeding, no new bowel or bladder symptoms, no pregnancy plans within the next 2 years and no contraindications to hormonal therapy. She uses condoms with her long-term male partner. Beta-hCG is negative. She has no family history of breast cancer or VTE, no smoking and BMI 23. She is willing to continue medical therapy but wants more effective symptom relief; she remains reluctant to undergo surgery in the short term.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.t: Switch to LNG-IUS or trial a 3-6 month GnRH analogue with add-back HRT
Stop hormonal suppression and proceed to abdominal hysterectomy with bilateral oophorectomy
Add tranexamic acid 1 g three times daily during menses to current therapy
Empirical IVF using fresh embryo transfer for presumed endometriosis-related infertility
Continue current therapy and add long-acting oxycodone for breakthrough cyclical pain

stem: A 34-year-old G0 woman is referred with 14 months of secondary subfertility and a 9-month history of progressively heavy menstrual bleeding requiring iron supplementation (Hb 108 g/L on presentation, now 118 g/L). Her cycles are regular and ovulatory. Her partner has a normal semen analysis and her tubes are patent on HyCoSy. Transvaginal ultrasound and MRI both demonstrate a single 5 cm submucosal fibroid that is FIGO type 2 (>=50% intramural component, projecting into the endometrial cavity and distorting it) and two further small subserosal fibroids that do not distort the cavity. Her endometrial biopsy is benign. She has no other gynaecological pathology, no contraindications to surgery, no chronic disease, and is actively trying to conceive. She has discussed expectant management and medical options with her clinician and would prefer a definitive intervention that addresses both her heavy bleeding and improves her chance of conception.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.t: Hysteroscopic myomectomy as a fertility-sparing intervention addressing both HMB and subfertility
Levonorgestrel intrauterine system insertion as definitive treatment for both subfertility and HMB
Uterine artery embolisation to reduce fibroid volume and HMB while preserving the uterus
Total abdominal hysterectomy as definitive management for both fibroid bulk and HMB
GnRH analogue with add-back HRT as long-term medical therapy for HMB and subfertility

subtopic_detail: POI - bone and cardiovascular protection plan
stem: A 34-year-old woman has been formally diagnosed with premature ovarian insufficiency: 6 months of secondary amenorrhoea, FSH 56 IU/L and 62 IU/L on samples 5 weeks apart with low oestradiol, negative beta-hCG, normal TSH and normal prolactin. She has hot flushes, vaginal dryness with deep dyspareunia and disturbed sleep. Family history: her mother had menopause at 41 and a paternal aunt is on bisphosphonate therapy for osteoporosis. She has had no chemotherapy, no pelvic radiotherapy and no autoimmune disease known to date. Karyotype is pending and fragile X premutation screen, adrenal antibodies and thyroid antibodies have been ordered. She has no contraindications to oestrogen (no breast cancer, no current VTE, no migraine with aura). BMI 23. BP 118/74. She is in a long-term partnership, not currently planning pregnancy, but is open to consideration of IVF with donor oocytes in the future. A baseline DXA is planned.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate hormone management strategy for bone and cardiovascular protection?

options.t: Standard-dose physiological oestradiol with cyclical progestogen or LNG-IUS until the average age of menopause
Bisphosphonate therapy as first-line bone protection without any concurrent hormone therapy
Combined oral contraceptive pill is equivalent to HRT and may be continued lifelong
Defer all hormone therapy and review symptoms again in 6 months
Topical vaginal oestrogen alone as the primary hormone management strategy for this woman

stem: A 37-year-old G2P2 woman attends her GP for contraceptive review. She has been on a combined oral contraceptive pill (30 mcg ethinyloestradiol with levonorgestrel) for the past 4 years. At today's review her blood pressure is 162/96 mmHg, confirmed on a repeat reading 15 minutes later at 158/94 mmHg. Home BP readings over the past 2 weeks have averaged 156/94 mmHg. She has no headaches, no visual disturbance, no chest pain and no peripheral oedema. Urinalysis is negative for protein and blood. She has no history of stroke, transient ischaemic attack, ischaemic heart disease, VTE or migraine with aura. She is a non-smoker, BMI 31, has otherwise normal bloods (renal function, electrolytes, lipids), and no diabetes (HbA1c 5.4%). She has completed her family and would like ongoing reliable contraception. She is reluctant to use barrier methods alone. She has no postnatal depression or mood disorder and her family history is otherwise unremarkable.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate contraceptive recommendation?

options.t: Continue the current combined oral contraceptive pill and re-check blood pressure in 3 months
Switch to a progestogen-only method such as the LNG-IUS, POP, etonogestrel implant or DMPA
Switch to a transdermal combined hormonal patch as this avoids the first-pass hepatic metabolism
Add an antihypertensive and continue the combined oral contraceptive at a lower oestrogen dose
Recommend tubal ligation as the only acceptable contraceptive option for hypertensive women over 35

stem: A 34-year-old G2P2 woman attends her GP for contraceptive review. She was diagnosed 14 months ago with stage II oestrogen-receptor-positive, progesterone-receptor-positive, HER2-negative invasive ductal carcinoma of the right breast. She had wide local excision plus axillary node sampling (1 of 3 nodes positive), adjuvant chemotherapy and radiotherapy, and commenced tamoxifen 20 mg daily 6 months ago which she plans to continue for at least 5 to 10 years. She has been amenorrhoeic since chemotherapy but recent bloods show a partial return of menses and an FSH of 18 IU/L with oestradiol 280 pmol/L, suggesting recovery of ovarian function. She uses condoms intermittently with her long-term male partner. She has completed her family. She is anxious to avoid pregnancy because she does not wish to interrupt her endocrine therapy and is aware of the teratogenicity of tamoxifen. She has no other major comorbidity. BMI 24, BP 118/74.
lead_in: Which contraceptive method is most appropriate for this woman?

options.t: Copper intrauterine device (Cu-IUD) inserted at the next available family planning visit
Levonorgestrel intrauterine system (Mirena) inserted at the next available appointment
Combined oral contraceptive pill at the lowest effective oestrogen dose available
Etonogestrel subdermal contraceptive implant (Implanon NXT) for 3 years
Depot medroxyprogesterone acetate 150 mg IM every 12 weeks

stem: A 31-year-old G1P1 woman with systemic lupus erythematosus (diagnosed 6 years ago) attends for contraceptive review. Her disease has been clinically and serologically stable for 18 months on hydroxychloroquine and low-dose prednisolone (5 mg daily). She has previously had two episodes of cutaneous lupus and one episode of mild lupus nephritis treated with mycophenolate, now in remission. Her most recent immunology shows positive lupus anticoagulant on two occasions 12 weeks apart, anticardiolipin IgG strongly positive, and anti-beta-2-glycoprotein-I positive (triple positive antiphospholipid profile). She has no personal history of venous thromboembolism, stroke or ischaemic heart disease and no obstetric morbidity but is high risk for VTE. BP 120/76, BMI 25, non-smoker. She has completed her family, uses condoms inconsistently and would like a more reliable method. She is concerned about both medication interactions with hydroxychloroquine and the impact of contraception on her lupus and VTE risk.
lead_in: Which contraceptive method is most appropriate for this woman?

options.t: Combined oral contraceptive pill with the lowest available oestrogen dose to minimise VTE risk
Levonorgestrel intrauterine system (LNG-IUS) inserted at the next available appointment
Combined hormonal contraceptive vaginal ring as it avoids first-pass hepatic metabolism
Depot medroxyprogesterone acetate 150 mg IM every 12 weeks for ongoing contraception
Continue barrier contraception alone (condoms) given the complexity of medication interactions

stem: A 31-year-old G2P2 woman attends for contraceptive review 7 months after Roux-en-Y gastric bypass surgery. She has lost 28 kg (BMI now 32, down from 42 pre-operatively). She has not yet resumed menses since surgery and reports breast tenderness, fatigue and intermittent nausea, which she attributes to her vitamin and mineral supplementation. A urine beta-hCG today is negative. Her past medical history includes well-controlled hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg daily (BP today 128/78) and previous gestational diabetes (no current diabetes; HbA1c 5.5%). She uses condoms inconsistently with her long-term male partner. Pre-operatively she was on the combined oral contraceptive pill which was stopped 6 weeks before surgery as part of the VTE risk minimisation protocol. She is fully aware that bariatric surgery is associated with rapid return of fertility and that pregnancy is recommended to be deferred for 12 to 24 months post-operatively. She wishes to use a highly effective method until she is ready for a third pregnancy in 18 months.
lead_in: Which contraceptive method is most appropriate for this woman?

options.t: Levonorgestrel intrauterine system (Mirena), copper IUD or etonogestrel subdermal implant
Combined oral contraceptive pill at standard dose with continued condom backup
Progestogen-only pill taken daily with a 3-hour mistiming window
Fertility awareness method timed to predicted ovulation each cycle
Recommend immediate pregnancy attempts given strong weight loss and improving metabolic profile

subtopic_detail: Vaginal discharge - microscopy and Amsel recall
stem: A 27-year-old woman presents with 2 weeks of thin grey-white vaginal discharge with a fishy odour, worse after intercourse. She has no vulval itch, no dysuria, no abnormal bleeding and no fever. She is in a monogamous relationship of 18 months using a copper IUD, last cervical screening 14 months ago was normal. Speculum: thin homogeneous off-white discharge coating the vaginal walls without inflammation. Vaginal pH at the lateral wall 5.2; KOH whiff test positive; wet mount shows clue cells, scant lactobacilli, no motile trichomonads, no pseudohyphae. She is not currently pregnant. She had a similar episode 5 months ago which was treated with a 7-day course of oral metronidazole 400 mg twice daily; she completed the course, and symptoms resolved for 4 months before recurring.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Repeat the same 7-day oral metronidazole 400 mg twice daily course
Treat with oral metronidazole 400 mg bd for 7 days, then commence intravaginal metronidazole gel twice weekly for 4-6 months as suppressive therapy
Reassure that this is normal discharge and no treatment is needed
Empirically treat both her and her male partner with metronidazole
Remove the copper IUD as it is the underlying cause of recurrent BV

stem: A 23-year-old woman presents to a sexual health clinic with a 48-hour history of painful vulval ulceration, dysuria so severe she is voiding in the shower, and tender bilateral inguinal lymphadenopathy. She has had one new male partner over the past two months and has never had similar symptoms before. She is otherwise well, BMI 22, not pregnant (urine beta-hCG negative today), takes the combined oral contraceptive pill, has no immunosuppression and is fully vaccinated. On examination there are multiple shallow tender vesicles and ulcers on bilateral labia minora and the introitus with surrounding erythema, and several smaller vesicles on the perineum. There is no cervical motion tenderness on speculum examination, no abnormal vaginal discharge, and no urinary retention requiring catheterisation at this point. A swab from a deroofed vesicle is sent for HSV PCR and type-specific HSV-1/HSV-2 serology has been requested. Renal function is normal, she has no allergies, and her last sexual health screen six months ago was negative for chlamydia, gonorrhoea, syphilis and HIV. She asks how the clinician will treat this presumed first episode while results are pending.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate empirical antiviral regimen for this first clinical episode of genital herpes?

options.t: Aciclovir 200 mg orally five times daily for 3 days
Aciclovir 400 mg orally three times daily for 5 to 10 days
Aciclovir 400 mg orally twice daily continuously as suppressive therapy
Valaciclovir 500 mg orally twice daily for 3 days
Topical aciclovir 5% cream five times daily for 5 days

stem: A 21-year-old woman at 18 weeks of pregnancy presents with multiple soft non-keratinised fleshy lesions on the vulva and perianal area, first noticed 6 weeks ago and increasing in number. She is otherwise well with an uncomplicated pregnancy on midwifery shared care. She did not receive the HPV vaccine in school. On examination there are approximately 10 papillomatous lesions 2-5 mm distributed over the labia majora and perianal area; no internal vaginal, cervical or intra-anal lesions; cervical screening normal 4 months ago. She is keen to treat them before delivery and prefers home application.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment for her anogenital warts?

options.t: Self-applied podophyllotoxin 0.5% solution twice daily for 3 days, then 4 days off, repeated up to 4 cycles
Self-applied imiquimod 5% cream three nights per week for up to 16 weeks
Clinician-applied cryotherapy with liquid nitrogen weekly
Watch and wait until after delivery as warts always regress postpartum
Plan elective caesarean section solely to prevent vertical HPV transmission

stem: A 22-year-old woman is being treated for chlamydia trachomatis (NAAT positive on self-collected vaginal swab; gonorrhoea negative). She is on the etonogestrel implant, has no penicillin allergy, no pelvic or abdominal pain, no cervical motion tenderness, and a normal bimanual examination. She reports anal intercourse with two regular male partners in the past 3 months and asks for treatment that covers all sites where she could have acquired infection. She is not pregnant. She has heard from a friend that 'a single azithromycin pill' is the treatment.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate antimicrobial regimen for this woman in Australian practice?

options.t: Azithromycin 1 g orally as a single dose
Doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 7 days
Doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 21 days
Amoxicillin 500 mg orally three times daily for 7 days
Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM as a single dose

stem: A 24-year-old woman presents to a sexual health clinic with five days of yellow vaginal discharge, mild lower abdominal discomfort and intermittent intermenstrual bleeding. She has had two new male partners over the past two months, neither using condoms reliably, and one partner recently disclosed being treated for an STI without specifying which. She is not pregnant (urine beta-hCG negative), takes the combined oral contraceptive pill, has no drug allergies, and has never had gonorrhoea or chlamydia before. On speculum examination there is mucopurulent endocervical discharge with easily induced contact bleeding from a friable cervix. There is no cervical motion tenderness, no adnexal tenderness, no fever and no peritonism. An endocervical swab NAAT is positive for Neisseria gonorrhoeae and Chlamydia trachomatis, and a culture has been sent for antimicrobial susceptibility. She is keen to commence treatment today and the clinic has both intramuscular and oral antibiotics available.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate empirical treatment regimen?

options.t: Ciprofloxacin 500 mg orally as a single dose with no other agent
Azithromycin 2 g orally as a single dose with no other agent
Cefixime 400 mg orally as a single dose with no other agent
Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM single dose plus azithromycin 1 g orally single dose
Benzathine penicillin 1.8 g intramuscularly as a single dose with no other agent

stem: A 28-year-old woman presents to a sexual health clinic with a painless ulcer on the right labia majora first noticed eight days ago. She has had two new sexual contacts over the past three months including one overseas partner. The ulcer is solitary, indurated and clean-based with a rolled edge and is associated with non-tender right inguinal lymphadenopathy. She has no rash, no oral lesions, no fever and no joint pain. She is not pregnant (urine beta-hCG negative today), has no penicillin allergy, no immunosuppression, and is otherwise well with a BMI of 23. Dark-field microscopy is not available locally but a swab from the ulcer base has been sent for Treponema pallidum PCR. Treponemal (CMIA) serology returns reactive and the RPR titre is 1:32. She has never previously been diagnosed with or treated for syphilis. HIV, hepatitis B, hepatitis C and gonorrhoea/chlamydia screens have been sent.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate treatment regimen for this stage of syphilis?

options.t: Doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 14 days as monotherapy
Benzathine benzylpenicillin 1.8 g intramuscularly as a single stat dose
Benzathine benzylpenicillin 1.8 g intramuscularly weekly for three doses
Procaine penicillin 1.5 g intramuscularly once daily for 10 days
Ceftriaxone 1 g intramuscularly once daily for 10 days total

stem: A 24-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with five days of lower abdominal pain, deep dyspareunia, intermenstrual bleeding and a low-grade fever. She has had one new sexual partner over the past three months and uses condoms inconsistently. She is not pregnant (serum beta-hCG < 5 IU/L), has no allergies, takes only the combined oral contraceptive pill, has no recent gynaecological instrumentation and no IUD in situ. She has not had previous PID. On examination her temperature is 37.9 degrees C, abdomen is mildly tender suprapubically without peritonism, bowel sounds are normal, and on bimanual examination there is bilateral adnexal tenderness with marked cervical motion tenderness. Speculum reveals mucopurulent cervical discharge. Endocervical NAATs and gonococcal culture have been sent. There is no palpable adnexal mass, no fluctuant cul-de-sac mass on examination, and the patient tolerates oral fluids and analgesia. The treating registrar judges her safe for outpatient management with close 48-hour review.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate empirical outpatient antibiotic regimen?

options.t: Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM stat plus doxycycline 100 mg bd and metronidazole 400 mg bd for 14 days
Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg orally twice daily for 14 days as oral monotherapy regimen
Doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 14 days as oral monotherapy regimen
Azithromycin 1 g orally weekly for 2 weeks as a oral monotherapy regimen
Ceftriaxone 1 g IV daily plus metronidazole 500 mg IV twice daily until afebrile for 24 hours

subtopic_detail: HIV pre-exposure prophylaxis - eligibility and monitoring
stem: A 29-year-old cisgender woman attends a sexual health service requesting HIV pre-exposure prophylaxis. Her current male partner of three months has HIV-1 with a viral load that he reports has been detectable on most recent testing because of treatment interruption while changing jobs. They have condomless vaginal sex 2-3 times per week. She has no symptoms of acute HIV illness in the past 6 weeks (no fever, rash, lymphadenopathy or sore throat), is not pregnant (urine beta-hCG negative), is on the etonogestrel implant for contraception, has no history of renal disease, and is hepatitis B surface antibody positive (vaccinated). She has not injected drugs. Her last STI screen 8 months ago was negative. She has no allergies and no regular medications other than the implant. She is asking whether she is eligible for PrEP, what baseline tests are required, and what monitoring she will need on therapy.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate plan for PrEP initiation and monitoring?

options.t: Prescribe daily tenofovir disoproxil 300 mg plus emtricitabine 200 mg only after a 4-week negative HIV window-period repeat test
Prescribe on-demand (2+1+1) tenofovir disoproxil plus emtricitabine, suitable for all genders in this clinical scenario today
Prescribe daily tenofovir disoproxil 300 mg plus emtricitabine 200 mg with baseline HIV/HBV/renal screen, then HIV/STI screening every 3 months
Defer PrEP and recommend condom use only, as PrEP is contraindicated when the partner has detectable viral load
Prescribe daily oral dolutegravir 50 mg as PrEP monotherapy in this scenario at first visit

stem: A 24-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden-onset severe left iliac fossa pain that began two hours ago while exercising, associated with one episode of vomiting. The pain is colicky on a background of constant severe pain and radiates to the left flank. She has no fever, no dysuria, no diarrhoea, no anorexia and no migratory periumbilical pain. On examination temperature is 36.9 degrees C, pulse 104, BP 116/72. The abdomen is tender in the left iliac fossa with voluntary guarding but no rebound or percussion tenderness; right iliac fossa is non-tender. Bimanual examination reveals a tender left adnexal fullness without cervical motion tenderness and the right adnexa is non-tender. Serum beta-hCG is < 5 IU/L. Urinalysis is negative for nitrites and leukocyte esterase. Bedside ultrasound by the registrar shows a 7 cm left ovarian cyst with absent venous Doppler flow and reduced arterial flow.
lead_in: Which is the most likely diagnosis?

options.t: Left ovarian torsion with ischaemia
Ruptured ectopic pregnancy with haemoperitoneum
Acute pelvic inflammatory disease with tubo-ovarian inflammation
Acute appendicitis with pelvic peritonism
Renal colic from a left ureteric stone

stem: A 28-year-old G2P1 woman at 18+3 weeks gestation presents to her midwife with two weeks of a thin off-white vaginal discharge with a fishy odour. She has no abdominal pain, no abnormal bleeding, no fever, no contractions and no fluid leakage. She is otherwise well, BMI at booking 25, no medical comorbidities, no previous preterm birth (the first pregnancy delivered at 39+2), no known cervical insufficiency, and the dating scan at 9 weeks confirmed a singleton intrauterine pregnancy. Her booking bloods including HIV, syphilis, hepatitis B and rubella serology were negative or immune. Speculum examination shows a thin homogeneous off-white discharge coating the vaginal walls without inflammation, a closed cervix without lesions, and no liquor pooling. Vaginal pH at the lateral wall is 5.1 and addition of 10% potassium hydroxide produces a strong amine odour. Wet mount microscopy shows clue cells and few lactobacilli with no motile trichomonads and no pseudohyphae.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate treatment for this woman's bacterial vaginosis at 18+3 weeks gestation?

options.t: No treatment required as bacterial vaginosis in pregnancy is self-limiting and treatment does not reduce preterm birth
Clindamycin 2% vaginal cream applied intravaginally nightly for 7 nights as monotherapy regimen
Oral metronidazole 400 mg twice daily for 5 to 7 days as monotherapy
Tinidazole 2 g orally as a single dose with no further course required
Metronidazole 2 g orally as a single dose with no further course required

stem: A 32-year-old woman who is breastfeeding her 4-month-old infant (exclusive breastfeeding, no formula) presents with 3 weeks of frothy yellow-green vaginal discharge, vulval irritation and dyspareunia. Strawberry cervix on speculum, vaginal pH 5.6, KOH whiff positive, saline wet mount shows motile flagellated protozoa. Gonorrhoea and chlamydia NAAT have been sent. She has had two new male partners in the past 4 months. She has no allergies and no liver disease. She wishes to continue breastfeeding without interruption if possible.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line management?

options.t: Single 2 g oral metronidazole dose to her and her recent partners; pause breastfeeding for 12-24 hours and discard expressed milk
Intravaginal metronidazole 0.75% gel nightly for 5 nights; no partner treatment
Tinidazole 2 g orally as a single dose with continued breastfeeding throughout
Permanently cease breastfeeding so that metronidazole can be given safely
Doxycycline 100 mg bd for 7 days to her only

stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 at 38 weeks gestation presents to the birth suite with a sudden onset of constant, severe lower abdominal pain and a moderate amount of dark red vaginal bleeding. She has a history of pre-eclampsia in her previous pregnancy, which was delivered via emergency lower segment caesarean section at 36 weeks. In this pregnancy, her blood pressure has been mildly elevated but unmedicated. Her 20-week morphology ultrasound showed an anterior placenta clear of the cervical os. On examination, she is visibly distressed. Abdominal examination reveals a tense, tender uterus with a fundal height of 39 cm. Foetal parts are difficult to palpate. Speculum examination confirms dark blood in the vaginal vault with no active cervical lesions. A continuous CTG is commenced, showing a baseline of 165 bpm, reduced variability, and recurrent late decelerations.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.t: Perform an urgent bedside transvaginal ultrasound.
Administer intravenous oxytocin infusion for induction.
Perform an immediate emergency caesarean section.
Artificial rupture of membranes and internal monitoring.
Administer intramuscular betamethasone and observe.

stem: A 26-year-old nulliparous woman presents to the ED with 2 days of right iliac fossa pain and scant dark vaginal spotting at LMP+7 weeks. Previous PID 3 years ago, treated. No contraception. On arrival: HR 132, BP 88/52, RR 22, SpO2 98%, peripherally cool with delayed capillary refill 4 seconds, pale, conversing but anxious. Abdominal guarding, rebound tenderness across the right iliac fossa, distended abdomen with shifting dullness. Cervical excitation, right adnexal tenderness with palpable fullness. Urine beta-hCG positive. Bedside FAST shows free fluid in the pelvis and Morrison's pouch. IV access x2 has been obtained; group and hold and crossmatch sent.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Transfer immediately to operating theatre for diagnostic laparoscopy with potential laparotomy and activate the massive transfusion protocol
Perform an urgent transvaginal ultrasound to confirm location of pregnancy before further action
Administer single-dose intramuscular methotrexate 50 mg/m2 and admit for monitoring
Commence broad-spectrum IV antibiotics for presumed pelvic inflammatory disease and reassess in 6 hours
Repeat beta-hCG in 48 hours and discharge home with safety-net advice

stem: A 29-year-old G1P0 at 41 weeks gestation is undergoing an induction of labour for post-dates. A vaginal dinoprostone (Prostin) pessary was inserted 6 hours ago. The midwife calls you urgently for a review. The patient is distressed, complaining of continuous, severe abdominal pain without relief between contractions. Palpation of the abdomen reveals that she is having 6 contractions every 10 minutes, with increased resting uterine tone. A continuous cardiotocograph (CTG) is running. The baseline foetal heart rate was 140 bpm an hour ago. For the past 4 minutes, the CTG has demonstrated a single, sudden, and prolonged deceleration down to a nadir of 80 bpm with loss of baseline variability.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate medical intervention?

options.t: Administer an intravenous bolus of magnesium sulfate.
Commence an intravenous oxytocin augmentation infusion.
Perform an artificial rupture of the amniotic membranes.
Perform an immediate emergency category one caesarean section.
Administer a stat dose of subcutaneous terbutaline.

stem: A 24-year-old woman presents to the gynaecology clinic seeking assistance with falling pregnant. She and her partner have been trying to conceive for 18 months. She has a history of secondary amenorrhoea, having not menstruated for the past 12 months. Her body mass index (BMI) is 32 kg/m^2. On examination, she has moderate facial hirsutism and cystic acne along her jawline. An initial infertility workup reveals normal serum prolactin and thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) levels, but her calculated free testosterone is elevated. Her partner's semen analysis is entirely normal. A hysterosalpingogram confirms bilateral tubal patency.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacological agent for ovulation induction?

options.t: Prescribe oral clomiphene citrate for ovulation induction.
Prescribe oral metformin to improve insulin sensitivity.
Refer for gonadotropin injections and timed intercourse.
Refer for immediate in vitro fertilisation treatment.
Prescribe oral letrozole to induce follicular development.

stem: A 28-year-old woman attends for routine cervical screening. She has no history of abnormal results and last screened 6 years ago overseas. She is asymptomatic, not pregnant, and has no immunosuppression. Her current test shows oncogenic HPV type 16 detected with no visible abnormality on speculum examination. She asks whether she should simply repeat screening in 12 months because she has never had an abnormal smear before. She also wants to know whether self-collection would have been acceptable. She is anxious because her mother had cervical cancer at age 52, but she has no postcoital bleeding or pelvic pain. Her only medication is the combined oral contraceptive pill.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Repeat HPV testing in 12 months only
Arrange immediate colposcopic assessment
Return to 5-yearly routine screening because the cervix looks normal
Collect a reflex liquid-based cytology specimen and await that result
Defer screening until age 30 because she is asymptomatic

stem: A 29-year-old woman presents with 2 days of light vaginal bleeding and intermittent left iliac fossa pain. She is 7 weeks from her last menstrual period and conceived spontaneously. She has a history of pelvic inflammatory disease 4 years ago and laparoscopic appendicectomy as a teenager. She feels mildly light-headed when standing but has no syncope. Pregnancy test is positive. Transvaginal ultrasound shows no intrauterine gestational sac, a 19 mm left adnexal heterogeneous mass, and a small volume of free fluid in the pouch of Douglas. Serum beta-hCG is 2350 IU/L and was 2280 IU/L 48 hours earlier.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.t: Repeat beta-hCG and ultrasound in 7 days because this is likely an early viable intrauterine pregnancy
Offer single-dose intramuscular methotrexate after confirming suitability for medical management
Proceed directly to emergency laparotomy because free fluid confirms rupture
Arrange dilation and curettage to exclude missed miscarriage before treatment
Commence oral progesterone and repeat beta-hCG after 48 hours

stem: A 31-year-old primigravida at 27 weeks gestation attends antenatal clinic for review of her oral glucose tolerance test. Her BMI at booking was 33 kg/m2 and her mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus. Foetal morphology scan at 20 weeks was normal. She feels well and reports normal foetal movements. Fundal height measures slightly ahead of dates but there is no polyhydramnios on bedside scan. The OGTT was performed because of risk factors and showed fasting glucose 5.3 mmol/L, 1-hour glucose 9.6 mmol/L, and 2-hour glucose 7.8 mmol/L after a 75 g glucose load. A medical student states that the result is normal because only the fasting level is mildly elevated and the post-load values are below older diagnostic thresholds.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate interpretation of these results?

options.t: The OGTT is normal because only the fasting value is abnormal and the 2-hour value remains below 8.5 mmol/L
The OGTT confirms gestational diabetes because the fasting glucose meets the diagnostic threshold
The OGTT is indeterminate and should be repeated at 32 weeks because post-load glucose levels are normal
The OGTT suggests pre-existing type 2 diabetes because fasting glucose exceeds 5.0 mmol/L
The OGTT should be interpreted as impaired fasting glucose rather than gestational diabetes in pregnancy

stem: A 27-year-old woman attends her GP for review of cervical screening results. She had her first cervical screening test at age 25, which was negative for oncogenic HPV. She has no abnormal bleeding, pelvic pain, or postcoital symptoms. She received partial HPV vaccination during adolescence. Her current test result shows oncogenic HPV 16 detected with negative liquid-based cytology. She asks whether she can simply repeat the test in 12 months because the cytology is normal and she feels well. She has no history of immunosuppression or previous cervical procedures.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.t: Repeat cervical screening in 12 months because cytology is negative and she is asymptomatic
Refer for colposcopy because oncogenic HPV 16 carries higher cervical neoplasia risk
Repeat HPV testing in 5 years because prior screening at age 25 was normal
