Arrange immediate excisional treatment because HPV 16 confirms high-grade dysplasia
Commence HPV vaccination booster doses before deciding whether colposcopy is required

stem: A 27-year-old woman, G1P0, presents with 6 hours of right-sided lower abdominal pain and light vaginal spotting at 7 weeks by last menstrual period. She had a positive urine pregnancy test 10 days ago. Her cycles are usually regular. She is haemodynamically stable but uncomfortable. On examination her abdomen is mildly tender in the right iliac fossa, and there is mild cervical motion tenderness. The cervix is closed.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.t: Urgent transvaginal ultrasound
Repeat serum beta-hCG in 48 hours only
Dilation and curettage to confirm a failed pregnancy
Abdominal CT scan with contrast
Discharge with reassurance and routine antenatal booking

stem: A 23-year-old woman G2P0 presents to ED with 2 days of increasing vaginal bleeding and crampy abdominal pain at 13+0 weeks gestation by ultrasound 1 week ago that showed a live intrauterine pregnancy. She is haemodynamically stable, cervix closed on speculum, uterus appropriate for dates. She is blood group O Rh(D) negative, antibody screen negative, partner Rh positive. She has not yet received any anti-D in this pregnancy. She previously had a TOP at 10 weeks last year and was given anti-D 250 IU IM at that time.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate anti-D management now?

options.t: Administer anti-D 250 IU IM as a single dose now
Administer anti-D 625 IU IM within 72 hours now
Administer routine antenatal anti-D 1500 IU IM now to cover this event and the 28-week dose
Withhold anti-D as the cervix is closed and bleeding is settling
Withhold anti-D as she received it last year and the half-life provides ongoing protection

stem: A 39-year-old multiparous woman with gestational diabetes managed on insulin is in the second stage of labour at 40 weeks gestation. Estimated foetal weight at 38 weeks was 4.4 kg. She has been pushing effectively for 35 minutes and the foetal head delivers spontaneously. Immediately afterwards the head retracts tightly against the perineum and fails to restitute. Gentle downward traction does not deliver the shoulders.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next manoeuvre?

options.t: Apply stronger downward traction because urgent delivery is required to reduce hypoxic injury risk
Perform McRoberts manoeuvre with suprapubic pressure while avoiding fundal pressure
Apply fundal pressure to increase expulsive force and facilitate anterior shoulder delivery
Perform immediate episiotomy before attempting any positional or internal manoeuvres
Proceed directly to posterior arm delivery because foetal bradycardia confirms failed basic manoeuvres

stem: A 26-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with severe lower abdominal pain and collapse at home. She is approximately 8 weeks pregnant based on her last menstrual period and had attended a GP 3 days earlier for light vaginal spotting. At that time her serum beta-hCG was 2100 IU/L and transvaginal ultrasound showed no intrauterine pregnancy but a possible right adnexal lesion. She was scheduled for repeat testing today. Instead, she developed sudden right-sided abdominal pain radiating to the shoulder tip followed by dizziness and near-syncope. On arrival she is pale, diaphoretic, and intermittently confused. Abdominal examination reveals marked lower abdominal tenderness with guarding. A bedside FAST scan demonstrates free fluid in the pelvis and Morrison pouch.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next step in management?

options.t: Administer methotrexate because haemodynamic instability does not alter management when beta-hCG is low
Repeat transvaginal ultrasound because FAST scanning may overestimate physiologic pelvic free fluid
Commence urgent resuscitation and proceed to emergency surgical management
Observe after fluid resuscitation because spontaneous tubal abortion may stabilise without intervention
Arrange urgent CT abdomen because appendicitis remains an important differential diagnosis

stem: A 31-year-old multiparous woman undergoes spontaneous vaginal delivery of a healthy infant at 39 weeks gestation after induction for gestational diabetes. The placenta delivers intact 10 minutes later. Shortly afterwards she develops ongoing heavy vaginal bleeding with large clots. Uterine massage is attempted while additional staff are called. The registrar notes the uterus feels enlarged and boggy above the umbilicus. There is no obvious genital tract trauma on initial inspection.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next intervention?

options.t: Perform immediate hysterectomy because severe postpartum bleeding rarely responds to medical management
Commence uterotonic therapy while continuing uterine massage and haemodynamic resuscitation
Observe for spontaneous uterine contraction because the patient remains conscious and responsive
Arrange urgent CT angiography because retained products are the most likely cause of bleeding
Prioritise broad-spectrum antibiotics because postpartum endometritis commonly causes major haemorrhage

stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 at 38+2 in spontaneous labour has SROM during VE (cervix 6 cm) and the midwife palpates a pulsatile cord at the introitus. CTG shows sustained foetal bradycardia at 70 bpm despite left lateral position. The on-call obstetric registrar and anaesthetist are 5 minutes away, theatre is being prepared and an emergency category-1 caesarean section has been called. The woman is in a single delivery room (no nearby toilets, no easy access to Foley-bag elevation kit). Maternal vital signs are stable.
lead_in: Which combination is the most appropriate immediate action while awaiting theatre?

options.t: Place the woman in knee-chest position, elevate the presenting part manually with a vaginal hand, and instil 500-700 mL normal saline into the bladder via Foley catheter to maintain elevation; administer tocolysis (terbutaline 250 mcg SC)
Instruct active maternal pushing to expedite vaginal delivery before theatre arrives
Manually replace the cord into the uterine cavity to restore foetal circulation
Repeat digital VE every 5 minutes to assess for cervical progress before theatre
Wrap the cord in warm saline-soaked gauze outside the vagina and await spontaneous resolution

stem: A 33-year-old G2P1 at 39+0 weeks presents in spontaneous labour at 4 cm dilated. Her pregnancy has been uncomplicated. Her GBS status is unknown - the screening swab at 36 weeks was not performed because she was travelling interstate. Her previous pregnancy 4 years ago was uncomplicated, and she delivered vaginally at term. Her first infant did not develop GBS disease. On direct questioning, she reports no symptoms of a urinary tract infection during this pregnancy and no history of a positive GBS swab. Her temperature is 36.7°C, and observations are normal. The membranes are intact at present. The CTG is reassuring with a baseline of 140 bpm and normal variability. The woman has no known drug allergies.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis plan?

options.t: No intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis is required; treat only if the infant develops clinical signs of sepsis
Benzylpenicillin 1.2 g IV loading dose, then 600 mg IV 4-hourly until delivery, commencing immediately as she is in labour with unknown GBS status
Take a low vaginal swab for GBS culture and PCR now, and commence benzylpenicillin 1.2 g IV loading dose only if the swab returns positive or membranes rupture

Monitor for the development of clinical risk factors - if membranes rupture for ≥18 hours or maternal temperature reaches ≥38.0°C, commence benzylpenicillin 1.2 g IV loading dose then 600 mg IV 4-hourly
Cefazolin 2 g IV loading dose then 1 g IV 8-hourly as the preferred antibiotic for unknown GBS status with no risk factors

stem: A 34-year-old primigravida at 41+3 weeks gestation presents for a scheduled induction of labour for post-dates pregnancy. She has been well throughout the pregnancy with normal foetal movements, a reactive CTG, and an amniotic fluid index of 10 cm on yesterday's ultrasound. She has no significant past medical history. On vaginal examination, the cervix is posterior, 2 cm long, firm in consistency, closed at the external os, and the presenting part is at station -3. Her Bishop score is calculated as 2. The CTG on admission is normal with a baseline of 140 bpm and moderate variability. She is counselled about the need for cervical ripening prior to oxytocin induction. She has no contraindications to prostaglandin use (no previous uterine surgery, no severe asthma, no known allergy).
lead_in: What is the most appropriate cervical ripening agent and initial dose?

options.t: Dinoprostone (PGE2) 3 mg vaginal tablet inserted into the posterior fornix, repeated after 6 hours if needed up to a maximum of two doses in 24 hours
Dinoprostone (PGE2) 2 mg vaginal gel inserted into the posterior fornix, repeated after 6 hours if needed up to a maximum of two doses in 24 hours
Misoprostol (PGE1) 25 mcg vaginal tablet inserted into the posterior fornix, repeated after 4 hours if needed
Foley catheter balloon inserted through the cervix and inflated with 30 mL sterile water, left in situ for 12 hours
Oxytocin IV infusion starting at 2 mU/min and titrated every 30 minutes according to contractions without prior cervical ripening

stem: A 36-year-old G2P1 at 29+0 weeks gestation was diagnosed with gestational diabetes at 26 weeks on a 75 g OGTT (fasting 5.4 mmol/L, 1-hour 10.5 mmol/L, 2-hour 8.9 mmol/L). Her BMI is 32 kg/m². She has been managed with lifestyle modification (diet and exercise) for 3 weeks. Her fasting BGLs have consistently been 5.0-5.3 mmol/L (target <5.1 mmol/L) and her 2-hour postprandial BGLs are 6.2-6.6 mmol/L (target <6.7 mmol/L). An ultrasound at 28 weeks showed an estimated foetal weight on the 78th centile with a normal amniotic fluid index. She asks about medication options to improve her fasting glucose. She has no contraindications to metformin or insulin. She is concerned about the risk of needing insulin and the need for injections.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in managing her fasting hyperglycaemia?

options.t: Commence metformin 500 mg orally at night, titrate up to 1500 mg daily as tolerated, and continue diet and exercise measures
Commence basal insulin (isophane or insulin detemir) at 0.1 units/kg at night, titrated according to fasting BGL, and cease metformin
Continue lifestyle measures alone for a further 2 weeks, as the fasting BGLs are only marginally above target and postprandial targets are met
Commence glibenclamide (glyburide) 2.5 mg orally at night, titrating to a maximum of 20 mg daily
Commence both metformin 500 mg BD and basal insulin 0.2 units/kg at night simultaneously to rapidly achieve glycaemic control

stem: A 26-year-old primigravida who delivered by emergency caesarean section for failure to progress 3 days ago presents with fever, abdominal pain, and offensive lochia. The caesarean was performed after 18 hours of labour with ruptured membranes for 14 hours. She had three vaginal examinations during labour. Her temperature is 38.8°C, HR 104 bpm, BP 110/70 mmHg. The abdomen is tender in the lower segment with guarding, and the uterine fundus is tender and boggy 2 cm above the umbilicus. The lochia is heavy, purulent, and malodorous. The caesarean wound is clean and intact with no erythema. She is breastfeeding. She has no known drug allergies. Her full blood count shows WCC 18.5 x 10^9/L with neutrophil predominance, Hb 95 g/L (down from 112 g/L antenatally). Blood cultures are pending.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate empiric intravenous antibiotic regimen?

options.t: Amoxicillin 2 g IV 6-hourly PLUS metronidazole 500 mg IV 8-hourly PLUS gentamicin 5 mg/kg IV daily dosing
Clindamycin 900 mg IV 8-hourly PLUS gentamicin 5 mg/kg IV daily dosing (with subsequent dosing based on serum levels and renal function)
Ceftriaxone 2 g IV daily PLUS metronidazole 500 mg IV 8-hourly
Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV 6-hourly as monotherapy
Amoxicillin-clavulanate 1.2 g IV 8-hourly as monotherapy

stem: A 29-year-old woman presents for her routine cervical screening test as part of the National Cervical Screening Program. She has no symptoms and no previous abnormal results. Her screening test was performed as an HPV test with partial genotyping. The result shows oncogenic HPV type 16 detected, with reflex liquid-based cytology reported as possible low-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (pLSIL). She has never had a colposcopy before. She is nulliparous and has no significant past medical history. She is a non-smoker. She uses a levonorgestrel intrauterine device for contraception.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step according to the NCSP guidelines?

options.t: Refer for colposcopy within 2-4 weeks for assessment, as the presence of HPV 16 mandates direct colposcopic evaluation regardless of cytology
Repeat the HPV test in 12 months, as the finding of pLSIL in a woman under 30 with HPV 16 can be managed conservatively due to high regression rates
Perform a repeat liquid-based cytology test in 6 months without HPV testing, as the current result is low-grade and may resolve spontaneously
Perform a colposcopy only if the woman develops symptoms (such as postcoital bleeding or intermenstrual bleeding), as asymptomatic low-grade abnormalities can be observed
Treat empirically with large loop excision of the transformation zone (LLETZ) without prior colposcopy, as HPV 16 is a high-risk type that warrants definitive treatment

stem: A 31-year-old primigravida at 36+6 weeks gestation is admitted to the antenatal ward with new-onset hypertension. She has been well throughout her pregnancy until 3 days ago when she noted mild ankle swelling and a frontal headache. Her blood pressure on arrival is 155/105 mmHg, confirmed on repeat measurement 30 minutes later. Urinalysis shows 2+ protein on dipstick with a spot urine protein-to-creatinine ratio of 45 mg/mmol (normal <30). She reports no visual disturbances, no epigastric pain, and no nausea. On examination, she has mild pitting ankle oedema, normal reflexes with no clonus, and a soft non-tender abdomen. The foetal CTG is reactive with a baseline of 145 bpm, normal variability, and accelerations. Ultrasound shows an appropriately grown foetus on the 55th centile with a normal amniotic fluid index and normal umbilical artery Dopplers. Her blood results show haemoglobin 118 g/L, platelets 195 x 10^9/L, creatinine 62 micromol/L, ALT 28 U/L, and urate 0.36 mmol/L. She has been commenced on oral labetalol 200 mg BD, and her blood pressure has improved to 140/90 mmHg after 24 hours.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate timing for delivery?

options.t: Induction of labour within the next 24-48 hours, as delivery is recommended for this hypertensive disorder of pregnancy at or beyond 37+0 weeks
Continue oral labetalol and expectant management with twice-weekly review until 39+0 weeks, as her blood pressure is now controlled and she has no severe features
Perform immediate caesarean section, as the proteinuria with a PCR of 45 mg/mmol and hypertension at 36+6 weeks meets severe-features criteria requiring urgent delivery
Discharge home on labetalol 200 mg BD with instructions for daily BP monitoring and twice-weekly CTG and ultrasound, with delivery planned at 40+0 weeks
Wait until 38+0 weeks to allow full corticosteroid effect, then induce labour if the blood pressure remains controlled on oral labetalol without development of severe features

stem: A 24-year-old primigravida delivered vaginally 10 days ago following induction of labour at 38 weeks for gestational hypertension. The third stage was managed actively with intramuscular oxytocin 10 IU, and the placenta was delivered complete by controlled cord traction. She was discharged home on day 2 post-delivery with no concerns. She now presents to the emergency department with new-onset heavy vaginal bleeding that began 4 hours ago, soaking through 3 maternity pads. She also reports a 2-day history of dull lower abdominal pain, malaise, and a temperature of 38.2°C measured at home. The lochia has been malodorous for 3 days. On examination, she looks pale and unwell. Her HR is 106 bpm, BP 100/60 mmHg, and temperature is 38.4°C. The uterus is tender, boggy, and palpable 3 cm below the umbilicus. On speculum, there is heavy dark red blood with clots in the vagina, and the os is open with blood and tissue visible. Her haemoglobin is 88 g/L (was 108 g/L antenatally), WCC is 16.2 x 10^9/L, and CRP is 125 mg/L.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.t: Commence IV clindamycin 900 mg 8-hourly and IV gentamicin 5 mg/kg daily for endometritis, and administer IV oxytocin 40 units infusion for uterine atony
Perform a bimanual examination and attempt digital removal of retained tissue from the uterine cavity under sedation, followed by IV oxytocin and antibiotics

Perform an urgent pelvic ultrasound to assess for retained products of conception, commence IV antibiotics (clindamycin and gentamicin), administer an IV oxytocin infusion, and arrange for suction curettage in theatre if retained products are confirmed
Administer IV tranexamic acid 1 g, IV ergometrine 250 mcg, and oral misoprostol 800 mcg, and observe for 4 hours; if bleeding persists, arrange a pelvic ultrasound
Commence oral doxycycline 100 mg BD and oral metronidazole 400 mg TDS for endometritis, and arrange a pelvic ultrasound as an outpatient within 48 hours if the bleeding settles

stem: A 38-year-old G2P1 at 30+1 weeks gestation was diagnosed with GDM at 27 weeks on a 75 g OGTT (fasting 5.6 mmol/L, 1-hour 10.8 mmol/L, 2-hour 9.2 mmol/L). She commenced metformin 500 mg BD at diagnosis, titrated to 1 g BD over 2 weeks, and has been on this dose for the past 3 weeks with ongoing dietary modification. Her fasting BGLs have been 5.3-5.8 mmol/L consistently. Her 2-hour postprandial BGLs have been 6.5-7.2 mmol/L. An ultrasound at 29 weeks showed an estimated foetal weight on the 82nd centile with a normal amniotic fluid index. She has no contraindications to insulin and has been counselled about the need to escalate therapy. She weighs 78 kg. Her HbA1c is 5.8% (40 mmol/mol).
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in her glycaemic management?

options.t: Continue metformin 1 g BD and add basal insulin (isophane or insulin detemir) at night, starting at 0.1-0.2 units/kg, titrated to achieve fasting BGL below 5.1 mmol/L

Cease metformin and commence basal-bolus insulin regimen with isophane at night and rapid-acting insulin analogue before each meal to target both fasting and postprandial hyperglycaemia simultaneously
Increase metformin to 1.5 g BD (total 3 g daily) and add glibenclamide 2.5 mg at night for an additional 2 weeks before considering insulin
Continue current metformin dose and dietary measures for a further 2 weeks as the fasting BGLs are only marginally above target, then reassess with repeat HbA1c
Commence rapid-acting insulin analogue before breakfast only, as her 2-hour postprandial BGLs are above target while fasting values are approaching acceptable levels

stem: A 34-year-old primigravida at 37+1 weeks gestation attends her routine antenatal appointment. On abdominal palpation, the foetus is found to be in a breech presentation with the foetal head ballotable at the fundus. A bedside ultrasound confirms a frank breech presentation with the foetal legs extended, a flexed foetal head, normal amniotic fluid index, and a posterior placenta not covering the os. The estimated foetal weight is on the 55th centile. She is otherwise well with no significant past medical history. She has no contraindications to external cephalic version (ECV) and is keen to avoid a caesarean section if possible. She has read about breech vaginal delivery and asks whether this is a safe option for her.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate management recommendation?

options.t: Offer external cephalic version, and if unsuccessful or declined, discuss planned caesarean section at 39+0 weeks as the safest mode of delivery for a term breech

Offer external cephalic version, and if unsuccessful, plan a vaginal breech delivery as this primigravida has a frank breech with flexed head and normal foetal weight on the 55th centile, which are favourable factors

Proceed directly to booking an elective caesarean section at 39+0 weeks without offering ECV, as primigravidae have a lower ECV success rate and ECV carries risks of foetal distress and placental abruption
Perform external cephalic version immediately at this visit without tocolysis, as she is 37 weeks, and if successful, discharge home to await spontaneous labour

Advise the woman that breech presentation at 37 weeks has a high chance of spontaneous version before term, and arrange a repeat ultrasound at 39 weeks to reassess presentation before making a delivery plan

stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 at 33+5 weeks presents with a single episode of painless bright red vaginal bleeding. Ultrasound at 32 weeks showed an anterior placenta completely covering the internal os (major praevia). She is haemodynamically stable, bleeding has settled, CTG reassuring, cervical exam deferred. She has had no previous corticosteroid courses this pregnancy. The plan is admission for monitoring and elective caesarean at 36+0 to 37+0 weeks. She is keen on whatever course will best protect her baby's lung function.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate corticosteroid plan?

options.t: Administer betamethasone 11.4 mg IM, two doses 24 hours apart, now
Administer no corticosteroids as she is now beyond 34 weeks
Administer a single dose of betamethasone 11.4 mg IM
Administer weekly betamethasone 11.4 mg IM until delivery
Administer dexamethasone 6 mg IM, four doses 12-hourly, in place of betamethasone, on the basis of superior neurodevelopmental outcomes

stem: A 25-year-old primipara who had a spontaneous vaginal delivery 5 days ago presents to the postnatal clinic with fever, lower abdominal pain and heavy malodorous lochia. She is breastfeeding without difficulty. Examination: T 38.7, HR 108, BP 110/68, RR 18; the uterus is bulky and tender, lochia heavy and offensive; breasts full but no focal tenderness or erythema; perineal repair healing; calves soft. Bedside urinalysis nil. Beta-hCG negative. WCC 16.2, CRP 110, Hb 102. She had a 4th-degree tear at delivery, repaired in theatre, with a single-shot prophylactic dose of cefazolin and metronidazole. She has no allergies.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate empirical antibiotic regimen?

options.t: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg twice daily as outpatient
Admit and commence IV ampicillin, gentamicin and metronidazole until clinical improvement, then de-escalate to oral
Oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 14 days as outpatient
IV ceftriaxone monotherapy until afebrile, then discharge
Withhold antibiotics and arrange urgent pelvic ultrasound to exclude retained products before treatment

stem: A 30-year-old nullipara at 29+3 weeks presents with new hypertension 170/110 mmHg, headache, and epigastric pain. Urine protein:creatinine ratio 98 mg/mmol. Platelets 130 ×10⁹/L, ALT 110 U/L, creatinine 92 µmol/L. She has received the first dose of betamethasone. CTG is reactive with normal variability. She is commenced on labetalol and magnesium sulphate. After stabilisation, the team discusses timing of delivery. What is the most appropriate plan?
lead_in: What is the most appropriate timing of delivery?

options.t: Complete the 48-hour steroid course and deliver electively at 48-72 hours, provided maternal and foetal status remain stable
Proceed to delivery within 24 hours, as severe-features hypertensive disease with hepatic involvement mandates delivery regardless of gestational age
Continue expectant management with close monitoring until 34 weeks, as the CTG is reassuring and maternal status is stable on treatment
Administer the second dose of betamethasone at 24 hours and plan delivery for 48 hours after the first dose, with continuous monitoring
Deliver immediately by caesarean, as severe-features hypertension with abnormal LFTs and platelets <150 ×10⁹/L meets haemolysis-elevated-liver-enzymes-low-platelets criteria

stem: A 26-year-old G2P1 underwent suction curettage for a suspected molar pregnancy at 10 weeks gestation after an ultrasound showed a 'snowstorm' appearance and no foetal pole. Histopathology confirms a complete hydatidiform mole. She is keen to conceive again as soon as possible. Her hCG level 2 weeks post-evacuation is 2500 IU/L, down from 120,000 IU/L pre-operatively. She has no vaginal bleeding, and pelvic ultrasound shows an empty uterus with normal adnexa. What is the most appropriate advice regarding future pregnancy?
lead_in: What is the most appropriate advice regarding future pregnancy?

options.t: She may attempt pregnancy once hCG becomes undetectable, with no increased risk in subsequent pregnancies and no surveillance needed
Avoid pregnancy for at least 6 months after hCG normalises, use reliable contraception, with monthly hCG surveillance throughout that period
She should undergo a hysterectomy because complete moles have a 15-20% risk of progression to choriocarcinoma and future pregnancy is contraindicated
She requires a single dose of intramuscular methotrexate 50 mg/m² immediately to prevent progression to persistent trophoblastic disease at any stage
She should undergo CT chest and abdomen to stage the disease before pregnancy, as complete moles frequently metastasise to the lungs

stem: A 28-year-old primigravida at 22+5 weeks presents with painful regular contractions and cervical dilation 3 cm with bulging membranes. Foetal ultrasound: singleton, cephalic, AFI normal, estimated foetal weight 540 g (50th centile). She has been counselled with the neonatology team. After detailed discussion of likely outcomes at periviable gestation, the family has indicated they wish for active resuscitation if the infant is liveborn.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate plan regarding antenatal corticosteroids?

options.t: Administer betamethasone 11.4 mg IM, two doses 24 hours apart, since active resuscitation is planned
Withhold ACS as gestation is below 23 weeks where benefit is uncertain
Administer a single dose of betamethasone 11.4 mg IM only
Administer dexamethasone 6 mg IM every 6 hours for four doses
Defer the decision until membranes rupture and only give if delivery is within 24 hours

stem: A 35-year-old woman presents after her third consecutive miscarriage at 9 weeks. She had one previous live birth at term 4 years ago. She has no significant medical history. Which set of investigations is most appropriate?
lead_in: What is the most appropriate set of investigations?

options.t: Anticardiolipin antibodies, lupus anticoagulant, beta-2 glycoprotein I antibodies, plus pelvic ultrasound for anomalies
Antiphospholipid antibodies, parental karyotypes, inherited thrombophilia screen, and outpatient diagnostic hysteroscopy
Full blood count, thyroid function tests, fasting glucose, and 75-gram oral glucose tolerance test
Serum anti-Müllerian hormone, early follicular FSH, oestradiol level, and ovarian antral follicle count
MTHFR mutation testing, factor V Leiden, prothrombin gene mutation, and fasting homocysteine level

stem: A 32-year-old woman at 40+2 weeks is attempting vaginal birth after one prior lower-segment caesarean (VBAC). She is contracting 4 in 10 minutes, and CTG is reassuring. She requests epidural analgesia. Her labour is augmented with oxytocin at 6 mU/min for slow progress. She suddenly develops constant severe abdominal pain and vaginal bleeding. The CTG shows a prolonged foetal bradycardia. What is the most likely diagnosis?
lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.t: Uterine rupture
Placental abruption
Cord prolapse
Normal labour pain with anxiety
Amniotic fluid embolism

stem: A 34-year-old woman who had diet-controlled gestational diabetes diagnosed at 28 weeks delivered a healthy infant at 39 weeks. She is now 8 weeks postpartum. What is the most appropriate follow-up test?
lead_in: What is the most appropriate follow-up test?

options.t: 75 g oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) with fasting, 1h, and 2h glucose
Fasting blood glucose only
HbA1c
No test required; she will be screened at her next routine health check at age 40
Home glucose monitoring for 1 week

stem: A 16-year-old girl reports 8 months of severe crampy lower abdominal pain with each period, missing school for 1-2 days each cycle. Her cycles are regular at 28 days with menarche at 13. She has tried regular paracetamol 1 g qid with minimal benefit. There is no pain outside of menses, no dyspareunia (she is not sexually active), no abnormal bleeding, no urinary or bowel symptoms and no fever. Examination is normal. Pregnancy test negative. She is otherwise well, has no contraindication to NSAIDs and no medication allergies. Her mother is concerned and asks whether laparoscopy is required to exclude endometriosis before further treatment.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Commence regular naproxen 500 mg twice daily for 3-5 days starting 1-2 days before menses, with review at 3 months
Refer for diagnostic laparoscopy now to exclude endometriosis
Continue paracetamol 1 g four times daily and add codeine 30 mg qid as needed
Commence GnRH agonist (goserelin) to induce amenorrhoea
Reassure and recommend transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation as first-line

stem: A 28-year-old nulliparous woman has 2 years of severe dysmenorrhoea unresponsive to NSAIDs and the COCP, deep dyspareunia, and chronic pelvic pain. Pelvic ultrasound is normal. What is the gold standard for diagnosis?
lead_in: What is the gold standard for diagnosis?

options.t: Diagnostic laparoscopy with visualisation of endometriotic lesions
Transvaginal ultrasound
MRI pelvis
Serum CA-125 level
Empiric treatment with a GnRH agonist for 3 months as a diagnostic test

stem: A 30-year-old primigravida at 32 weeks gestation reports severe pain over the pubic symphysis that radiates to the inner thighs, worse with walking, climbing stairs, and turning in bed. Examination reveals tenderness over the symphysis pubis and positive FABER test. What is the most appropriate first-line management?
lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line management?

options.t: Refer to a women's health physiotherapist for pelvic girdle exercises, support belt fitting, and analgesia
Induction of labour at 37 weeks gestation to minimise further musculoskeletal damage and ongoing maternal discomfort
Strict bed rest for the remainder of the pregnancy with bedside toileting and meals provided
Intra-articular corticosteroid injection into the symphysis pubis joint under image guidance in interventional radiology
Prescribe regular oral opioid analgesia such as immediate-release oxycodone for severe ongoing pelvic pain

stem: A 34-year-old G1P0 at 28 weeks with known fundal fibroid (8 cm at 20-week scan) presents with 36 hours of severe localised pain over the uterine fundus, mild nausea, and a low-grade temperature of 37.6. There is no vaginal bleeding, no leaking of fluid, no contractions and no urinary symptoms. Examination: tender over the fundal fibroid, no peritonism, normal bowel sounds, uterus otherwise soft. CTG reactive, foetus active. Ultrasound: 9 cm fibroid with heterogeneous echotexture and central cystic change, no free fluid, normal placental position, normal Dopplers. White cell count mildly elevated 14, CRP 35, urinalysis nil. Beta-hCG appropriate for gestation.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.t: Admit for analgesia (paracetamol +/- short-course oral or IV opioids), hydration, monitoring of foetal wellbeing and serial CTG, with avoidance of NSAIDs at this gestation
Commence indomethacin 50 mg orally tds for 7 days
Proceed to elective myomectomy at this gestation
Diagnose placental abruption and proceed to immediate caesarean section
Commence broad-spectrum IV antibiotics for presumed septic fibroid

stem: A 30-year-old G3P0 at 14+0 weeks attends a recurrent-pregnancy-loss clinic. She has had two previous second-trimester losses at 20 and 22 weeks, both with painless cervical dilation and rapid delivery of a liveborn infant who died shortly after delivery. No infection, no bleeding, no abruption identified at either loss. There are no other risk factors for preterm birth (no smoking, no LLETZ, no Mullerian anomaly, no DES exposure). Current TVUS cervical length 38 mm with closed internal os. She is otherwise well and asks specifically about the option of a prophylactic suture.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.t: Offer a history-indicated cervical cerclage at 12-14 weeks now
Serial TVUS cervical length every 2 weeks, with ultrasound-indicated cerclage only if length falls below 25 mm before 24 weeks
Commence vaginal progesterone 200 mg daily from 16 weeks without cerclage
Bed rest at home from 16 weeks with avoidance of intercourse and exercise
Await spontaneous cervical change and place emergency rescue cerclage only if dilation occurs

stem: A 28-year-old G2P1 at 36+2 weeks presents with reduced foetal movements for 12 hours. Bedside ultrasound confirms intrauterine foetal demise. She is haemodynamically stable, no bleeding, no contractions. The senior registrar has confirmed and broken the news, with bereavement midwifery support called. She is keen to take the baby home and is asking whether she could await spontaneous labour rather than be induced now.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate counselling and management plan?

options.t: Counsel that delaying delivery beyond approximately 48 hours after foetal death increases the risk of disseminated intravascular coagulation, and offer induction of labour as the preferred option, while supporting her preferences
Proceed to category 1 caesarean section as soon as theatre is available
Discharge home and await spontaneous labour at term
Administer betamethasone now and induce labour in 48 hours
Repeat ultrasound and CTG in 24 hours to reconfirm the diagnosis before discussing options

stem: A 30-year-old G3P2 at 38+0 weeks presents with sudden severe abdominal pain and minimal vaginal bleeding. Examination: tense, tender, woody-hard uterus, fundal height larger than expected. HR 128, BP 88/54, RR 24, SpO2 97%, peripherally cool. CTG: foetal bradycardia 70 bpm with absent variability. She has chronic hypertension on labetalol and was a smoker until early pregnancy. Bedside ultrasound: posterior placenta, no praevia, retroplacental haematoma visible. Bloods sent: Hb 78 g/L, platelets 92, INR 1.6, fibrinogen 1.4 g/L.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Activate the massive transfusion protocol and proceed immediately to category 1 caesarean section
Commence tocolysis with nifedipine and observe with serial CTG
Insert two large-bore IV cannulae, give 2 L crystalloid bolus and reassess in 30 minutes
Attempt artificial rupture of membranes to expedite vaginal delivery
Transfer to tertiary centre for higher-level obstetric input

stem: A 28-year-old nullipara at 41 weeks is in labour with oxytocin augmentation. The CTG shows a baseline of 150 bpm with normal variability but repetitive late decelerations after each contraction. Contractions are 5 in 10 minutes. What is the most appropriate next step?
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Cease the oxytocin infusion, reposition the mother to left lateral, and consider acute tocolysis with terbutaline 250 mcg SC
Continue oxytocin at the same rate and prepare for immediate vaginal delivery
Perform an immediate caesarean section without further assessment
Apply a foetal scalp electrode and take a foetal scalp blood sample
Administer a maternal oxygen mask at 8 L/min and increase the oxytocin to achieve better contractions

stem: A 28-year-old primipara, 6 weeks postpartum, reports persistent low mood, anhedonia, poor sleep when the baby sleeps, and feelings of guilt. Her Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS) score is 14. She is breastfeeding. She has no suicidal ideation. What is the most appropriate initial management?
lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.t: Refer for psychological therapy (CBT or interpersonal therapy) and consider sertraline 50 mg daily, with risk-benefit discussion in breastfeeding
Reassurance that the 'baby blues' are normal and will resolve without any intervention or follow-up within a few weeks
Immediate admission to a mother-baby psychiatric unit due to perceived risk of harm to the baby in the home setting
Commence fluoxetine 20 mg daily and advise her to stop breastfeeding due to neonatal serotonergic risk concerns
Advise increased exercise and structured social engagement only, with reassessment in routine clinic in four weeks time

stem: A 24-year-old G1P0 at 8+2 weeks gestation presents with vaginal bleeding and lower abdominal cramps. Speculum: cervical os is open with a small amount of tissue at the os. TVUS: empty uterus, endometrial thickness 8 mm, no retained products clearly visible, no adnexal mass, no free fluid. Beta-hCG 1200 IU/L today (was 8500 IU/L two days ago). She is haemodynamically stable, Hb 122, blood group A Rh-positive, no allergies. She has had no prior surgery and is keen to be home with her partner. The team is choosing between expectant, medical and surgical management.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Offer expectant, medical (misoprostol) or surgical (suction curettage) management options, and support her choice
Proceed directly to suction curettage in theatre today
Administer single-dose methotrexate as the pregnancy may be ectopic
Admit for IV oxytocin infusion to expedite expulsion of retained products
Discharge home with no follow-up; the falling beta-hCG indicates resolution

stem: A 24-year-old G2P1 at 29+4 weeks gestation presents with a sudden gush of clear fluid and ongoing trickling. She is afebrile and has no contractions. Speculum examination confirms pooling of clear fluid with positive nitrazine test. Cervix is closed and long. CTG is reassuring. She received a course of betamethasone at 27 weeks for a threatened preterm labour that resolved.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate antibiotic regimen?

options.t: Oral erythromycin 250 mg four times daily for 10 days
IV ampicillin 2 g 6-hourly plus IV gentamicin daily for 48 hours, then oral amoxicillin for 5 days
Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg twice daily for 10 days
No antibiotics are required as she is afebrile
Intramuscular ceftriaxone 1 g daily for 7 days

stem: A 32-year-old primigravida with diet-controlled GDM and an EFW of 4.5 kg has just had the foetal head deliver with retraction and turtle-neck sign. McRoberts plus suprapubic pressure (30 seconds), attempted delivery of the posterior arm (twice), and internal rotation manoeuvres (Rubin II and Woods' screw) have all failed. Time from delivery of the head: 4 minutes. CTG showed normal trace before delivery. The woman has an epidural in situ. The midwife asks the senior registrar if firm fundal pressure should now be tried while preparing for the next manoeuvre.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Roll the woman onto hands and knees (Gaskin all-fours manoeuvre) and reattempt delivery of the posterior arm
Apply firm fundal pressure to dislodge the impacted shoulder
Perform symphysiotomy to enlarge the pelvic outlet
Perform Zavanelli cephalic replacement and proceed to caesarean section
Repeat McRoberts and suprapubic pressure for another 60 seconds

stem: A 31-year-old woman with primary obstetric antiphospholipid syndrome (three consecutive first-trimester miscarriages; persistent lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin IgG and anti-beta2GPI IgG on testing 12 weeks apart, no history of thromboembolic event) is now 6 weeks pregnant. She has been on warfarin 5 mg daily for the past 9 months as 'prevention' prescribed by another clinician. She has had no thrombosis. She has no other medical conditions and no allergies.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate medication regimen for this pregnancy?

options.t: Continue warfarin 5 mg daily throughout pregnancy as she is already established on it
Stop warfarin immediately and commence low-dose aspirin 100 mg daily plus prophylactic-dose enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneously daily, continuing through pregnancy and for 6 weeks postpartum
Continue warfarin until 12 weeks then switch to LMWH
Continue warfarin until 6 weeks then aspirin alone for the rest of the pregnancy
Stop warfarin and commence intravenous immunoglobulin monthly as primary therapy

stem: A 26-year-old primigravida at 38 weeks with PROM 28 hours ago develops T 39.2, HR 124, RR 28, BP 88/52 (MAP 64), confused with GCS 14. Tender uterus, purulent vaginal discharge, foetal HR 175 bpm with reduced variability. Lactate 4.6, WCC 19. IV access obtained, bloods sent, blood cultures taken. The on-call team has called for theatre.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Administer IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (ampicillin + gentamicin + metronidazole) and a 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid bolus within the next hour, while preparing for emergency delivery
Transfer to ICU for central venous access before commencing antibiotics
Proceed immediately to caesarean section as the primary intervention before fluids or antibiotics
Administer IV paracetamol 1 g and reassess vital signs in 30 minutes before further intervention
Order CT abdomen and pelvis to identify the source before starting antibiotics

stem: A 34-year-old G3P2 at 39+2 in spontaneous labour at 5 cm dilatation has a history of one lower-segment caesarean and one classical caesarean 1 year ago for preterm breech delivery. The midwifery team had counselled her against TOLAC due to the classical scar, but she insisted on attempting vaginal birth. Over the last 15 minutes she has developed constant severe suprapubic pain not relieved between contractions; the CTG now shows prolonged bradycardia 70 bpm; the foetal parts are easily felt abdominally, the uterine fundus is not palpable, and on VE the presenting part has receded from -1 to -3. Maternal HR 130, BP 88/52.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.t: Activate the massive transfusion protocol and proceed to immediate category 1 caesarean section with anticipated laparotomy and possible hysterectomy
Commence IV oxytocin to augment contractions and expedite vaginal delivery
Apply fundal pressure to expedite vaginal delivery as the head was previously at -1
Administer subcutaneous terbutaline 250 mcg tocolysis and reassess in 10 minutes
Perform speculum examination and foetal fibronectin to confirm membrane rupture before surgery

stem: A 25-year-old primigravida at 10 weeks gestation has been admitted three times over the past 4 weeks for severe hyperemesis gravidarum. She has lost 7 kg (12% of pre-pregnancy weight) and has persistent ketonuria, hypokalaemia, and hyponatraemia despite regular intravenous 0.9% sodium chloride and antiemetics (ondansetron, metoclopramide).
lead_in: What is the most critical vitamin to administer before starting dextrose-containing parenteral nutrition?

options.t: Intravenous thiamine 500 mg three times daily for 2-3 days, then 250 mg daily, to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy
Oral folic acid 5 mg once daily for the duration of the hyperemesis admission today
Intravenous vitamin K 10 mg slowly over 30 minutes prior to commencing any dextrose containing fluids
Intramuscular vitamin B12 (hydroxocobalamin) 1000 mcg as a single dose now before glucose replacement
No specific vitamin supplementation is needed before commencing dextrose containing intravenous fluid replacement therapy

stem: A 30-year-old G3P0 at 21+5 weeks presents with pelvic pressure and a bulging sensation. Speculum: cervix dilated 3 cm with exposed amniotic membranes visible at the os. No fever, no uterine tenderness; WCC 10.2, CRP 8, vaginal swab GBS negative, foetal fibronectin not applicable, no pooling of liquor. Ultrasound: viable foetus, cervical length 0 mm with funnelling, normal AFI, no foetal anomalies. Neonatology has counselled the woman and she wants every appropriate intervention to prolong the pregnancy.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate combination of management steps?

options.t: Offer emergency McDonald cerclage, short-course indomethacin tocolysis (48 hours) given gestation under 32 weeks, antibiotic prophylaxis, and antenatal corticosteroids when delivery becomes likely
Cerclage is contraindicated with bulging membranes; give only bed rest and corticosteroids
Place cerclage and give corticosteroids but avoid indomethacin entirely due to ductal closure risk
Induce labour now as 21+5 is non-viable and ongoing pregnancy is futile
Place an abdominal cervicoisthmic cerclage today and commence IV magnesium sulfate

stem: A 37-year-old G5P4 with four prior lower-segment caesarean sections has anterior major placenta praevia. Ultrasound and MRI at 30 weeks confirm placenta accreta (loss of retroplacental clear zone, lacunae, hypervascularity at uterovesical interface), without percreta or bladder invasion. Foetal growth and Dopplers are normal. She has completed a course of betamethasone. She has been counselled about haemorrhage and hysterectomy and accepts a planned caesarean hysterectomy. The MDT is finalising timing and team composition.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate delivery plan?

options.t: Planned caesarean hysterectomy at 34-35+6 weeks at a tertiary centre by a multidisciplinary team (obstetrician, gynae-oncology, interventional radiology, anaesthesia, haematology, neonatology), with cell salvage and MTP on standby and a vertical/midline abdominal incision
Elective caesarean at 39 weeks under a single general obstetrician at her local hospital
Plan vaginal delivery with active third-stage management and theatre on standby
Plan delivery at 32 weeks to maximise prevention of any spontaneous labour
Admit at 34 weeks for inpatient monitoring and deliver only when spontaneous labour or distress occurs
